// QUESTION REVIEW

Correct: 0
Wrong: 0 Score: 0%
DOMAIN 1.0
General Security Concepts
Cryptography, PKI, authentication, security controls, and foundational principles.
Q 1.1MATCH
Match each cryptographic algorithm to its correct category.
Algorithm
AES-256
RSA-2048
SHA-256
RC4
Category Definitions
Symmetric Encryption — Same key to encrypt and decrypt
Asymmetric Encryption — Uses a public/private key pair
Hashing — One-way fixed-length output; integrity only
Stream Cipher — Encrypts one bit/byte at a time; deprecated
AES-256 — symmetric block cipher, same key encrypts/decrypts. RSA-2048 — asymmetric, public key encrypts, private key decrypts. SHA-256 — hashing, one-way 256-bit digest, no decryption. RC4 — stream cipher, deprecated (WEP, SSL), broken due to statistical bias.
Q 1.2ORDER
Arrange the PKI hierarchy from highest trust (top) to lowest (bottom).
  • ⠿Intermediate CA Certificate
  • ⠿Root CA Certificate
  • ⠿End-Entity (Leaf) Certificate
  • ⠿Trust Anchor (Root Store)
Trust Anchor (browser/OS root store) → Root CA (self-signed, offline) → Intermediate CA (online, signs leaf certs) → End-Entity Certificate (actual TLS/user cert). A broken chain at any level = untrusted certificate.
Q 1.3DRAG & DROP
Scenario: Your company is implementing MFA. Categorise each authentication method into the correct factor category.
Authentication Methods
PIN
Fingerprint scan
Hardware token (TOTP)
Geolocation (home IP)
Password
Retina scan
Factor Categories
Something You Know
Something You Have
Something You Are
Somewhere You Are
Know: PIN, Password. Have: Hardware token (TOTP). Are: Fingerprint, Retina scan. Where: Geolocation. True MFA must use factors from different categories.
Q 1.4SELECT
Scenario: A financial organisation wants to eliminate "trusted network zones" and verify every request regardless of origin.
Which security model BEST describes this approach?
  • ADefense in Depth — layering multiple security controls
  • BZero Trust Architecture — "never trust, always verify"
  • CNetwork Segmentation — dividing the network into zones
  • DLeast Privilege — granting minimum access required
Zero Trust: "never trust, always verify" — even inside the perimeter. Key pillars: MFA, device health checks, least privilege, micro-segmentation, continuous monitoring. Defense in Depth is layered controls; Network Segmentation is a ZTA component; Least Privilege is a ZTA principle.
Q 1.5CLI
Scenario: You are a sysadmin setting up a test web server. You need to generate a self-signed TLS certificate valid for 365 days using OpenSSL, outputting the key to server.key and cert to server.crt.
Type the correct OpenSSL command below:
$
openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:2048 -keyout server.key -out server.crt -days 365 -nodes

req: certificate request. -x509: output self-signed cert instead of CSR. -newkey rsa:2048: generate new 2048-bit RSA key. -keyout server.key: write private key. -out server.crt: write certificate. -days 365: valid for 1 year. -nodes: no passphrase on key (needed for automated server startup).
Q 1.6MATCH
Match each access control model to the scenario that BEST describes it.
Scenario
A hospital classifies records as Confidential or Top Secret and only allows users with the matching clearance level to access them.
A file owner grants their colleague read access to a shared document using a permissions dialog.
All employees in the Finance department can access payroll data because they are assigned the Finance role.
Access is granted based on the user's department, their device compliance status, and the time of day.
Access Control Models
MAC — Mandatory Access Control: labels and clearances set by system/policy, not users
DAC — Discretionary Access Control: resource owners control who can access their resources
RBAC — Role-Based Access Control: access determined by the user's assigned role
ABAC — Attribute-Based Access Control: access based on multiple attributes (user, device, time, location)
MAC: enforced by the OS or system administrator using labels (Confidential, Secret, Top Secret). Users cannot override it. Common in government and military. DAC: the resource owner decides — like sharing a Google Drive file. Flexible but risky if owners make mistakes. RBAC: access follows the job role, not the individual. Easiest to manage at scale (add/remove a role = instant access change). ABAC: the most flexible and granular — combines any attribute (who you are, what device you use, where you are, what time it is). Used in Zero Trust architectures.
DOMAIN 2.0
Threats, Vulnerabilities & Mitigations
Malware analysis, social engineering, vulnerability scanning, and attack vectors.
Q 2.1MATCH
Match each threat description to the correct malware type.
Description
Self-replicates and spreads without user interaction
Requires a host file to execute and replicate
Disguises itself as legitimate software
Provides covert remote access to an attacker
Hides itself deep in the OS to avoid detection
Types
Worm — autonomous, exploits network vulnerabilities
Virus — needs a host file, spreads when host runs
Trojan — masquerades as benign software
RAT — Remote Access Trojan, backdoor control
Rootkit — kernel-level stealth persistence
Key exam distinction: Worm = self-replicates autonomously (WannaCry). Virus = needs a host file and user action to spread. Trojan = appears legitimate, carries hidden payload. RAT = Trojan subtype giving full remote control. Rootkit = hides in kernel, survives reboots, hardest to detect.
Q 2.2SELECT
Scenario: An attacker sends a carefully crafted email to the CFO of a company, referencing her name, her assistant's name, and a real upcoming board meeting. The email asks her to urgently wire $500,000 to a new vendor account.
This attack is BEST described as:
  • APhishing — bulk untargeted email attack
  • BWhaling — targeted spear phishing against a senior executive
  • CVishing — voice-based social engineering
  • DSmishing — SMS-based phishing attack
Whaling = spear phishing targeting C-level executives (CEO, CFO, CTO). The personalised details (name, assistant, real meeting) indicate OSINT research — classic whaling setup. BEC (Business Email Compromise) is often the goal. Phishing = bulk generic. Spear phishing = targeted individual. Whaling = targeted executive.
Q 2.3ORDER
Arrange the NIST Incident Response phases in the correct order.
  • Containment, Eradication & Recovery
  • Preparation
  • Post-Incident Activity (Lessons Learned)
  • Detection & Analysis
NIST SP 800-61: 1-Preparation (build the IR plan, tools, team) → 2-Detection & Analysis (identify the incident) → 3-Containment, Eradication & Recovery (stop it, remove it, restore) → 4-Post-Incident Activity (lessons learned, report). Memory aid: Prep Detectives Contain Problems.
Q 2.4MATCH
Match each attacker action to the correct MITRE ATT&CK tactic.
Attacker Action
Attacker sends phishing email to gain credentials
Attacker creates a new admin account to survive reboot
Attacker exploits local privilege to become SYSTEM
Attacker uses stolen creds to move to finance server
Attacker uploads data to external cloud storage
MITRE ATT&CK Tactics
Initial Access — first entry into the environment
Persistence — maintain foothold across reboots
Privilege Escalation — gain higher-level permissions
Lateral Movement — move between systems internally
Exfiltration — steal data out of the environment
MITRE ATT&CK framework describes adversary behaviour in 14 tactics. This is the kill chain order for most breaches: Initial Access (phishing gets foot in door) → Persistence (new admin account survives reboot) → Privilege Escalation (local exploit to SYSTEM) → Lateral Movement (pivot to other systems) → Exfiltration (data leaves the network). Knowing these tactics is essential for Security+ exam and real SOC work.
Q 2.5ORDER
Scenario: A SOC analyst receives an alert at 02:00 that ransomware has encrypted files on 3 servers. The analyst must follow the NIST SP 800-61 Incident Response lifecycle.
Drag to arrange the Incident Response phases in the correct order.
  • ⠿Containment — isolate affected servers from the network
  • ⠿Preparation — policies, tools, and IR team in place before incident
  • ⠿Lessons Learned — post-incident review to improve defences
  • ⠿Eradication — remove malware, patch vulnerabilities
  • ⠿Detection & Analysis — confirm it is ransomware, determine scope
  • ⠿Recovery — restore from clean backups, verify systems
NIST SP 800-61 defines 4 phases (but 6 steps): 1-Preparation (train the team, have tools ready — before anything happens). 2-Detection & Analysis (confirm the incident, scope it, log everything). 3-Containment (stop the spread FIRST — network isolation before cleanup). 4-Eradication (kill the malware, patch the hole it used). 5-Recovery (bring systems back online, verify clean). 6-Lessons Learned (post-incident report within 2 weeks — what failed, what worked, update playbooks). A common exam trap: Containment always comes before Eradication.
DOMAIN 3.0
Security Architecture
Network security, cloud, virtualisation, and infrastructure design.
Q 3.1DRAG & DROP
Scenario: You are designing a three-zone network architecture. Place each component into the correct network zone.
Components
WAF / Web Server
Database Server
Internal App Server
Mail Relay (SMTP)
HR Workstation
Domain Controller
Network Zones
🌐 DMZ (Demilitarised Zone)
🔒 Internal Network
🏰 Restricted / Secure Zone
DMZ: WAF/Web Server, Mail Relay — internet-facing services that need to accept external connections but must be isolated from internal systems. Internal Network: HR Workstation, Internal App Server — user devices and internal-only applications. Restricted Zone: Database Server, Domain Controller — highest-value assets that should never be directly accessible from DMZ or internet.
Q 3.2SELECT
Scenario: A company wants to move its email to the cloud. They want the provider to manage everything — servers, storage, OS, and the email application itself. The company only manages user accounts and data.
Which cloud service model BEST fits this requirement?
  • AIaaS — Infrastructure as a Service
  • BPaaS — Platform as a Service
  • CSaaS — Software as a Service
  • DFaaS — Function as a Service
SaaS: provider manages everything including the application. Customer only manages data and users. Examples: Microsoft 365, Gmail, Salesforce. PaaS: provider manages OS + runtime, customer manages application code (e.g. Heroku). IaaS: provider manages hardware + virtualisation, customer manages OS upwards (e.g. AWS EC2). Memory aid: SaaS = Somebody else's app. PaaS = Platform ready, you code. IaaS = Infrastructure only.
Q 3.3ORDER
Scenario: A packet arrives at a stateful firewall. Arrange the order in which the firewall evaluates the packet.
Drag to arrange from first checked (top) to last (bottom).
  • Match against ACL rules (top-down)
  • Check connection state table (existing session?)
  • Apply implicit deny (no rule matched)
  • Validate packet headers (IP, port, protocol)
Stateful firewall evaluation: 1-State table (if packet belongs to established session, allow immediately — most efficient). 2-Header validation (is the packet well-formed? Right flags?). 3-ACL rules (match top-down, first match wins). 4-Implicit deny (all firewalls end with "deny all" — if nothing matched, drop the packet). This is why rule order matters — a broad allow rule early can bypass later deny rules.
DOMAIN 4.0
Security Operations
IAM, monitoring, endpoint hardening, and digital forensics.
Q 4.1MATCH
Match each IAM term to its correct definition.
IAM Term
SSO
MFA
RBAC
Provisioning
Federation
Definitions
Single login grants access to multiple systems
Multiple verification factors required
Access based on job role
Account lifecycle management
Cross-domain trust for authentication
SSO: authenticate once, access many systems (e.g. Google Workspace). MFA: 2+ factors from different categories. RBAC: permissions assigned to roles, not individuals — scales well. Provisioning: creating, modifying, and deprovisioning accounts throughout lifecycle. Federation: trust between separate identity domains (e.g. login with Google to access a third-party app via SAML/OAuth).
Q 4.2CLI
Scenario: You are a security analyst performing a vulnerability scan. You need to run an nmap SYN scan (stealth scan) against 192.168.1.0/24, scanning only the top 100 ports, with OS detection enabled, and output to a file called scan.txt.
Type the correct nmap command:
$
nmap -sS -O --top-ports 100 192.168.1.0/24 -oN scan.txt

-sS: SYN scan (stealth/half-open, doesn't complete TCP handshake, harder to detect in logs). -O: OS detection. --top-ports 100: scan the 100 most common ports. 192.168.1.0/24: target subnet (CIDR). -oN scan.txt: normal output to file. Other output formats: -oX (XML), -oG (grepable), -oA (all formats).
DOMAIN 5.0
Security Program Management & Oversight
Governance, risk, compliance, data privacy, and security awareness.
Q 5.1MATCH
Match each compliance framework to the industry or requirement it primarily serves.
Framework
PCI DSS
HIPAA
GDPR
NIST CSF
ISO 27001
Primary Focus
Payment card data security — mandatory for card processors
US healthcare patient data (PHI) — HIPAA covered entities
EU personal data protection — right to erasure, consent
US federal voluntary cybersecurity framework (5 functions)
International certifiable ISMS standard
PCI DSS: mandatory for any org storing/processing card data. HIPAA: US law; applies to covered entities and business associates handling PHI. GDPR: EU regulation; right to erasure, portability, 72-hour breach notification. NIST CSF: voluntary framework, 5 functions: Identify-Protect-Detect-Respond-Recover. ISO 27001: internationally certifiable ISMS; requires documented ISMS + risk treatment plan.
Q 5.2ORDER
Arrange the steps of a formal quantitative risk assessment in the correct order.
  • Calculate Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE = SLE × ARO)
  • Identify and value assets
  • Implement controls if ALE exceeds control cost
  • Calculate Single Loss Expectancy (SLE = Asset Value × EF)
  • Identify threats and calculate Exposure Factor (EF)
Quantitative formula chain: 1-Asset Value → 2-Exposure Factor (what % lost per incident?) → 3-SLE = AV × EF (loss per single event) → 4-ALE = SLE × ARO (ARO = Annual Rate of Occurrence; expected annual loss) → 5-Control decision (only implement if annual control cost < ALE reduction). Example: $1M server, 40% EF, 2×/year → SLE=$400K, ALE=$800K.
// REFERENCE
Security+ Acronyms SY0-701
Every acronym tested on the exam — full name and explanation.
// ╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗ // ║ FULL QUESTION BANK — ALL DOMAINS ║ // ╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝ // ─── OBJECTIVE 1.1 QUESTIONS (25) ─── const Q_1_1 = [ {s:false,q:"Which category of security control includes firewalls, antivirus software, and encryption?",o:["A. Physical","B. Managerial","C. Operational","D. Technical"],a:"D",e:"Technical controls are hardware or software-based safeguards embedded in systems. Firewalls, antivirus, IDS/IPS, and encryption are all technical controls. Physical = tangible barriers; Managerial = policies; Operational = human procedures."}, {s:false,q:"An organization implements a mandatory security awareness training policy for all employees. This is BEST classified as which control category?",o:["A. Technical","B. Physical","C. Managerial","D. Directive"],a:"C",e:"Managerial controls are administrative in nature — policies, risk assessments, governance frameworks, and training requirements. The policy mandating training is managerial. Note: the training content delivery could be technical, but the mandate itself is managerial."}, {s:true,sc:"A healthcare organization cannot upgrade the operating system on an MRI machine because the hardware vendor no longer supports the upgrade. The machine handles protected health information (PHI) and must remain in operation.",q:"Which control type would be MOST appropriate to mitigate the risk?",o:["A. Corrective","B. Preventive","C. Compensating","D. Directive"],a:"C",e:"A Compensating control is used when the primary control (patching/upgrading) cannot be applied. Network isolation, enhanced monitoring, or a WAF in front of the system compensate for the inability to patch the legacy system."}, {s:false,q:"A login banner stating 'Unauthorized access is prohibited and will be prosecuted' is BEST described as which control type?",o:["A. Preventive","B. Detective","C. Corrective","D. Deterrent"],a:"D",e:"A Deterrent control discourages attackers psychologically but does not technically prevent access. Warning banners, security signs, and visible cameras all deter without blocking. Compare to Preventive (technically stops), Detective (identifies after), Corrective (fixes after)."}, {s:false,q:"Which control type is specifically designed to STOP an attack before it occurs?",o:["A. Detective","B. Corrective","C. Preventive","D. Compensating"],a:"C",e:"A Preventive control is specifically designed to stop attacks before they happen. Examples: MFA, firewalls, access control lists, locked doors. Detective controls identify attacks in progress; corrective controls fix after an attack."}, {s:true,sc:"After a ransomware attack, the security team restores all encrypted data from offline backups within 4 hours. The backups had been tested monthly and were verified clean.",q:"The backup system represents which control type?",o:["A. Detective","B. Preventive","C. Deterrent","D. Corrective"],a:"D",e:"Corrective controls restore systems to their pre-attack state after an incident. Backups are the classic corrective control — they don't prevent ransomware but correct the damage caused by it. An IPS would be preventive; an IDS would be detective."}, {s:false,q:"An organization installs motion-activated lights in their parking lot and server room corridors. These lights serve PRIMARILY as which control type?",o:["A. Preventive","B. Detective","C. Deterrent","D. Corrective"],a:"C",e:"Motion-activated lights primarily serve as a deterrent — they discourage attackers by eliminating hiding spots and making unauthorized presence visible. They may have some detective value but their primary security purpose is deterrence."}, {s:false,q:"An IDS monitors network traffic and sends an alert when it detects a known attack signature. This is BEST described as which control type?",o:["A. Preventive","B. Detective","C. Corrective","D. Compensating"],a:"B",e:"An IDS (Intrusion Detection System) is a detective control — it identifies and alerts on suspicious activity but does not stop it. An IPS (Intrusion Prevention System) is preventive. Remember the distinction: IDS detects, IPS prevents."}, {s:true,sc:"A company's security team wants to implement a control to limit the impact if a staff member's credentials are stolen. They configure the system so that each user can only access the specific files and systems required for their current job role, and nothing else.",q:"This describes which security principle implemented as which control type?",o:["A. Least privilege — Preventive","B. Non-repudiation — Detective","C. Separation of duties — Compensating","D. Least privilege — Corrective"],a:"A",e:"Least privilege is the principle of giving users only the access they need. It is implemented as a preventive control because it limits the damage that can be done if credentials are stolen — stopping lateral movement before it starts."}, {s:false,q:"Physical security controls include which of the following?",o:["A. Group Policy settings","B. Risk assessment documentation","C. Security awareness training","D. Biometric access readers"],a:"D",e:"Physical controls are tangible, real-world barriers. Biometric access readers physically control who enters a space. Group Policy is technical; risk assessments are managerial; security training is operational."}, {s:false,q:"After detecting a data breach, a company revokes all compromised access tokens and reissues new credentials to affected users. This action is BEST classified as which control type?",o:["A. Preventive","B. Detective","C. Corrective","D. Deterrent"],a:"C",e:"Revoking compromised tokens and reissuing credentials corrects the damage after the incident. This is a corrective control — it restores security posture after a breach is discovered."}, {s:true,sc:"A financial institution's compliance team discovers that due to regulatory requirements, all user activity on trading systems must be recorded and stored for 7 years. Users are notified that 'all sessions are recorded and subject to audit.'",q:"The recording and audit log requirement represents which control category and type?",o:["A. Technical — Detective","B. Managerial — Directive","C. Operational — Corrective","D. Physical — Preventive"],a:"A",e:"Technical — Detective: The audit logs are implemented through technology (technical category) and they identify/record activity for review (detective type). The user notification banner would be a deterrent. The policy requiring the logging would be managerial."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following is an example of an OPERATIONAL security control?",o:["A. Encryption of data at rest","B. Security incident response procedures performed by staff","C. Access control lists on a firewall","D. Physical locks on server room doors"],a:"B",e:"Operational controls are carried out by people as part of day-to-day security work. Incident response procedures executed by staff, security patrols, and background checks are operational. Encryption and ACLs are technical; physical locks are physical."}, {s:false,q:"Which control type is described as an alternative safeguard used when the primary control cannot be implemented?",o:["A. Corrective","B. Directive","C. Preventive","D. Compensating"],a:"D",e:"A Compensating control provides an alternative safeguard when the standard control is not feasible. Example: If you can't patch a legacy system, you compensate with network isolation and enhanced monitoring. Compensating controls are common in PCI-DSS compliance."}, {s:true,sc:"A security audit reveals that a specific web server running a business-critical application cannot be taken offline for patching during business hours. The application has known vulnerabilities that currently have no vendor patch available.",q:"Which TWO compensating controls would be MOST effective? (Select the best single answer)",o:["A. Decommission the server immediately","B. Deploy a Web Application Firewall and implement strict network segmentation around the server","C. Apply the patch manually by editing configuration files","D. Create a policy that users should avoid the application"],a:"B",e:"A WAF (Web Application Firewall) filters malicious traffic targeting the unpatched vulnerabilities, and network segmentation limits blast radius if compromised. These are both compensating controls used when patching is not immediately possible."}, {s:false,q:"A policy requiring all employees to wear visible ID badges at all times represents which control category?",o:["A. Technical","B. Physical","C. Managerial","D. Operational"],a:"D",e:"Requiring employees to wear ID badges is an operational control — it relies on human behavior and daily procedures. The badge reader that scans the badge is technical; the physical badge itself is a physical control; the policy mandating it is managerial. But wearing it is operational."}, {s:false,q:"Security cameras installed at building entrances serve PRIMARILY as which control type?",o:["A. Preventive","B. Corrective","C. Detective and Deterrent","D. Compensating"],a:"C",e:"Security cameras are both detective (they record and identify incidents) and deterrent (visible cameras discourage would-be attackers). They don't physically prevent entry, so they are not primarily preventive."}, {s:true,sc:"A penetration tester gains initial access to a corporate network using stolen credentials. She is surprised to find that after gaining access, she can reach servers in Finance, HR, Engineering, and the Domain Controller all from the same network segment with no additional authentication required.",q:"This finding indicates a failure of which control type?",o:["A. Compensating controls","B. Preventive controls through lack of network segmentation","C. Detective controls","D. Corrective controls"],a:"B",e:"The lack of network segmentation means there are insufficient preventive controls to stop lateral movement after initial access. Micro-segmentation and VLANs are preventive technical controls. The scenario describes what happens when these preventive controls are absent."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following BEST describes a directive control?",o:["A. A firewall that blocks malicious traffic automatically","B. A policy document telling employees how to handle sensitive data","C. An audit log recording all database queries","D. A backup system that restores data after an attack"],a:"B",e:"A directive control directs, guides, or mandates behavior — it tells people what to do. Policies, standards, procedures, and acceptable use agreements are all directive controls. A firewall = technical/preventive; audit logs = technical/detective; backups = corrective."}, {s:false,q:"What is the PRIMARY difference between a deterrent control and a preventive control?",o:["A. Deterrent controls only apply to physical security","B. Preventive controls technically block threats; deterrent controls only discourage them","C. Deterrent controls are always technical; preventive controls are always physical","D. There is no practical difference between the two"],a:"B",e:"A preventive control technically stops an attack from occurring (e.g., a locked door, MFA). A deterrent control discourages an attack psychologically but doesn't technically block it (e.g., warning signs, visible cameras). The key is whether the control physically/technically prevents or merely discourages."}, {s:true,sc:"A company implements the following controls for their database server: (1) encrypts all data at rest, (2) requires MFA for all admin access, (3) runs daily automated vulnerability scans, (4) has a tested backup and recovery plan.",q:"Which of these represents a CORRECTIVE control?",o:["A. Encrypting data at rest","B. Requiring MFA","C. Running vulnerability scans","D. Backup and recovery plan"],a:"D",e:"The backup and recovery plan is the corrective control — it restores operations after an incident. Encrypting data = preventive; MFA = preventive; vulnerability scans = detective. Corrective controls fix damage; they operate after an incident has occurred."}, {s:false,q:"A security guard checking employee ID cards at the entrance to a data center represents which type and category of control?",o:["A. Technical — Detective","B. Operational — Preventive","C. Physical — Deterrent","D. Managerial — Directive"],a:"B",e:"A security guard is an operational control (carried out by a person) that is preventive (they stop unauthorized people from entering). While guards also deter, their primary function when actively checking IDs is prevention of unauthorized access."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following BEST represents a combination of multiple control types working together?",o:["A. A firewall rule blocking all inbound Telnet traffic","B. A policy requiring quarterly password changes","C. Security lighting, cameras, and access logs at a data center","D. A patch management schedule"],a:"C",e:"Security lighting (deterrent/preventive), cameras (detective/deterrent), and access logs (detective) at a data center represent multiple control types layered together. This is defence in depth — using preventive, deterrent, and detective controls simultaneously."}, {s:true,sc:"During a compliance audit, the auditor notes that user accounts for employees who left the company six months ago still have active access to the CRM system. The security team was unaware these accounts existed.",q:"The absence of which control type MOST directly contributed to this vulnerability?",o:["A. Compensating — firewall rules","B. Preventive — account deprovisioning process","C. Detective — SIEM monitoring","D. Corrective — incident response plan"],a:"B",e:"Preventive — account deprovisioning: A formal offboarding process that immediately disables/removes access when employees leave is a preventive operational control. It prevents orphaned accounts from becoming attack vectors. The gap was in the preventive controls, not detective or corrective."}, {s:false,q:"The CIA Triad is itself an example of which type of security framework?",o:["A. A technical control","B. A compensating control","C. A managerial/conceptual framework guiding control selection","D. A physical control framework"],a:"C",e:"The CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) is a managerial/conceptual framework used to guide security decision-making and control selection. It isn't a control itself but a model that helps organizations identify what they're trying to protect."}, {s:false,q:"An employee is found to have been misusing their administrative access to a production server. The company immediately revokes their elevated privileges and resets the account to standard user permissions. This is BEST classified as:",o:["A. Preventive — Technical","B. Directive — Managerial","C. Corrective — Technical","D. Compensating — Operational"],a:"C",e:"Revoking admin privileges after discovering misuse is a corrective technical control — it corrects the security risk (remedies the situation after the fact) using a technical mechanism (access control modification). It's not preventive because the incident already occurred."}, ]; // ─── OBJECTIVE 1.2 QUESTIONS (25) ─── const Q_1_2 = [ {s:false,q:"Which component of the CIA Triad is PRIMARILY affected when an attacker intercepts and reads an encrypted email without the recipient's knowledge?",o:["A. Integrity","B. Availability","C. Confidentiality","D. Non-repudiation"],a:"C",e:"Confidentiality means information is only accessible to authorized parties. Even if the attacker can't decrypt it, the interception is an attack on confidentiality. If they modified the email, that would be integrity. If they blocked delivery, that would be availability."}, {s:true,sc:"A hospital's electronic health record system is taken offline for 18 hours due to a ransomware attack. During this time, doctors cannot access patient records and must resort to paper-based processes.",q:"Which component of the CIA Triad has been PRIMARILY violated?",o:["A. Confidentiality","B. Integrity","C. Availability","D. Non-repudiation"],a:"C",e:"The system being offline and inaccessible is an attack on Availability — the 'A' in CIA. The ransomware prevented authorized users from accessing records when needed. Note: if patient data was also stolen, Confidentiality would also be violated, but the primary operational impact described is Availability."}, {s:false,q:"A user submits a digitally signed contract but later denies doing so. Which security principle prevents this denial?",o:["A. Confidentiality","B. Availability","C. Integrity","D. Non-repudiation"],a:"D",e:"Non-repudiation prevents a party from denying an action they performed. Digital signatures provide non-repudiation because only the private key holder can create that signature — they cannot credibly deny signing the document later."}, {s:false,q:"In the AAA security framework, which component controls WHAT resources an authenticated user can access?",o:["A. Authentication","B. Accounting","C. Authorization","D. Auditing"],a:"C",e:"Authorization determines what permissions an authenticated user has — what they can read, write, or execute. Authentication proves identity; Accounting/Auditing tracks what they did. The distinction: Authentication = 'Who are you?' Authorization = 'What can you do?'"}, {s:true,sc:"A network engineer logs into a router using her credentials, configures a new route, then logs out. The SIEM records the login timestamp, the commands executed, the configuration changed, and the logout time under her username.",q:"The SIEM recording represents which component of AAA?",o:["A. Authentication","B. Authorization","C. Accounting","D. Availability"],a:"C",e:"Accounting (also called Auditing) tracks and records user actions after they've been authenticated and authorized. The SIEM capturing login time, commands, changes, and logout is accounting. This creates an audit trail for forensics and compliance."}, {s:false,q:"Zero Trust architecture operates on which core principle?",o:["A. Trust all traffic inside the corporate perimeter","B. Never trust, always verify","C. Trust verified users permanently after initial authentication","D. Physical location determines trust level"],a:"B",e:"Zero Trust operates on the principle of 'Never trust, always verify.' Every access request — regardless of whether it originates inside or outside the network — must be authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated. Location is not a trust factor."}, {s:false,q:"In Zero Trust, which component makes the actual grant or deny trust decision?",o:["A. Policy Enforcement Point (PEP)","B. Policy Administrator (PA)","C. Policy Engine (PE)","D. Adaptive Identity"],a:"C",e:"The Policy Engine (PE) is the decision-making brain of Zero Trust. It evaluates access requests against policies and produces a trust decision. The Policy Administrator communicates that decision; the PEP enforces it. PE decides → PA communicates → PEP enforces."}, {s:true,sc:"An employee logs in from her office laptop at 8:30am. Forty-five minutes later, the same account attempts to log in from an IP address registered in Eastern Europe. The security system prompts for additional biometric verification before allowing access.",q:"This behaviour BEST exemplifies which Zero Trust concept?",o:["A. Policy Enforcement Point","B. Threat Scope Reduction","C. Adaptive Identity","D. Implicit Trust Zones"],a:"C",e:"Adaptive Identity dynamically adjusts authentication requirements based on risk signals — in this case, impossible travel (same account, two geographically distant locations in 45 minutes). The system increased authentication requirements in response to elevated risk context."}, {s:false,q:"The Policy Enforcement Point (PEP) in a Zero Trust architecture is BEST described as:",o:["A. The component that makes access decisions","B. The component positioned between the subject and resource that enforces decisions","C. The component that communicates policy decisions","D. The component that stores user credentials"],a:"B",e:"The PEP sits between the subject (user/device) and the protected resource. It intercepts all access attempts and enforces the grant/deny decision received from the Policy Administrator. Firewalls, VPN gateways, WAFs, and API gateways can all act as PEPs."}, {s:true,sc:"An attacker compromises a developer's credentials and gains access to the corporate network. However, despite being 'inside' the network, the attacker is only able to access the development servers that the developer legitimately uses — they cannot reach finance systems, the HR database, or the Domain Controller.",q:"Which Zero Trust concept MOST directly limited the attacker's reach?",o:["A. Adaptive Identity","B. Threat Scope Reduction through least privilege and micro-segmentation","C. Policy Administrator","D. Implicit Trust Zones"],a:"B",e:"Threat Scope Reduction limits what any single compromised identity can access. By implementing least privilege (only access needed resources) and micro-segmentation (separate network zones per function), the blast radius of the compromise was contained to only the developer's legitimate resources."}, {s:false,q:"Which physical security control uses a two-door entry system where the first door must close before the second opens, preventing tailgating?",o:["A. Bollards","B. Access control vestibule (mantrap)","C. Fencing","D. Pressure sensor"],a:"B",e:"An access control vestibule (also called a mantrap) is a small buffer zone with two interlocking doors. This prevents tailgating — one person at a time can enter, and the outer door must fully close before the inner door opens. Common in data centers and government facilities."}, {s:false,q:"Bollards are PRIMARILY designed to protect against which type of physical attack?",o:["A. Tailgating","B. Shoulder surfing","C. Vehicle ramming","D. RFID cloning"],a:"C",e:"Bollards are short, sturdy posts installed in front of buildings specifically to prevent vehicle-based attacks (car/truck ramming). They protect against physical breaches using vehicles. They have no effect on digital attacks, tailgating (access vestibule), or RFID (signal-based)."}, {s:false,q:"A fake, decoy computer system configured to attract and monitor attackers while appearing to be a legitimate target is called a:",o:["A. Honeynet","B. Honeyfile","C. Honeypot","D. Honeytoken"],a:"C",e:"A honeypot is a single fake, decoy system designed to attract attackers. It looks like a legitimate target but is isolated and heavily monitored. A honeynet is a network of honeypots; a honeyfile is a decoy document; a honeytoken is a fake credential or data record."}, {s:true,sc:"A security team embeds a fake API key with a name like 'prod-database-admin-key' in a public code repository. The key doesn't work against any real system, but if any system attempts to use it, an immediate alert is generated and the source IP is logged.",q:"This is an example of which deception technology?",o:["A. Honeypot","B. Honeynet","C. Honeyfile","D. Honeytoken"],a:"D",e:"A honeytoken is a fake data element (credential, record, token) that has no legitimate use. If it is ever accessed or used, it definitively signals unauthorized activity. Fake API keys, fake credentials, and canary tokens are all examples of honeytokens."}, {s:false,q:"An infrared sensor detects intruders by:",o:["A. Emitting microwave pulses and measuring reflections","B. Detecting changes in heat signatures from moving bodies","C. Measuring pressure changes when weight is applied to a surface","D. Using sound waves to detect movement"],a:"B",e:"Infrared sensors detect heat emitted by living bodies. They identify intruders based on the heat differential between a human body and the surrounding environment. Microwave sensors emit microwave pulses; pressure sensors detect weight; ultrasonic sensors use sound waves."}, {s:false,q:"Which physical security sensor is capable of detecting movement THROUGH walls?",o:["A. Pressure sensor","B. Infrared sensor","C. Ultrasonic sensor","D. Microwave sensor"],a:"D",e:"Microwave sensors emit microwave radiation that can penetrate walls and non-metallic materials to detect movement. This allows them to cover larger areas and detect motion even through partitions. Infrared, pressure, and ultrasonic sensors generally require line-of-sight or direct contact."}, {s:true,sc:"A CISO is tasked with improving the company's security program. She reviews the current controls against the NIST Cybersecurity Framework and identifies 31 specific areas where the organization either has no controls or has weak controls compared to the framework recommendations. She prioritizes these by risk and creates a remediation roadmap.",q:"What process has the CISO performed?",o:["A. Penetration testing","B. Risk transfer analysis","C. Gap analysis","D. Vulnerability scanning"],a:"C",e:"The CISO performed a gap analysis — comparing the current security posture against a target framework (NIST CSF) to identify what is missing ('the gaps'). The output is a prioritized list of gaps with remediation steps. This is a strategic assessment tool common in compliance and maturity programs."}, {s:false,q:"Gap analysis in information security involves:",o:["A. Testing network firewalls for misconfigurations","B. Comparing current security controls to a desired target state or framework","C. Analyzing gaps in physical security barriers","D. Measuring the time between attack detection and response"],a:"B",e:"Gap analysis compares where you are now (current state) against where you need to be (target state — a framework, regulation, or standard). The differences are the 'gaps' that need to be closed through new or improved controls."}, {s:false,q:"An entire fake network of intentionally vulnerable systems, designed to attract and study advanced attackers, is called a:",o:["A. Honeypot","B. Honeynet","C. Honeyfile","D. DMZ"],a:"B",e:"A honeynet is a network of honeypots — a full fake environment with multiple systems, realistic traffic, and detailed logging. It provides a comprehensive view of attacker behavior. A honeypot is just one system; a DMZ is a real network segment, not a deception tool."}, {s:false,q:"A document named 'salary_database_2024.xlsx' is placed on a file server. It contains no real data, but any access to this file triggers an immediate security alert. This is an example of:",o:["A. Honeypot","B. Honeynet","C. Honeyfile","D. Honeytoken"],a:"C",e:"A honeyfile is a fake document placed as bait. Legitimate users have no reason to access it; only an attacker browsing file shares would open it. This makes it a high-fidelity detective control — any access is definitively malicious."}, {s:true,sc:"An organization's data integrity policy requires that all files stored on the backup server must have their hash values compared against the original hash values recorded at the time of backup, every time they are restored.",q:"This process PRIMARILY protects which component of the CIA Triad?",o:["A. Confidentiality","B. Availability","C. Non-repudiation","D. Integrity"],a:"D",e:"Comparing hash values verifies that files have not been altered since they were backed up. This protects Integrity — ensuring data is accurate and has not been tampered with. If the restored hash matches the original, the data is unchanged and trustworthy."}, {s:false,q:"Which Zero Trust concept involves dividing network infrastructure into small, isolated segments where each segment has its own access controls and default-deny posture?",o:["A. Adaptive Identity","B. Implicit Trust Zones","C. Policy-Driven Access Control","D. Threat Scope Reduction via micro-segmentation"],a:"D",e:"Threat Scope Reduction through micro-segmentation divides the network into isolated zones. Each segment has strict access controls and default-deny rules. This limits lateral movement — an attacker who compromises one segment cannot freely access others."}, {s:false,q:"The Zero Trust Data Plane contains which of the following components?",o:["A. Policy Engine and Policy Administrator","B. Adaptive Identity and Policy Engine","C. Policy Enforcement Point and Implicit Trust Zones","D. Threat Scope Reduction and Policy Administrator"],a:"C",e:"The Data Plane is where actual network traffic flows and is enforced. It contains: the Policy Enforcement Point (PEP), Subjects/Systems (the requesters), and Implicit Trust Zones. The Control Plane (PE, PA, Adaptive Identity, Policy-Driven Access) makes decisions; the Data Plane enforces them."}, {s:true,sc:"An auditor reviews access logs and discovers that a warehouse worker has been accessing the payroll system from his workstation for the past three weeks. When confronted, he claims 'I never accessed payroll — my computer must have been hacked.' The logs show his authenticated session ID, his username, and the specific records he accessed, with timestamps.",q:"Which security principle do these audit logs PRIMARILY support?",o:["A. Confidentiality","B. Availability","C. Non-repudiation","D. Least Privilege"],a:"C",e:"The audit logs provide irrefutable evidence that prevents the worker from denying his actions — this is non-repudiation. The authenticated session tied to his credentials proves it was him. Non-repudiation ensures parties cannot credibly deny performing an action."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following BEST describes the role of the Policy Administrator in Zero Trust?",o:["A. Makes the grant or deny trust decision","B. Physically blocks or allows traffic between subject and resource","C. Communicates the Policy Engine's decision to the Policy Enforcement Point","D. Stores and manages user credentials"],a:"C",e:"The Policy Administrator (PA) acts as the bridge between the Control Plane and the Data Plane. It takes the Policy Engine's decision and communicates it to the Policy Enforcement Point. The flow: PE decides → PA communicates → PEP enforces. The PA establishes or terminates communication paths."}, {s:false,q:"Ultrasonic sensors detect movement by:",o:["A. Detecting heat signatures from living bodies","B. Emitting sound waves and measuring changes in the reflected signal","C. Detecting pressure changes on floor surfaces","D. Emitting microwave pulses through walls"],a:"B",e:"Ultrasonic sensors emit high-frequency sound waves and detect changes in the reflected signal caused by movement. They are commonly used in indoor environments. Infrared detects heat; pressure sensors detect weight; microwave sensors can penetrate walls."}, ]; // ─── OBJECTIVE 1.3 QUESTIONS (25) ─── const Q_1_3 = [ {s:false,q:"What is the PRIMARY purpose of change management in an IT security context?",o:["A. To speed up software deployments","B. To ensure changes are reviewed, tested, approved, and reversible to minimize security risk","C. To automate system updates without human intervention","D. To document user complaints about system changes"],a:"B",e:"Change management exists to control the risk that comes with modifying IT systems. Every change could break security controls, introduce vulnerabilities, or cause outages. A formal process ensures changes are properly reviewed, tested, approved, and can be rolled back if needed."}, {s:false,q:"What is a 'backout plan' in change management?",o:["A. A plan to exit the organization after a failed project","B. A documented procedure to reverse a change if it fails or causes problems","C. A backup copy of data before the change","D. A plan for decommissioning old systems"],a:"B",e:"A backout plan (also called a rollback plan) is a documented procedure to reverse a change if it causes problems. It must be prepared and approved BEFORE the change is implemented. Exam tip: 'What must be prepared before implementing a change?' → Backout/rollback plan."}, {s:true,sc:"A junior administrator applies a security patch to a production web server without following the change management process. The patch causes the server to crash, taking down the company's customer-facing website for 4 hours during peak business hours.",q:"Which change management requirement was MOST directly violated that would have prevented this situation?",o:["A. Stakeholder notification","B. Approval process and testing in a non-production environment","C. Version control","D. Impact analysis documentation"],a:"B",e:"The critical failures were: no formal approval process and no testing in a non-production environment. Had the patch been tested first, the crash would have been identified before production impact. The approval process would have required this testing before changes could proceed."}, {s:false,q:"A 'maintenance window' in change management refers to:",o:["A. The time required to review and approve a change request","B. A scheduled period of low business activity when changes are applied to minimize impact","C. The window of time that a vulnerability is exposed before patching","D. The time allocated to test a change after implementation"],a:"B",e:"A maintenance window is a pre-agreed time period (typically nights, weekends, or low-traffic periods) when changes are scheduled. This minimizes disruption to business operations. Changes applied outside approved maintenance windows violate change management policy."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following BEST describes 'impact analysis' in change management?",o:["A. Analyzing the performance impact of the change on server resources","B. Evaluating what could go wrong with the change and what systems/users will be affected","C. Measuring the financial cost of implementing the change","D. Assessing user satisfaction after the change is deployed"],a:"B",e:"Impact analysis evaluates: What could go wrong? What systems or dependencies could be affected? What is the risk if the change fails? Who will be impacted? This step is essential before approval — it identifies risks so they can be mitigated or accepted."}, {s:true,sc:"A company needs to deploy a new security certificate to their customer web portal. The change management process requires: stakeholder sign-off, a test deployment to staging, a documented rollback procedure, and deployment during the Saturday 2am-6am maintenance window. The security team wants to deploy on Wednesday at 2pm instead to meet a compliance deadline.",q:"What should the security team do?",o:["A. Deploy immediately — security compliance always overrides change management","B. Submit an emergency change request through the formal process and get expedited approval","C. Deploy without approval since it's only a certificate","D. Wait until Saturday regardless of the compliance deadline"],a:"B",e:"Even urgent changes should go through an expedited or emergency change request process. Most change management frameworks have provisions for emergency changes with accelerated approval. Bypassing change management entirely — even for certificates — creates risk and audit failures."}, {s:false,q:"In change management, 'ownership' refers to:",o:["A. The department that owns the budget for the change","B. The individual accountable for a change from submission through completion","C. The vendor who owns the software being changed","D. Legal ownership of the intellectual property modified"],a:"B",e:"Ownership in change management refers to assigning one individual who is accountable for the change from start to finish. This person ensures the change follows the process, communicates with stakeholders, and is responsible if something goes wrong. Clear ownership prevents 'no one is responsible' situations."}, {s:false,q:"Version control in change management PRIMARILY provides which security benefit?",o:["A. Encrypts configuration files to prevent unauthorized access","B. Automatically deploys changes to all systems simultaneously","C. Maintains a complete history of changes enabling rollback to previous known-good states","D. Monitors for unauthorized changes in real time"],a:"C",e:"Version control maintains a complete history of all changes to files and configurations. This allows: rollback to previous states, identification of when a problem was introduced, attribution (who changed what and when), and audit trails. Git is the most common version control tool."}, {s:true,sc:"After a change to the firewall rule set, users report being unable to access the company's CRM application. The change was implemented 2 hours ago by a junior admin. The team needs to restore access as quickly as possible.",q:"Which change management artifact would MOST quickly resolve this situation?",o:["A. The impact analysis from before the change","B. The documented backout plan prepared before the change was implemented","C. The stakeholder approval email","D. The maintenance window schedule"],a:"B",e:"The backout plan (pre-prepared rollback procedure) should immediately restore the previous firewall rule set. This is exactly why backout plans are required before changes are approved — to enable rapid recovery when changes cause problems. Without it, the team would need to manually figure out what was changed."}, {s:false,q:"Allow lists and deny lists are MOST directly associated with which aspect of change management's technical implications?",o:["A. Version control","B. Backout planning","C. Restricted activities and technical configuration changes","D. Documentation updates"],a:"C",e:"Allow lists and deny lists are security configurations that often need to be updated when changes are made — for example, a new application might need to be added to a firewall allow list. Change management ensures these lists are properly updated as part of the change, not forgotten, which could create security gaps."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following is a 'technical implication' that must be considered in change management?",o:["A. Who are the project stakeholders","B. Whether the change requires board approval","C. Dependencies — other systems that rely on the system being changed","D. The training budget for users"],a:"C",e:"Dependencies are a key technical implication — a change to one system might break other systems that depend on it. For example, updating a database version might break applications that use that database. Identifying and testing dependencies prevents cascading failures."}, {s:true,sc:"A software development company uses a change management system where every configuration file is stored in a repository. Each change is labeled with a version number, the developer who made it, and a timestamp. Before any deployment, the configuration can be compared to the previous version to identify exactly what changed.",q:"This system MOST directly supports which change management principle?",o:["A. Impact analysis","B. Backout planning","C. Version control","D. Stakeholder management"],a:"C",e:"The system described — repository storage, version numbers, attribution, timestamps, and diff capabilities — is a textbook implementation of version control. It provides an auditable history of every change, enabling rollback, forensic investigation, and compliance reporting."}, {s:false,q:"What is a 'Standard Operating Procedure' (SOP) in the context of change management?",o:["A. A legal document authorizing the change","B. A documented step-by-step process that staff follow to implement routine changes consistently","C. A formal request submitted to the Change Advisory Board","D. An automated script that deploys changes"],a:"B",e:"A Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) is a documented, step-by-step process for performing routine tasks consistently. In change management, SOPs ensure that common changes (like password resets, certificate renewals, or firewall rule updates) are performed the same way every time, reducing errors and security gaps."}, {s:false,q:"When a system must be restarted as part of a change, this falls under which category of technical implications in change management?",o:["A. Documentation","B. Version control","C. Service restart and downtime","D. Legacy application considerations"],a:"C",e:"Service restarts and downtime are specific technical implications that must be planned for. A service restart may be required after applying patches or configuration changes. This must be communicated in advance and scheduled within the maintenance window to minimize business impact."}, {s:true,sc:"A financial services company is migrating from an on-premises email system to a cloud-based solution. After the migration, the company's network diagrams still show the old on-premises email server, and the acceptable use policy still references the old system.",q:"What change management process has been NEGLECTED?",o:["A. Impact analysis","B. Approval process","C. Documentation updates — updating diagrams and policies","D. Backout planning"],a:"C",e:"After a change, documentation must be updated to reflect the new reality. This includes network diagrams, system inventories, policies, and procedures. Stale documentation creates security risks (staff following outdated procedures), compliance issues, and confusion during incidents. Documentation updates are a critical but often overlooked change management step."}, {s:false,q:"'Test results' as a component of change management refers to:",o:["A. Results of penetration tests against the production system","B. Evidence that a proposed change has been tested in a non-production environment before approval","C. User acceptance test results submitted by end users","D. Performance benchmark results from the current system"],a:"B",e:"Test results in change management are the documented outcomes of testing the proposed change in a staging or dev environment before production deployment. They prove the change works as expected and that testing was performed. Without test results, a change cannot be approved."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following describes a 'legacy application' consideration in change management?",o:["A. Applications that were built using the latest development framework","B. Modern cloud-native applications","C. Older applications that may not support changes, updates, or integrations with newer systems","D. Applications that handle legacy data formats only"],a:"C",e:"Legacy applications are older systems that often cannot be easily patched, updated, or integrated with modern systems. Change management must account for these applications because changes that work for modern systems might break legacy apps, or legacy apps might prevent certain changes from being implemented."}, {s:true,sc:"An IT team is planning to upgrade the authentication system from LDAP to a modern SSO solution. The team identifies 14 applications that currently authenticate against the LDAP server and will need to be reconfigured. They also identify three applications from 2008 that may not support the new authentication method.",q:"The three 2008 applications represent which change management consideration?",o:["A. Version control challenge","B. Legacy application dependencies","C. Backout plan requirement","D. Stakeholder impact"],a:"B",e:"The three 2008 applications are legacy applications with dependencies on the old authentication system. They may not support modern protocols, requiring compensating controls or exceptions. This is exactly the type of dependency that impact analysis must identify before a change is approved."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following BEST describes the role of stakeholders in change management?",o:["A. Stakeholders only need to be notified after a change is completed","B. Relevant parties whose operations or responsibilities will be affected by a change, who review and provide sign-off","C. External auditors who verify the change complies with regulations","D. Only the IT team members directly implementing the change"],a:"B",e:"Stakeholders are individuals or teams affected by a proposed change — this could include the business unit, IT operations, security team, legal, and management. They must review the change, assess its impact on their area, and provide sign-off as part of the approval process."}, {s:false,q:"Updating network diagrams after a change is implemented is an example of:",o:["A. Impact analysis","B. Version control","C. Documentation update","D. Backout planning"],a:"C",e:"Updating network diagrams is a documentation update — a required step after any change that modifies the network architecture. Accurate documentation is critical for security (helps identify unauthorized changes), operations (troubleshooting), and compliance (evidence of controlled environment)."}, {s:true,sc:"A change manager reviews a proposed change request to replace the company's SSL certificates. She notes the request has: a detailed description of the change, stakeholder approvals, test results from staging, and a maintenance window of Sunday 1am-4am. However, she refuses to approve it.",q:"What critical element is MOST likely MISSING from the change request?",o:["A. Technical description of the change","B. Stakeholder approvals","C. A documented backout plan in case the certificate change fails","D. The date of the change"],a:"C",e:"The critical missing element is the backout plan. A backout plan is mandatory before any change is approved. If the SSL certificate change fails (e.g., wrong certificate, configuration error), the team needs a documented procedure to quickly revert to the previous state. Without it, a failed change could leave the website inaccessible."}, {s:false,q:"What does 'restricted activities' mean as a technical implication of change management?",o:["A. Activities that require a security clearance to perform","B. Certain actions (like production deployments or firewall changes) that can only be done during approved change windows","C. Activities restricted to senior management only","D. Physical activities restricted to certain zones of the data center"],a:"B",e:"Restricted activities are operations that may only be performed in accordance with change management policy — for example, production deployments, firewall modifications, or schema changes can only happen during approved maintenance windows with proper authorization. This prevents unauthorized or uncontrolled changes."}, {s:false,q:"When a change causes 'downtime,' what is the change manager's PRIMARY responsibility?",o:["A. Implement the change anyway and apologize afterwards","B. Cancel the change permanently","C. Ensure the expected downtime is communicated to stakeholders and scheduled within an approved maintenance window","D. Transfer responsibility to the vendor"],a:"C",e:"Planned downtime must be communicated in advance and scheduled within an approved maintenance window. The change manager ensures affected users and teams are notified, expectations are set, and the downtime occurs when it will have the least business impact."}, {s:true,sc:"A security engineer makes an unauthorized change to the production firewall by adding a temporary rule to allow traffic for a project. She doesn't submit a change request, doesn't document the rule, and plans to remove it later. Three months later, she leaves the company. The rule is still in the firewall.",q:"Which change management failure MOST directly caused the long-term security risk?",o:["A. Lack of backout plan","B. Lack of maintenance window adherence","C. Unauthorized change without documentation and approval — no audit trail or ownership","D. Missing test results"],a:"C",e:"The root cause is no change management process followed — no authorization, no documentation, no ownership. Because the change wasn't documented, no one knew the rule existed when she left. This is exactly why change management exists: to ensure all changes are tracked, owned, and removable. Undocumented changes become permanent security risks."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following demonstrates proper use of version control in change management?",o:["A. Emailing the new configuration file to the team","B. Storing all configuration changes in a Git repository with commit messages, author attribution, and timestamps","C. Keeping a spreadsheet of the last five changes made to each server","D. Taking a screenshot of the configuration before making changes"],a:"B",e:"A Git repository with commit messages, author attribution, and timestamps is the proper implementation of version control. It provides: full change history, the ability to diff versions, rollback to any previous state, audit trail, and attribution. Screenshots and spreadsheets are informal and not version control."}, {s:false,q:"The change management 'approval process' serves which primary security purpose?",o:["A. To increase bureaucracy and slow down IT","B. To ensure changes are reviewed by the right people, risks are assessed, and only authorized changes are made to production","C. To give management visibility into IT costs","D. To ensure vendors are notified before changes"],a:"B",e:"The approval process ensures: the right stakeholders have reviewed and accepted the risk, testing has been completed, a backout plan exists, and the change is authorized. This prevents unauthorized, untested, or risky changes from reaching production — which is a major source of security incidents and outages."}, ]; // ─── OBJECTIVE 1.4 QUESTIONS (25) ─── const Q_1_4 = [ {s:false,q:"Which type of encryption uses the same key for both encryption and decryption?",o:["A. Asymmetric","B. Public key","C. Symmetric","D. Hashing"],a:"C",e:"Symmetric encryption uses a single shared key for both encryption and decryption. It is fast and efficient for large data but has the 'key distribution problem' — how do you securely share the key? Examples: AES, DES, 3DES. Asymmetric uses a key pair (public/private)."}, {s:false,q:"What is the key distribution problem, and which encryption type solves it?",o:["A. Symmetric encryption — by using the same key everywhere","B. Asymmetric encryption — using a public key that can be shared freely","C. Hashing — by converting the key to a fixed-length digest","D. Salting — by adding randomness to the key"],a:"B",e:"The key distribution problem: if you use symmetric encryption, how do you securely share the key with the recipient? Asymmetric encryption solves this — you share your public key freely (it can't decrypt), and anyone uses it to encrypt data that only your private key can decrypt."}, {s:true,sc:"A company needs to encrypt 2 terabytes of data stored on backup tapes. Performance is critical — the encryption must complete in under 2 hours. Security requirements only need to prevent unauthorized access if tapes are stolen.",q:"Which encryption approach is MOST appropriate?",o:["A. RSA asymmetric encryption","B. AES symmetric encryption","C. ECC asymmetric encryption","D. SHA-256 hashing"],a:"B",e:"AES symmetric encryption is the correct choice. Symmetric encryption is significantly faster than asymmetric — critical when encrypting 2 TB. AES is the industry standard for bulk data encryption. Asymmetric (RSA, ECC) is too slow for large data volumes. SHA-256 is a hash — it doesn't encrypt."}, {s:false,q:"A Trusted Platform Module (TPM) is BEST described as:",o:["A. A software key management application","B. A dedicated security chip on the motherboard that stores cryptographic keys and verifies boot integrity","C. A hardware security device used in data centers for key management at scale","D. A cloud-based certificate authority"],a:"B",e:"A TPM is a dedicated microchip physically embedded in the motherboard. It securely stores cryptographic keys, certificates, and passwords, and verifies the system hasn't been tampered with during boot (measured boot). BitLocker uses the TPM to store its encryption key."}, {s:false,q:"What distinguishes a Hardware Security Module (HSM) from a TPM?",o:["A. HSM is a software tool; TPM is hardware","B. HSM is a dedicated, tamper-resistant external device for high-volume key management; TPM is a chip on the motherboard","C. TPM is used by enterprises; HSM is only for personal devices","D. There is no practical difference between HSM and TPM"],a:"B",e:"An HSM is a dedicated, tamper-resistant hardware device designed for enterprise-scale cryptographic operations — generating, storing, and managing keys at high volume. A TPM is a chip on a single computer's motherboard for that specific machine. HSMs are used by CAs, banks, and cloud providers for bulk key operations."}, {s:true,sc:"A payment processor stores credit card numbers in their database. To comply with PCI-DSS, they replace each card number with a randomly generated alphanumeric string. The original card number is stored in a secure vault system, and the random string is stored in the application database. When a payment is processed, the vault is queried to retrieve the original number.",q:"This technique is BEST described as:",o:["A. Encryption","B. Hashing","C. Tokenization","D. Data masking"],a:"C",e:"Tokenization replaces sensitive data with a non-sensitive substitute (token). The original value is stored separately in a secure vault. The token has no mathematical relationship to the original data (unlike encryption). PCI-DSS tokenization is a classic exam scenario."}, {s:false,q:"What is steganography?",o:["A. A method of encrypting data using a public key","B. The practice of hiding secret data within ordinary, non-secret files or messages","C. A technique for securely transmitting encryption keys","D. A hash function used to verify file integrity"],a:"B",e:"Steganography hides the existence of a message, not just its contents. A secret message might be hidden in the pixels of an image, in whitespace of a document, or in audio file data. Unlike encryption (which hides meaning), steganography hides the fact that a message exists at all."}, {s:false,q:"What is the purpose of 'salting' in password storage?",o:["A. To encrypt passwords before storing them","B. To add a unique random value to each password before hashing to defeat rainbow table attacks","C. To stretch the key over multiple rounds of encryption","D. To digitally sign the password hash"],a:"B",e:"Salting adds a unique, random value to each password before it is hashed. This ensures that even if two users have the same password, their hashes are different. It defeats rainbow table attacks (pre-computed hash lookup tables) because the attacker would need a separate rainbow table for every possible salt value."}, {s:true,sc:"An attacker obtains a database of password hashes. She has a list of common passwords with their MD5 hashes and plans to look up each hash in the list to find matches. However, every hash in the database has extra characters appended before hashing, making her lookup table useless.",q:"Which cryptographic technique defeated the attack?",o:["A. Key stretching","B. Tokenization","C. Salting","D. Digital signatures"],a:"C",e:"Salting defeated the rainbow table attack. By appending a unique random value (the salt) to each password before hashing, the stored hashes don't match any pre-computed table. The attacker would need to recompute the entire rainbow table for each unique salt — computationally infeasible."}, {s:false,q:"Which cryptographic concept involves intentionally making the hashing process computationally slow to defend against brute-force attacks?",o:["A. Salting","B. Key stretching","C. Tokenization","D. Steganography"],a:"B",e:"Key stretching (also called key derivation) uses algorithms like PBKDF2, bcrypt, or scrypt that deliberately perform thousands of hash iterations, making each hash attempt take milliseconds instead of nanoseconds. This dramatically slows down brute-force attacks. Even 1,000 iterations per hash makes cracking 1,000x harder."}, {s:false,q:"What is the PRIMARY purpose of a digital signature?",o:["A. To encrypt a message so only the recipient can read it","B. To prove authenticity and integrity — confirming who sent a message and that it wasn't altered","C. To provide a backup copy of a file in case of data loss","D. To hash a file for storage efficiency"],a:"B",e:"A digital signature provides: Authenticity (proves who sent it — only the private key holder can create it) and Integrity (detects if the message was altered — the signature fails to verify if the content changes). It does NOT provide confidentiality — the message can still be read by others."}, {s:true,sc:"A software company wants to ensure that users can verify that software downloaded from their website has not been tampered with or modified by a third party since it was released.",q:"Which cryptographic technique should the company use?",o:["A. Symmetric encryption of the software package","B. Publishing a digital signature or hash of the software signed with the company's private key","C. Storing the software in an HSM","D. Using tokenization on the download link"],a:"B",e:"Publishing a digital signature (or signed hash) allows users to verify: (1) the software came from the legitimate company (authenticity — only their private key could create the signature) and (2) it hasn't been modified (integrity — the hash would change if any bit was altered). This is code signing."}, {s:false,q:"A Certificate Revocation List (CRL) serves which purpose?",o:["A. Lists all certificates that have been issued by a Certificate Authority","B. Lists certificates that have been revoked before their expiry date","C. Provides real-time certificate status checking","D. Contains the Certificate Authority's public key"],a:"B",e:"A CRL is a list published by a Certificate Authority of certificates that have been revoked before their scheduled expiry — due to compromise, personnel changes, or decommissioning. Clients download the CRL and check it before trusting a certificate. OCSP provides the same service but in real-time."}, {s:false,q:"OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol) improves upon CRL by:",o:["A. Providing real-time, per-certificate status checks without downloading the full revocation list","B. Encrypting the certificate to prevent tampering","C. Automatically renewing certificates before they expire","D. Replacing the need for Certificate Authorities"],a:"A",e:"OCSP allows clients to query the status of a specific certificate in real time. Unlike a CRL (which is a periodically published list that must be fully downloaded), OCSP provides an immediate response for a single certificate. This is more efficient and provides more current information."}, {s:true,sc:"A Certificate Authority (CA) issues certificates to companies and individuals. The CA has a root certificate that is self-signed. It also operates two intermediate CAs that it signs, and those intermediate CAs sign end-entity certificates for websites.",q:"This describes which cryptographic infrastructure?",o:["A. Symmetric key exchange","B. Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) with a certificate chain of trust","C. A Hardware Security Module hierarchy","D. A blockchain certificate ledger"],a:"B",e:"This describes PKI (Public Key Infrastructure) with a chain of trust: Root CA → Intermediate CA → End-entity certificates. The root CA is the ultimate trust anchor (self-signed), intermediates distribute trust, and end-entity certificates are used by websites. Browsers trust websites because they can verify the entire chain back to a trusted root."}, {s:false,q:"What is a Certificate Signing Request (CSR)?",o:["A. A request to revoke an existing certificate","B. A document sent to a CA containing the applicant's public key and identity information, requesting a signed certificate","C. A request for the CA to extend a certificate's expiry date","D. An automated process for renewing certificates without human intervention"],a:"B",e:"A CSR is generated by the entity that wants a certificate (e.g., a web server admin). It contains the entity's public key and identity details (domain name, organization). It's sent to a CA, which verifies the identity and, if satisfied, signs the certificate and returns it. The private key never leaves the applicant."}, {s:false,q:"Data masking is BEST used in which scenario?",o:["A. Protecting data in transit between servers","B. Replacing sensitive data with realistic but fictional data for use in development and testing environments","C. Hiding secret messages inside image files","D. Generating a fixed-length digest of a file"],a:"B",e:"Data masking replaces real sensitive data with realistic-looking but fictional data. This allows developers and testers to work with realistic data structures without exposing actual PII, financial records, or PHI. For example, replacing real customer names and credit cards with fake-but-valid-format data in a test database."}, {s:true,sc:"A developer copies the production database to the development environment to test a new feature. The copy includes real customer names, email addresses, and payment card numbers.",q:"Which technique should have been applied to the data BEFORE copying it to the development environment?",o:["A. Encryption with AES","B. Tokenization only","C. Data masking","D. Digital signatures"],a:"C",e:"Data masking is specifically designed for this scenario — replacing real sensitive data with realistic but fictional data for development and testing environments. While encryption and tokenization protect data in storage, they don't allow developers to actually work with the data format naturally. Masking gives them useful data without exposure."}, {s:false,q:"A wildcard certificate (*.domain.com) provides which capability?",o:["A. Encrypts all traffic to all ports on the domain","B. Covers all first-level subdomains of the domain with a single certificate","C. Allows the certificate to be reused across different organizations","D. Provides both encryption and digital signing capabilities"],a:"B",e:"A wildcard certificate covers all first-level subdomains: *.domain.com covers mail.domain.com, www.domain.com, app.domain.com, etc. — with a single certificate. It does NOT cover sub-subdomains (sub.sub.domain.com) or the domain itself (domain.com without a prefix) unless both *.domain.com and domain.com are included."}, {s:false,q:"What is key escrow?",o:["A. The process of discarding old encryption keys","B. Storing a copy of an encryption key with a trusted third party for authorized recovery","C. Distributing encryption keys to multiple users simultaneously","D. The automatic rotation of keys on a scheduled basis"],a:"B",e:"Key escrow stores a copy of an encryption key with a trusted third party (an escrow agent). This allows authorized recovery if the key owner loses their key or for legal/law enforcement access. The risk: if the escrow agent is compromised, the escrowed keys are compromised. It's a balance between security and recovery."}, {s:true,sc:"A company discovers that an employee suspected of fraud has encrypted all documents on their work laptop using their personal encryption key. The employee refuses to provide the key. Law enforcement has a valid court order to access the documents.",q:"Which cryptographic mechanism, if implemented beforehand, would have allowed authorized access to the encrypted data?",o:["A. Salting","B. Digital signatures","C. Key escrow","D. Tokenization"],a:"C",e:"Key escrow would have allowed this — if the company had stored a copy of the encryption key with a trusted escrow agent, they could have used the court order to retrieve the key and decrypt the documents. Without key escrow, encrypted data is inaccessible if the key holder won't cooperate."}, {s:false,q:"Which hashing algorithm produces a 256-bit digest and is commonly used for file integrity verification and digital signatures?",o:["A. MD5","B. SHA-1","C. SHA-256","D. DES"],a:"C",e:"SHA-256 (part of the SHA-2 family) produces a 256-bit hash digest. It is the current standard for file integrity verification, digital signatures, and certificate signing. MD5 (128-bit) and SHA-1 (160-bit) are considered broken/deprecated due to collision vulnerabilities. DES is an encryption algorithm, not a hash."}, {s:false,q:"Full disk encryption (FDE) PRIMARILY protects against which threat?",o:["A. Malware executing on a running system","B. Unauthorized access to data if a physical device is lost or stolen","C. Man-in-the-middle attacks on network traffic","D. Brute-force password attacks over the network"],a:"B",e:"Full disk encryption encrypts the entire storage device. Its primary protection is against physical theft — if a laptop is stolen, the attacker cannot read the data without the decryption key. FDE does NOT protect a running system (the drive is decrypted while in use) or network-based attacks."}, {s:true,sc:"A financial services company is deploying a new certificate infrastructure. They want to ensure that even if an Intermediate CA's private key is compromised, the impact is limited and they don't have to invalidate ALL certificates issued by their entire PKI hierarchy.",q:"Which PKI design principle does this requirement reflect?",o:["A. Using self-signed certificates for all servers","B. Separating the Root CA and Intermediate CAs — keeping the Root CA offline","C. Using wildcard certificates everywhere","D. Implementing OCSP instead of CRL"],a:"B",e:"Keeping the Root CA offline and signing through Intermediate CAs limits the blast radius of a CA key compromise. If an Intermediate CA is compromised, only certificates signed by that intermediate need to be revoked — not the entire PKI hierarchy.
