The Root CA key, kept offline, is never exposed to network attacks."}, {s:false,q:"Transport-level encryption protects data in which state?",o:["A. Data at rest — stored on hard drives","B. Data in use — actively being processed in RAM","C. Data in transit — moving across a network","D. Data in archive — stored in cold storage"],a:"C",e:"Transport encryption (TLS, HTTPS, IPSec, SSH) protects data in transit — data moving across a network. It prevents eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks. Data at rest is protected by storage encryption (FDE, file encryption); data in use is protected by memory encryption and secure enclaves."}, ]; // ─── FINAL EXAM QUESTIONS ─── // 50 CompTIA-generated + 100 placeholders for instructor questions // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ // DOMAIN 2.0 — THREATS, VULNERABILITIES & MITIGATIONS // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ const Q_2_1 = [ // Compare/contrast threat actors & motivations {s:false,q:"Which threat actor type is characterized by having the GREATEST resources, nation-state backing, and willingness to conduct long-term covert operations?",o:["A. Script Kiddie","B. Hacktivist","C. Nation-State","D. Insider Threat"],a:"C",e:"Nation-state actors are sponsored by governments and have the highest resources, most sophisticated tools, and longest patience for persistent attacks (APTs). They conduct espionage, sabotage, and influence operations against other nations and critical infrastructure."}, {s:false,q:"An employee who uses their legitimate access to steal customer data for personal financial gain is BEST described as which type of threat actor?",o:["A. Nation-State","B. Hacktivist","C. Insider Threat","D. Organized Crime"],a:"C",e:"An insider threat is someone with authorized access who misuses it — either maliciously (intentional theft, sabotage) or accidentally (negligence). Insiders are particularly dangerous because they bypass perimeter controls and already have trust."}, {s:false,q:"Which threat actor is PRIMARILY motivated by ideological, political, or social beliefs and uses hacking to promote their cause?",o:["A. Organized Crime","B. Nation-State","C. Script Kiddie","D. Hacktivist"],a:"D",e:"A hacktivist is motivated by political, social, or ideological causes — using hacking as a form of protest or activism. Examples include defacing government websites, DDoS attacks on companies they oppose, or leaking sensitive documents to expose wrongdoing."}, {s:false,q:"A threat actor uses pre-written exploit tools downloaded from the internet with no understanding of how they work. This actor is BEST described as:",o:["A. Nation-State","B. Unskilled Attacker (Script Kiddie)","C. Hacktivist","D. Organized Crime"],a:"B",e:"An unskilled attacker (script kiddie) uses existing exploit tools, scripts, or malware without deep technical knowledge. They rely on the work of more skilled attackers. They have low sophistication but can still cause significant damage using powerful tools."}, {s:true,sc:"A large corporation discovers that attackers have been silently present in their network for 18 months, systematically exfiltrating intellectual property related to defense contracts. The attack used custom zero-day exploits and showed signs of significant resource investment.",q:"Which type of threat actor MOST likely conducted this attack?",o:["A. Script Kiddie","B. Hacktivist","C. Insider Threat","D. Nation-State"],a:"D",e:"This describes an Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) — a long-term, resource-intensive, covert operation targeting high-value assets. The use of custom zero-days, patience (18 months), and focus on defense IP are hallmarks of a nation-state actor."}, {s:false,q:"Which threat actor motivation involves attempting to gather sensitive government or corporate intelligence for competitive or strategic advantage?",o:["A. Financial Gain","B. Revenge","C. Espionage","D. Disruption/Chaos"],a:"C",e:"Espionage as a motivation involves covertly gathering sensitive intelligence — government secrets, military plans, trade secrets, or competitive business information. Nation-states and organized crime groups conduct espionage, often over long periods without detection."}, {s:false,q:"An attacker compromises a company's systems and threatens to publish sensitive customer data unless a payment is made. Which motivation BEST describes this?",o:["A. Espionage","B. Blackmail","C. Disruption","D. Hacktivism"],a:"B",e:"Blackmail (extortion) involves threatening to expose sensitive or damaging information unless demands are met. This differs from ransomware (which encrypts data) — blackmail specifically threatens publication of already-stolen data. Also called 'double extortion' when combined with ransomware."}, {s:false,q:"Shadow IT MOST directly represents which category of threat?",o:["A. External Nation-State Attack","B. Insider Risk from unsanctioned technology","C. Supply Chain Compromise","D. Zero-Day Exploit"],a:"B",e:"Shadow IT refers to systems, applications, or services used by employees without IT department approval or knowledge. It creates insider risk because these tools may not meet security standards, could exfiltrate data, or introduce vulnerabilities without the security team's awareness."}, {s:true,sc:"A disgruntled employee who was recently passed over for promotion deliberately deletes critical customer records from the database before resigning.",q:"Which attributes BEST describe this threat actor?",o:["A. External, high sophistication, financial motivation","B. Internal, low-to-medium sophistication, revenge motivation","C. External, nation-state, espionage motivation","D. Internal, organized crime, financial motivation"],a:"B",e:"This is an insider threat with: Internal (employee with access), low-medium sophistication (used legitimate access to delete, no advanced techniques), motivated by revenge (passed over for promotion). Understanding actor attributes helps predict and detect behavior patterns."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following BEST describes organized crime as a threat actor?",o:["A. Government-sponsored hackers conducting espionage","B. Individual hackers using pre-built tools for notoriety","C. Coordinated criminal groups motivated primarily by financial gain through cybercrime","D. Activists hacking to protest corporate policies"],a:"C",e:"Organized crime groups operate like businesses — motivated by financial profit. They conduct ransomware campaigns, banking trojans, fraud operations, and stolen data sales. They have significant resources and technical capability but focus on profit rather than espionage or disruption."}, {s:false,q:"Which threat actor attribute distinguishes nation-state actors from most other threat actors?",o:["A. They only attack during business hours","B. They have virtually unlimited resources and sophisticated capabilities","C. They always use publicly available exploit tools","D. They are always located within the target country"],a:"B",e:"Nation-state actors are distinguished by virtually unlimited resources and funding from their sponsoring government. This enables custom malware development, zero-day acquisition, large teams, and long-term persistent operations that other threat actors cannot sustain."}, {s:false,q:"A threat actor's PRIMARY motivation is to cause widespread chaos and undermine public confidence in institutions, with no specific financial or political goal. Which motivation category is this?",o:["A. Financial Gain","B. Espionage","C. Disruption/Chaos","D. Revenge"],a:"C",e:"Disruption/Chaos motivation involves causing damage, confusion, and loss of public trust without a specific financial or ideological objective. Some attackers are motivated purely by the destruction they can cause. This can also be a cover for more strategic motivations."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following BEST describes the level of sophistication of a hacktivist compared to a nation-state actor?",o:["A. Hacktivists are equally sophisticated","B. Hacktivists typically have moderate skills using publicly available tools; nation-states develop custom capabilities","C. Nation-states always use less sophisticated tools to avoid attribution","D. Hacktivists always have more resources than nation-states"],a:"B",e:"Hacktivists typically use publicly available tools (DDoS tools, SQL injection, web defacement) with moderate technical skill. Nation-states develop custom malware, zero-day exploits, and highly sophisticated persistent implants. The gap in sophistication and resources is significant."}, {s:true,sc:"A company's competitor seems to have suspiciously accurate knowledge of the company's product roadmap, pricing strategies, and key personnel. No breach has been detected, but an IT audit reveals that an executive's assistant has been accessing systems outside their job role.",q:"Which threat type and motivation does this MOST suggest?",o:["A. Nation-state espionage — political motivation","B. Insider threat — financial gain or coercion by competitor","C. Hacktivist — ideological motivation","D. Script kiddie — notoriety"],a:"B",e:"This scenario describes an insider threat motivated by financial gain (selling intelligence to a competitor) or possibly coercion. The insider's legitimate access makes them nearly invisible to perimeter security. The pattern of accessing systems outside their role is a key indicator."}, {s:false,q:"Data exfiltration as a threat actor motivation typically serves which ultimate purpose?",o:["A. Causing system downtime","B. Disrupting business operations","C. Stealing sensitive data for financial gain, espionage, or blackmail","D. Defacing websites for publicity"],a:"C",e:"Data exfiltration serves multiple goals: financial gain (selling data on dark web), espionage (stealing state/corporate secrets), or blackmail (threatening to publish). It is a precursor to many other attack objectives. The Confidentiality component of CIA is the primary victim."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following is TRUE about insider threats compared to external threats?",o:["A. Insider threats are always more technically sophisticated","B. Insiders are harder to detect because they have legitimate access and bypass perimeter controls","C. Insider threats are always accidental and never malicious","D. External threats cause more financial damage than insider threats"],a:"B",e:"Insiders are harder to detect because they have legitimate access credentials and bypass perimeter security. Their activities look like normal authorized behavior. This is why insider threat programs focus on behavioral analytics, least privilege, and monitoring for anomalous access patterns."}, {s:false,q:"A competitor hires a former employee to gain access to trade secrets from their previous employer. This is an example of which threat actor type and motivation?",o:["A. Nation-State — Espionage","B. Hacktivist — Ideological","C. Organized Crime — Financial via corporate espionage","D. Script Kiddie — Notoriety"],a:"C",e:"Hiring a former employee to steal trade secrets is corporate espionage conducted for financial competitive advantage. While it shares characteristics with nation-state espionage, the competitive commercial context makes organized crime (or simply corporate espionage) the best fit. This also involves an insider threat element."}, {s:false,q:"Which motivation describes an attacker who targets an organization's critical infrastructure specifically to disrupt a nation's essential services as an act of warfare?",o:["A. Financial Gain","B. Revenge","C. War","D. Disruption/Chaos"],a:"C",e:"War as a motivation involves cyber attacks as an act of warfare — targeting critical infrastructure (power grids, water systems, financial systems) to damage a nation's capabilities. This is state-sponsored and represents the highest-stakes cyber conflict scenario."}, {s:true,sc:"A security researcher discovers that attackers broke into a software company's build system and inserted malicious code into a software update that was distributed to thousands of customers. The attack was attributed to a foreign government.",q:"Which threat actor type and motivation BEST describes this attack?",o:["A. Hacktivist — Ideological","B. Nation-State — Espionage/Disruption via supply chain attack","C. Organized Crime — Financial","D. Insider Threat — Revenge"],a:"B",e:"This describes a nation-state supply chain attack (similar to SolarWinds). By compromising the build system, attackers gained access to all downstream customers simultaneously — maximizing reach with one intrusion. Nation-states use supply chain attacks for large-scale espionage and disruption."}, {s:false,q:"Which resource attribute MOST directly enables nation-state actors to conduct zero-day exploit development?",o:["A. Low funding and volunteer contributors","B. Significant government funding and teams of skilled researchers","C. Access to public bug bounty programs","D. Use of open-source security tools"],a:"B",e:"Zero-day discovery and exploitation requires significant funding and teams of skilled security researchers — resources only available to nation-states or very well-funded organizations. Finding and weaponizing unknown vulnerabilities takes substantial time, skill, and investment."}, {s:false,q:"An attacker who was fired sends a threatening email saying they will delete all backups unless paid. Which motivation category does this represent?",o:["A. War","B. Espionage","C. Blackmail","D. Financial Gain through Ransomware"],a:"C",e:"Threatening to cause damage unless payment is made is blackmail/extortion. The context (fired employee with potential knowledge of backup systems) also makes this an insider threat (former insider). The specific motivation — payment to prevent deliberate destruction — is blackmail."}, {s:false,q:"Which threat actor attribute refers to whether the attacker is an employee/contractor versus an outsider?",o:["A. Resources/Funding","B. Level of Sophistication","C. Internal/External","D. Motivation"],a:"C",e:"Internal/External is a key attribute that determines what access the threat actor has, how they gained it, and how difficult they are to detect. Internal actors have legitimate access; external actors must first breach perimeter defenses. Insider threats are internal; nation-states are typically external."}, {s:false,q:"A hacktivist group takes down a pharmaceutical company's website to protest pricing policies. Which two attributes BEST describe this group?",o:["A. External, moderate sophistication, ideological motivation","B. Internal, high sophistication, financial motivation","C. External, nation-state level, espionage motivation","D. Internal, low sophistication, disruption motivation"],a:"A",e:"Hacktivists are typically external attackers with moderate technical sophistication (using publicly available DDoS tools, SQL injection) motivated by ideological/political beliefs. They are not nation-state level but are more capable than script kiddies."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following threat actor motivations is MOST associated with ransomware attacks?",o:["A. Espionage","B. Ideological","C. Financial Gain","D. Disruption/War"],a:"C",e:"Ransomware is overwhelmingly motivated by financial gain — attackers encrypt data and demand payment. Organized crime groups operate ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) businesses. While some nation-states use destructive ransomware (NotPetya) for disruption, the dominant motivation in most ransomware attacks is money."}, {s:false,q:"Which characteristic BEST distinguishes an Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) from a typical cybercriminal attack?",o:["A. APTs always demand ransom payment","B. APTs use only publicly available tools","C. APTs conduct long-term, stealthy, targeted operations often with nation-state backing","D. APTs focus exclusively on financial institutions"],a:"C",e:"An APT (Advanced Persistent Threat) is characterized by: long-term presence (months/years), extreme stealth, specific high-value targets, and typically nation-state backing. They conduct surgical operations for espionage or strategic disruption, not opportunistic financial crime."}, ]; const Q_2_2 = [ // Common threat vectors and attack surfaces {s:false,q:"Which threat vector involves attackers sending fraudulent communications via electronic mail to trick users into revealing credentials?",o:["A. Vishing","B. Smishing","C. Phishing","D. Pharming"],a:"C",e:"Phishing uses email as the attack vector — fraudulent messages designed to trick recipients into clicking malicious links, opening malicious attachments, or providing credentials. Email remains the most common initial attack vector for ransomware and data breaches."}, {s:false,q:"An attacker calls employees pretending to be the IT helpdesk to obtain their login credentials. This is an example of:",o:["A. Phishing","B. Vishing","C. Smishing","D. Spear Phishing"],a:"B",e:"Vishing (Voice Phishing) uses telephone calls as the attack vector. Attackers impersonate IT support, banks, or government agencies to socially engineer targets. The telephone medium creates urgency and leverages authority cues that email lacks."}, {s:false,q:"A user receives a text message claiming their bank account has been locked and provides a link to verify their identity. What type of attack is this?",o:["A. Vishing","B. Phishing","C. Smishing","D. Watering Hole"],a:"C",e:"Smishing (SMS Phishing) uses text messages (SMS) as the attack vector. The link in the SMS leads to a phishing page. Mobile users are often more vulnerable because mobile browsers hide full URLs and users expect action-required texts from service providers."}, {s:false,q:"An attacker compromises a legitimate website frequented by security researchers, knowing that visitors will automatically be infected. This technique is called:",o:["A. Phishing","B. Typosquatting","C. Watering Hole Attack","D. Business Email Compromise"],a:"C",e:"A watering hole attack compromises websites that the intended targets frequently visit. The attacker 'poisons the watering hole' — waiting for victims to come to the compromised site rather than going to them. This is effective against well-defended targets who are trained to avoid phishing."}, {s:true,sc:"A threat intelligence team discovers that attackers registered 'microsofft.com' and 'paypa1.com' and are using these to conduct phishing campaigns, counting on users to not notice the subtle differences from legitimate domains.",q:"What attack technique is being used?",o:["A. DNS Poisoning","B. Typosquatting","C. Domain Hijacking","D. Watering Hole"],a:"B",e:"Typosquatting (URL hijacking) registers domain names that are common misspellings or visual lookalikes of legitimate domains (microsofft, paypa1). Users who mistype the URL or don't notice the subtle difference are redirected to malicious sites. This exploits human inattention."}, {s:false,q:"Which attack vector involves exploiting vulnerabilities in the hardware, software, or services provided by third-party vendors to gain access to target organizations?",o:["A. Email-based phishing","B. Supply Chain Attack","C. Removable Device Attack","D. Watering Hole"],a:"B",e:"A supply chain attack compromises a target by attacking their suppliers or service providers. By compromising a trusted vendor's product or update mechanism, attackers gain access to all downstream customers. SolarWinds and Kaseya are famous examples."}, {s:false,q:"An employee plugs in a USB drive found in the parking lot, unknowingly installing malware. This attack uses which threat vector?",o:["A. Email-based","B. Voice Call","C. Removable Device","D. Image-based"],a:"C",e:"The removable device attack vector uses physical media (USB drives, external hard drives) to deliver malware. Attackers deliberately leave infected USB drives where targets will find them (baiting). Air-gapped systems can be compromised through removable media — this is how Stuxnet was delivered."}, {s:false,q:"Which term describes an attack where an attacker sends a fraudulent email appearing to come from a company executive, instructing the finance team to wire transfer funds?",o:["A. Watering Hole","B. Smishing","C. Business Email Compromise (BEC)","D. Typosquatting"],a:"C",e:"Business Email Compromise (BEC) is a sophisticated attack where fraudsters impersonate executives or trusted parties to trick employees into making wire transfers or revealing sensitive information. BEC attacks cause billions in losses annually and often involve email spoofing or account compromise."}, {s:false,q:"An attacker finds that an organization's IoT cameras are still using factory default credentials. Which attack surface does this represent?",o:["A. Supply Chain","B. Default Credentials","C. Unsecure Wireless","D. Open Service Ports"],a:"B",e:"Default credentials are a major attack surface — many devices ship with well-known default usernames and passwords that are publicly documented. Attackers systematically scan the internet for devices with default credentials. Organizations must change all defaults during device provisioning."}, {s:false,q:"A managed service provider (MSP) is compromised, giving attackers simultaneous access to hundreds of the MSP's client networks. Which threat vector does this represent?",o:["A. Email Phishing","B. Removable Media","C. Supply Chain via MSP","D. Open Service Ports"],a:"C",e:"MSPs represent a high-value supply chain attack vector. By compromising an MSP, attackers gain access to all their clients through the trusted management channels the MSP uses. This is supply chain compromise through managed service providers — a growing threat."}, {s:true,sc:"Security researchers detect an increase in attacks where users' browsers are infected simply by visiting legitimate but compromised news websites, without clicking anything. The exploit targets an unpatched browser vulnerability.",q:"What attack vector and technique is being used?",o:["A. Phishing via email link","B. Drive-by download via watering hole attack","C. Smishing via SMS link","D. Business Email Compromise"],a:"B",e:"This is a drive-by download delivered via a watering hole attack. The compromised legitimate website automatically exploits browser vulnerabilities to install malware without any user interaction beyond visiting the page. This is why browser patching is critical."}, {s:false,q:"Which attack technique involves creating a false sense of legitimacy by impersonating a brand in communications to steal credentials?",o:["A. Pretexting","B. Brand Impersonation","C. Vishing","D. Watering Hole"],a:"B",e:"Brand impersonation involves copying a legitimate brand's visual identity (logos, colors, email format) in malicious communications to appear trustworthy. Users are more likely to click links or provide credentials when communications appear to come from brands they trust."}, {s:false,q:"An attacker creates a fake scenario claiming to be a government agency auditor to convince an employee to provide network access credentials. This is an example of:",o:["A. Phishing","B. Pretexting","C. Watering Hole","D. Typosquatting"],a:"B",e:"Pretexting involves creating a fabricated scenario (pretext) to manipulate victims — the attacker invents a convincing backstory to justify their request. Pretexting is broader than phishing; it's used in vishing calls, in-person social engineering, and any situation where a false identity/scenario is created."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following represents an 'image-based' threat vector?",o:["A. An email with a malicious PDF attachment","B. A malicious executable disguised as an image file, or steganography hiding malware in image data","C. A fake login page that looks like a real website","D. A USB drive with autorun malware"],a:"B",e:"Image-based attacks use image files as the attack medium — either malicious executables disguised as images, or steganography embedding malicious code/data within image files. Some image parsing vulnerabilities have allowed code execution simply from viewing a specially crafted image."}, {s:false,q:"An organization uses a client-based vulnerability scanner agent installed on all endpoints. What is the PRIMARY advantage over an agentless scan?",o:["A. Agentless scans are more accurate for internal vulnerabilities","B. Agent-based scans can access local configuration data and credentials not visible from the network","C. Agentless scans consume fewer resources","D. Agent-based scans don't require network access"],a:"B",e:"Agent-based vulnerability scans run locally on the endpoint with full access to installed software, configurations, and local security settings. They find vulnerabilities that aren't visible from a network scan. Agentless scans are easier to deploy but have limited visibility into internal host state."}, {s:true,sc:"An organization's security team discovers that their legacy ERP system has not received a vendor security patch in over two years because the vendor discontinued support for that version.",q:"What vulnerability type does this represent?",o:["A. Zero-Day Vulnerability","B. Misconfiguration","C. Unsupported System/End-of-Life Software","D. Supply Chain"],a:"C",e:"Unsupported/end-of-life software no longer receives security patches, leaving known vulnerabilities permanently unaddressed. This is a systemic vulnerability. Options: upgrade (if possible), implement compensating controls (network isolation, WAF, enhanced monitoring), or decommission. This is a major compliance and risk concern."}, {s:false,q:"Open service ports on internet-facing systems represent which type of attack surface?",o:["A. Social Engineering","B. Supply Chain","C. Network Attack Surface — exposed services","D. Physical Attack Surface"],a:"C",e:"Open service ports expose network services to attack. Each open port is a potential entry point — attackers scan for open ports and probe services for vulnerabilities. The principle of minimizing attack surface means closing unnecessary ports and services (hardening)."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following BEST describes a 'misinformation/disinformation' attack vector?",o:["A. Sending fake emails to collect credentials","B. Spreading false information to manipulate beliefs, behavior, or undermine trust","C. Intercepting network traffic to alter data","D. Exploiting weak passwords through brute force"],a:"B",e:"Misinformation/disinformation spreads false information (intentionally in disinformation's case) to manipulate targets — causing panic, undermining trust, influencing elections, or misdirecting incident response. Social media amplifies these attacks. Security teams must consider information operations as a threat vector."}, {s:false,q:"An attacker sends SMS messages claiming a user has won a prize, with a link to claim it that installs spyware. What threat vector and technique is this?",o:["A. Email Phishing — Business Email Compromise","B. SMS (Smishing) — Mobile Social Engineering","C. Voice Call — Vishing","D. Removable Media — Baiting"],a:"B",e:"This is smishing (SMS phishing) combined with baiting (prize offer). The SMS message is the threat vector; the fake prize is the social engineering lure. Mobile devices are increasingly targeted because users are often less cautious on mobile than on desktop."}, {s:true,sc:"A threat actor registers the domain 'supportmicrosoft.com' and populates it with Microsoft-branded content. They then purchase Google ads so it appears at the top of search results for 'Microsoft support'.",q:"What threat technique does this BEST represent?",o:["A. Watering Hole Attack","B. DNS Poisoning","C. Typosquatting / Brand Impersonation via malvertising","D. Business Email Compromise"],a:"C",e:"This combines brand impersonation (fake Microsoft-branded site) with malvertising (purchasing ads to appear legitimate in search results). While 'supportmicrosoft.com' isn't technically a typosquat, the intent — tricking users into believing they're contacting legitimate Microsoft support — is the same."}, {s:false,q:"Which attack vector does BYOD (Bring Your Own Device) MOST directly increase?",o:["A. Supply Chain Risk","B. Unsecured personal devices connecting to corporate networks — expanding the attack surface","C. Email-based phishing","D. Physical security risk"],a:"B",e:"BYOD expands the attack surface by introducing personal devices (often with lower security standards, unmanaged software, personal apps) onto corporate networks. These devices may not meet corporate security policies, have vulnerable apps, or be shared with family members — each representing a potential compromise vector."}, {s:false,q:"What does 'impersonation' mean as a social engineering attack vector?",o:["A. Intercepting and reading network communications","B. Pretending to be a trusted individual or authority to manipulate targets","C. Sending mass phishing emails","D. Exploiting web application vulnerabilities"],a:"B",e:"Impersonation involves pretending to be someone trusted — a co-worker, IT support, executive, vendor, or authority figure — to manipulate the target into complying with requests. This can be done via email, phone, in-person, or messaging platforms."}, {s:false,q:"Which Bluetooth-based attack steals data (contacts, messages) from a device without the owner's knowledge?",o:["A. Bluejacking","B. Bluesnarfing","C. Bluebugging","D. Evil Twin"],a:"B",e:"Bluesnarfing exploits Bluetooth vulnerabilities to unauthorized access and steal data from a device — contacts, messages, calendar entries. Unlike Bluejacking (sending unsolicited messages), Bluesnarfing actually exfiltrates data. Bluebugging takes full device control. Keep Bluetooth off when not needed."}, {s:false,q:"A software vendor's update server is compromised, and malicious code is inserted into a legitimate software update distributed to all customers. Which attack vector does this represent?",o:["A. Email Phishing","B. Zero-Day Exploit","C. Software Supply Chain via malicious update","D. Removable Device"],a:"C",e:"This is a software supply chain attack via malicious update — compromising the update distribution mechanism to deliver malware to all users who install the 'legitimate' update. Trust in the update process is weaponized. Examples: SolarWinds Orion, Asus Live Update, CCleaner backdoor."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following BEST describes the concept of 'attack surface'?",o:["A. The physical area within which an attacker can operate","B. The sum total of all points where an unauthorized user could try to enter or extract data from a system","C. The number of known vulnerabilities in a system","D. The geographic area affected by a cyberattack"],a:"B",e:"The attack surface is the complete set of all entry points and pathways that an attacker could potentially exploit — open ports, web interfaces, APIs, physical access points, users, vendors, etc. Reducing the attack surface (closing unused ports, removing unnecessary software, limiting access) is a fundamental security principle."}, ]; const Q_2_3 = [ // Explain various types of vulnerabilities {s:false,q:"Which application vulnerability occurs when an attacker inserts malicious SQL commands into a web form field to manipulate the backend database?",o:["A. Cross-Site Scripting","B. Buffer Overflow","C. SQL Injection","D. Race Condition"],a:"C",e:"SQL Injection (SQLi) occurs when user input containing SQL syntax is executed by the database. An attacker can bypass login, dump all data, modify records, or execute system commands. Prevention: parameterized queries/prepared statements, input validation, stored procedures."}, {s:false,q:"Which vulnerability type occurs when an application writes more data to a memory buffer than it can hold, potentially allowing attackers to execute arbitrary code?",o:["A. SQL Injection","B. Buffer Overflow","C. Race Condition","D. Cross-Site Scripting"],a:"B",e:"A buffer overflow occurs when more data is written to a memory buffer than allocated space. The excess overwrites adjacent memory, potentially corrupting data or allowing attackers to inject and execute malicious code. Mitigations: input validation, safe programming languages, ASLR, DEP/NX."}, {s:false,q:"An attacker injects malicious JavaScript into a web page that executes in other users' browsers when they visit the page. This is:",o:["A. SQL Injection","B. Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)","C. CSRF","D. Directory Traversal"],a:"B",e:"Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) injects malicious client-side scripts (usually JavaScript) into web pages viewed by other users. The malicious script executes in the victim's browser with the trust of the legitimate site — can steal cookies, redirect users, or perform actions on their behalf."}, {s:false,q:"A zero-day vulnerability is BEST described as:",o:["A. A vulnerability that has been known for exactly zero days","B. A vulnerability that is unknown to the vendor or for which no patch has been released","C. A vulnerability that only affects systems that have been operational for less than a day","D. A vulnerability with a CVSS score of zero"],a:"B",e:"A zero-day vulnerability is one the vendor has zero days to prepare a fix — it's unknown to them or exploited before a patch is available. These are extremely dangerous because standard patch management cannot address them. Defenders must rely on behavior-based detection and compensating controls."}, {s:true,sc:"A banking application checks whether a user has sufficient funds before approving a transfer. However, an attacker has found that if they submit two simultaneous transfer requests faster than the system can process them, both transfers are approved even when only one would normally be allowed.",q:"What type of vulnerability is being exploited?",o:["A. SQL Injection","B. Buffer Overflow","C. Race Condition (Time-of-Check/Time-of-Use)","D. Insecure Direct Object Reference"],a:"C",e:"This is a race condition vulnerability (TOC/TOU). The check (balance sufficient?) and the use (deduct funds) occur at different times, and an attacker exploits the window between them. By submitting concurrent requests, both pass the check before either deduction occurs. This is the classic double-spend attack."}, {s:false,q:"A software update is released by a vendor to fix security issues. An attacker intercepts this update and replaces it with malware before the user downloads it. This represents which vulnerability type?",o:["A. Zero-Day","B. Malicious Update","C. Memory Injection","D. VM Escape"],a:"B",e:"A malicious update attack tampers with the software update process — either by compromising the distribution server, intercepting downloads, or compromising the build system. This weaponizes the trust users place in vendor-provided updates. Code signing and HTTPS help mitigate this."}, {s:false,q:"Which virtualization vulnerability allows malicious code in a guest virtual machine to break out and access the host system or other VMs?",o:["A. Resource Reuse","B. VM Escape","C. Container Breakout","D. Hypervisor Overflow"],a:"B",e:"VM Escape is a critical virtualization vulnerability where malicious code in a guest VM exploits hypervisor vulnerabilities to 'escape' the isolation boundary and access the host OS or other VMs. This completely breaks the security isolation that virtualization provides."}, {s:false,q:"A smartphone user installs an unofficial app from a website outside the official app store, bypassing the manufacturer's vetting process. This mobile vulnerability is called:",o:["A. Jailbreaking","B. Side Loading","C. Rooting","D. Firmware Flashing"],a:"B",e:"Side loading installs apps from sources outside the official app store (bypassing Apple App Store or Google Play Store security vetting). This introduces risk from unverified apps that may contain malware, request excessive permissions, or lack security updates."}, {s:false,q:"A user modifies their smartphone's OS to remove manufacturer restrictions and gain root access to all system functions. This vulnerability is called:",o:["A. Side Loading","B. Jailbreaking/Rooting","C. Firmware Attack","D. End-of-Life Exploitation"],a:"B",e:"Jailbreaking (iOS) or Rooting (Android) removes manufacturer security restrictions, granting full root/admin access to the OS. While providing more control, it eliminates the security model the device was built on — disabling secure boot, allowing any app installation, and voiding MDM protections."}, {s:false,q:"Which vulnerability category specifically refers to weaknesses introduced through hardware or software components acquired from vendors or manufacturers?",o:["A. Cloud-Specific","B. Supply Chain","C. Cryptographic","D. Operating System-Based"],a:"B",e:"Supply chain vulnerabilities exist in the hardware, software, or services provided by third-party vendors. A compromised chip manufacturer, software library, or hardware component introduces vulnerabilities that persist throughout the supply chain. These are extremely difficult to detect and remediate."}, {s:true,sc:"A cloud storage service has a misconfiguration where data stored in one customer's bucket can be accessed by authenticated users of other tenants on the same shared infrastructure.",q:"What type of cloud vulnerability is this?",o:["A. VM Escape","B. Resource Reuse / Tenant Isolation Failure","C. Zero-Day in Hypervisor","D. Malicious Update"],a:"B",e:"This is a resource reuse / tenant isolation failure — a cloud-specific vulnerability where shared infrastructure fails to properly isolate one customer's data from another. This could be through misconfigured storage permissions, hypervisor resource reuse not clearing sensitive data, or broken multi-tenant isolation."}, {s:false,q:"Which vulnerability type is caused by hardware components reaching end-of-life, meaning the manufacturer no longer provides firmware or security updates?",o:["A. Zero-Day","B. Race Condition","C. End-of-Life / Legacy Hardware","D. Misconfiguration"],a:"C",e:"End-of-life hardware no longer receives firmware updates or security patches. Known vulnerabilities in the hardware or its firmware remain permanently unaddressed. Organizations must either replace end-of-life hardware, implement compensating controls, or accept the risk."}, {s:false,q:"A web application exposes direct references to database records in URLs (e.g., /user?id=1234). An attacker changes the ID to access other users' data. This is:",o:["A. SQL Injection","B. Buffer Overflow","C. Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)","D. XSS"],a:"C",e:"IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) occurs when an application uses a user-controllable value (like a record ID in a URL) to access objects without proper authorization checks. An attacker can enumerate or guess IDs to access unauthorized records. Fix: server-side authorization checks on every access."}, {s:false,q:"Cryptographic vulnerabilities include which of the following?",o:["A. Buffer overflows in web applications","B. Use of weak/deprecated algorithms like MD5 or DES for sensitive data","C. SQL injection in database queries","D. Race conditions in transaction processing"],a:"B",e:"Cryptographic vulnerabilities include using weak/deprecated algorithms (MD5, SHA-1, DES), short key lengths, improper random number generation, hard-coded keys, and protocol downgrade attacks. These undermine the mathematical security guarantees that cryptography provides."}, {s:false,q:"A server is running with default credentials, unnecessary services enabled, and firewall rules left open from initial testing. This represents which vulnerability type?",o:["A. Zero-Day","B. Misconfiguration","C. Memory Injection","D. Supply Chain"],a:"B",e:"Misconfiguration is the most common vulnerability type — default credentials, unnecessary services, overly permissive access controls, insecure default settings. These are not software bugs but configuration choices that create security gaps. Hardening, configuration management, and CIS benchmarks address misconfigurations."}, {s:true,sc:"A developer uses a popular open-source logging library in their application. A critical zero-day vulnerability is discovered in that library that allows remote code execution. The developer's application is now vulnerable even though their own code has no bugs.",q:"What type of vulnerability does this represent?",o:["A. Operating System Vulnerability","B. Third-Party Software / Supply Chain Dependency Vulnerability","C. Application Logic Flaw","D. Cryptographic Weakness"],a:"B",e:"This represents a third-party software / supply chain dependency vulnerability (similar to Log4Shell). Modern applications depend on many libraries; a vulnerability in any dependency affects all applications using it. Software composition analysis (SCA) tools inventory and monitor dependencies for known vulnerabilities."}, {s:false,q:"Which operating system vulnerability type occurs when an attacker injects malicious code into a running process's memory space?",o:["A. Race Condition","B. Buffer Overflow","C. Memory Injection","D. Directory Traversal"],a:"C",e:"Memory injection injects malicious code or data directly into the memory of a running process — exploiting process injection techniques (DLL injection, reflective DLL loading, process hollowing). This allows malware to execute within the context of a legitimate process, evading detection."}, {s:false,q:"A web application allows users to input filenames that are passed directly to file system operations. An attacker inputs '../../etc/passwd' to access system files outside the web directory. This is:",o:["A. SQL Injection","B. Command Injection","C. Directory Traversal / Path Traversal","D. Buffer Overflow"],a:"C",e:"Directory traversal (path traversal) exploits insufficient input validation to navigate outside the intended directory using '../' sequences. Attackers can read, write, or execute files outside the web application's directory structure. Prevention: validate/sanitize input, use chroot jails, avoid passing user input to file operations."}, {s:false,q:"Cloud-specific vulnerabilities include which of the following?",o:["A. Buffer overflows in desktop applications","B. Firmware vulnerabilities in network switches","C. Misconfigured storage buckets, identity federation issues, and improper IAM permissions","D. Physical access to server hardware"],a:"C",e:"Cloud-specific vulnerabilities include: misconfigured storage (S3 buckets open to public), overly permissive IAM policies, tenant isolation failures, insecure APIs, misconfigured security groups, and identity federation misconfigurations. The shared responsibility model makes it critical to understand what the customer is responsible for securing."}, {s:false,q:"Which type of vulnerability specifically targets the firmware of network devices, IoT devices, or hardware components?",o:["A. SQL Injection","B. Cross-Site Scripting","C. Firmware Vulnerability","D. Race Condition"],a:"C",e:"Firmware vulnerabilities affect the low-level software embedded in hardware devices — routers, switches, IoT devices, printers. Firmware runs at a layer below the OS, giving attackers deep persistence. Updates are infrequent, and many devices never receive patches. These are particularly dangerous in IoT environments."}, {s:true,sc:"A penetration tester discovers that a Windows 2008 server is still in production because decommissioning it would require significant application rework. The server has not received security updates in 3 years.",q:"What category and specific vulnerability type does this represent?",o:["A. Zero-Day — Application Layer","B. Operating System Legacy/End-of-Life Vulnerability","C. Supply Chain — Third-Party Vendor","D. Cryptographic Weakness"],a:"B",e:"Windows 2008 reached end-of-life in January 2020. Running it represents an OS-based legacy/end-of-life vulnerability — numerous known, unpatched vulnerabilities exist with no vendor patches forthcoming. This is a risk acceptance or compensating control scenario (isolation, enhanced monitoring, WAF)."}, {s:false,q:"A CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) score is used to:",o:["A. Encrypt vulnerability data for secure sharing","B. Quantify the severity of vulnerabilities to help prioritize patching","C. Identify the specific attacker who exploited a vulnerability","D. Automatically patch vulnerable systems"],a:"B",e:"CVSS provides a standardized numerical score (0-10) for vulnerability severity based on exploitability, impact, and scope. Organizations use CVSS scores to prioritize patching — Critical (9.0-10.0) and High (7.0-8.9) scored vulnerabilities require faster remediation than Low/Medium ones."}, {s:false,q:"Which vulnerability type is created when code runs at an unexpected time, allowing attackers to manipulate the sequence of operations?",o:["A. Buffer Overflow","B. SQL Injection","C. Race Condition","D. Directory Traversal"],a:"C",e:"A race condition exploits timing — when the outcome depends on the sequence/timing of events, and an attacker can influence that timing. TOC/TOU (Time-of-Check/Time-of-Use) is the most common form. Concurrent programming without proper locking creates race conditions."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following is an example of an operating system-based vulnerability?",o:["A. An unpatched SQL Server with a known privilege escalation bug in the database engine","B. An unpatched Windows kernel vulnerability allowing local privilege escalation","C. A misconfigured S3 bucket in AWS","D. A vendor's network switch using default passwords"],a:"B",e:"An unpatched Windows kernel vulnerability allowing local privilege escalation is an OS-based vulnerability — it exists in the operating system kernel itself. Examples: EternalBlue (MS17-010), PrintNightmare, Dirty COW. These are distinct from application-layer vulnerabilities."}, {s:false,q:"What does CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) provide?",o:["A. Automatic patches for known vulnerabilities","B. A standardized identifier and description for publicly known cybersecurity vulnerabilities","C. A scoring system for vulnerability severity","D. A list of approved security tools for patching"],a:"B",e:"CVE provides standardized identifiers (e.g., CVE-2021-44228 for Log4Shell) for publicly known vulnerabilities. This common naming system allows security tools, databases, and teams worldwide to speak the same language when referencing specific vulnerabilities. CVSS provides the scoring; CVE provides the identification."}, ]; const Q_2_4 = [ // Analyze indicators of malicious activity {s:false,q:"Which type of malware encrypts a victim's files and demands cryptocurrency payment to restore access?",o:["A. Rootkit","B. Worm","C. Ransomware","D. Spyware"],a:"C",e:"Ransomware encrypts files or systems and demands payment (typically cryptocurrency) for the decryption key. Modern ransomware groups also exfiltrate data before encrypting (double extortion) to increase leverage. Offline backups are the primary defense."}, {s:false,q:"A user's computer is secretly monitoring their keystrokes and sending the data to a remote attacker. This malware is BEST classified as:",o:["A. Ransomware","B. Worm","C. Rootkit","D. Keylogger"],a:"D",e:"A keylogger secretly records keystrokes — capturing passwords, credit card numbers, messages, and other sensitive typed input. Keyloggers can be software-based (malware) or hardware-based (physical device attached between keyboard and computer). Captured data is sent to the attacker."}, {s:false,q:"Which type of malware self-replicates and spreads across networks without requiring user interaction or a host file?",o:["A. Trojan","B. Worm","C. Virus","D. Spyware"],a:"B",e:"A worm self-replicates and propagates across networks autonomously — no host file needed, no user interaction required. Worms exploit network vulnerabilities or shared resources. Famous examples: WannaCry, NotPetya, Code Red. They can rapidly infect thousands of systems."}, {s:false,q:"A network administrator notices a system making large numbers of DNS requests to unusual domains and sending data out on port 443 late at night. These are indicators of which type of malicious activity?",o:["A. DDoS attack in progress","B. Command and Control (C2) communication / data exfiltration","C. Network scanning","D. Password spraying"],a:"B",e:"Unusual DNS requests (often to algorithmically generated domains), late-night activity, and outbound encrypted traffic on unexpected port patterns are indicators of Command and Control (C2) communication — the malware checking in with its operators — and potential data exfiltration."}, {s:true,sc:"A SOC analyst reviews logs and finds that a user account had successful logins from both New York and Tokyo within 20 minutes of each other. The user is not known to travel internationally.",q:"Which indicator of malicious activity does this represent?",o:["A. Account Lockout","B. Impossible Travel","C. Resource Consumption","D. Concurrent Session Usage"],a:"B",e:"Impossible travel is when logins occur from geographically distant locations within a timeframe that makes physical travel impossible. This strongly indicates credential compromise — two people (the legitimate user and an attacker) are using the same account simultaneously from different locations."}, {s:false,q:"A logic bomb is BEST described as:",o:["A. Malware that encrypts files and demands ransom","B. Malicious code that executes when a specific condition or trigger is met","C. A worm that spreads via network shares","D. Spyware that monitors user behavior"],a:"B",e:"A logic bomb is dormant malicious code that executes when a specific condition is met — a date/time trigger, a user account deletion, a file being accessed, or any programmable condition. Often planted by insiders, they can delete data, crash systems, or trigger ransomware."}, {s:false,q:"Which malware type hides itself deep in the OS and provides persistent, privileged remote access while avoiding detection?",o:["A. Ransomware","B. Spyware","C. Rootkit","D. Bloatware"],a:"C",e:"A rootkit provides persistent privileged access while hiding itself by modifying the OS kernel, system calls, or bootloader. It can hide files, processes, network connections, and registry keys from security tools. Detecting rootkits requires offline scanning or specialized anti-rootkit tools."}, {s:false,q:"During a DDoS attack, the attacker sends a small request to a third-party server that responds with a much larger response directed at the victim. What type of DDoS technique is this?",o:["A. Direct Volumetric Attack","B. Amplified/Reflected DDoS","C. Application Layer DDoS","D. Protocol Exploitation DDoS"],a:"B",e:"An amplified/reflected DDoS uses third-party servers (DNS resolvers, NTP servers, memcached) to amplify attack traffic. The attacker sends a small packet spoofing the victim's IP; the server responds with a much larger response to the victim. DNS amplification can achieve 50-70x amplification."}, {s:true,sc:"A security analyst notices that multiple user accounts have had five consecutive failed login attempts within seconds, followed by successful login on the sixth attempt — but only for accounts with commonly known passwords. The activity spans hundreds of accounts.",q:"Which attack type do these indicators suggest?",o:["A. Credential Stuffing","B. Brute Force on a single account","C. Password Spraying","D. Keylogging"],a:"C",e:"Password spraying — trying a small number of common passwords across many accounts, staying under lockout thresholds. The pattern: multiple accounts, few attempts per account, successful on common passwords. Credential stuffing uses previously breached username/password pairs; brute force hammers one account."}, {s:false,q:"Spyware MOST commonly compromises which CIA component?",o:["A. Availability","B. Integrity","C. Confidentiality","D. Non-Repudiation"],a:"C",e:"Spyware secretly monitors user activity, browsing, keystrokes, and communications — stealing private information. This is a Confidentiality violation. The user's private data is accessed without authorization. Examples: stalkerware, adware with data collection, keyloggers."}, {s:false,q:"An indicator of compromise (IoC) showing 'Out-of-Cycle Logging' means:",o:["A. Log files are stored in the wrong directory","B. Logging activity at unexpected times suggests malware activity or tampering","C. Logs are being archived too frequently","D. The logging system has run out of storage"],a:"B",e:"Out-of-cycle logging — system or application activity being logged at unusual times (e.g., 3am, weekends) when legitimate users aren't active — can indicate malware execution, attacker activity, or scheduled malicious tasks. Baseline normal activity and alert on deviations."}, {s:false,q:"A system normally processes 1,000 transactions per hour. During an attack, it receives 1,000,000 requests per second and becomes unresponsive. Which indicator of malicious activity is this?",o:["A. Account Lockout","B. Impossible Travel","C. Resource Consumption","D. Missing Logs"],a:"C",e:"Abnormal resource consumption — CPU, memory, network bandwidth, or storage being consumed far beyond baseline — is an indicator of DoS/DDoS attacks, cryptomining malware, or intensive scanning activity. Resource monitoring and thresholds should trigger alerts when consumption is anomalous."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of a Trojan horse malware?",o:["A. Disguises itself as legitimate software","B. Requires user execution to activate","C. Self-replicates across networks without user interaction","D. Can contain malicious payloads like RATs or keyloggers"],a:"C",e:"Trojans do NOT self-replicate autonomously — that's a worm's characteristic. Trojans: (A) disguise as legitimate software ✓, (B) require user execution ✓, (D) contain malicious payloads ✓. The key distinction: trojans spread through user deception, not automatic self-propagation."}, {s:true,sc:"An employee reports that their computer is running extremely slowly. Investigation reveals the CPU is at 100% utilization, there are outbound network connections to multiple IP addresses in various countries, and security software cannot be uninstalled.",q:"Which type of malware MOST likely describes this situation?",o:["A. Ransomware","B. Cryptomining Malware with rootkit characteristics","C. Logic Bomb","D. Bloatware"],a:"B",e:"Cryptomining malware (cryptojacker): 100% CPU (mining cryptocurrency) + outbound connections to mining pools (various IPs) + resistance to removal (rootkit-like persistence mechanisms). The combination of resource consumption and network indicators is a classic cryptomining pattern."}, {s:false,q:"'Concurrent Session Usage' as an indicator of compromise means:",o:["A. A single account is logged in from multiple locations simultaneously — suggesting credential compromise","B. Multiple accounts using the same password","C. A system running multiple processes simultaneously","D. Users sharing session tokens"],a:"A",e:"Concurrent session usage — the same user account authenticated from multiple locations simultaneously — is a strong indicator of compromised credentials. One user cannot physically be in two places at once. Modern identity systems can detect and alert on concurrent sessions from different IPs/locations."}, {s:false,q:"Which malware type is typically pre-installed on devices or bundled with legitimate software, consumes resources, and may collect telemetry without directly harming the system?",o:["A. Ransomware","B. Rootkit","C. Bloatware","D. Worm"],a:"C",e:"Bloatware is unwanted software pre-installed by manufacturers or bundled with other software. While not directly malicious, it consumes storage/RAM/CPU, may collect data, reduce performance, and sometimes contains security vulnerabilities. It can also serve as a vector for more serious malware."}, {s:false,q:"A DNS attack that poisons the resolver's cache to redirect all users querying a specific domain to an attacker's server is called:",o:["A. DNS Hijacking","B. DNS Amplification","C. DNS Cache Poisoning","D. NXDOMAIN Attack"],a:"C",e:"DNS Cache Poisoning corrupts a DNS resolver's cached records with false IP mappings, redirecting all users of that resolver to attacker-controlled servers. It affects all users of the poisoned resolver without them clicking anything. DNSSEC was created specifically to prevent DNS cache poisoning."}, {s:false,q:"Which indicator suggests that an attacker may have tampered with logging systems to hide their tracks?",o:["A. Out-of-cycle logging","B. Missing logs","C. Resource consumption","D. Account lockout"],a:"B",e:"Missing logs — gaps in normally continuous log data — indicate an attacker may have deleted or disabled logging to cover their tracks. Attackers often clear Windows Event Logs, delete bash history, or disable syslog. Centralized SIEM with real-time log forwarding makes log deletion less effective."}, {s:false,q:"A network attack where an attacker positions themselves between two communicating parties to intercept or alter communications is called:",o:["A. DDoS","B. DNS Poisoning","C. On-Path (Man-in-the-Middle) Attack","D. Credential Replay"],a:"C",e:"An on-path (MitM) attack positions the attacker between communicating parties — intercepting, reading, and potentially modifying traffic without detection. Techniques include ARP poisoning, DNS spoofing, SSL stripping, and rogue access points. TLS/HTTPS, certificate pinning, and MFA mitigate MitM."}, {s:true,sc:"An analyst reviews authentication logs and notices that a service account that normally authenticates once per day at 8am has made 847 authentication attempts across 200 different systems over the past 3 hours, all using valid credentials.",q:"Which indicator of malicious activity BEST describes this?",o:["A. Password Spraying","B. Account Lockout Pattern","C. Lateral Movement via Credential Replay","D. Bloatware Installation"],a:"C",e:"Lateral movement via credential replay — an attacker has stolen valid service account credentials and is using them to authenticate across the network, moving laterally to discover and compromise systems. The pattern: valid credentials, many systems, unusual time/frequency, is a key lateral movement indicator."}, {s:false,q:"'Blocked content' as an indicator of compromise refers to:",o:["A. A firewall successfully blocking all attacks","B. Security tools blocking suspicious or malicious content — indicating an attempted attack","C. Users being blocked from accessing the internet","D. Encrypted content that cannot be inspected"],a:"B",e:"Blocked content events in security logs (from firewalls, web proxies, AV, EDR) indicate that a threat was attempted. A spike in blocked content can indicate an active attack campaign targeting the organization, even if none have succeeded yet. These events are valuable early warning indicators."}, {s:false,q:"Which characteristic BEST distinguishes a computer worm from a computer virus?",o:["A. Worms encrypt data; viruses do not","B. Worms self-replicate without needing a host file; viruses require a host file to spread","C. Viruses target networks; worms target individual files","D. Worms demand ransom; viruses just cause damage"],a:"B",e:"A virus requires a host file — it attaches to legitimate files and spreads when infected files are shared. A worm is standalone and self-replicating — it spreads autonomously across networks by exploiting vulnerabilities without any host file or user action needed."}, {s:false,q:"Which type of network attack involves replaying captured authentication tokens or session data to impersonate a legitimate user?",o:["A. Password Spraying","B. Credential Replay Attack","C. Man-in-the-Middle","D. DNS Poisoning"],a:"B",e:"A credential replay attack captures valid authentication tokens, cookies, or credentials (often via network interception or malware) and replays them to authenticate as the victim. Pass-the-Hash and Pass-the-Ticket are Windows-specific replay attacks. HTTPS, token expiration, and MFA mitigate replay attacks."}, {s:false,q:"A published/documented indicator of compromise (IoC) refers to:",o:["A. IoCs that the organization has internally developed","B. Threat intelligence sharing platforms publishing known malware hashes, C2 IPs, and attack signatures","C. Compliance documentation for security incidents","D. Public disclosure of a company's breach"],a:"B",e:"Published/documented IoCs from threat intelligence sharing platforms (VirusTotal, MISP, STIX/TAXII feeds, ISACs) include known malware file hashes, C2 server IP addresses, malicious domains, and attack signatures. These allow organizations to proactively detect known threats."}, {s:false,q:"Account lockout as an indicator of malicious activity MOST directly suggests:",o:["A. A hardware failure preventing authentication","B. A brute force or password spraying attack triggering lockout thresholds","C. A user forgetting their password","D. An expired certificate blocking authentication"],a:"B",e:"Account lockout occurs when the maximum failed authentication attempts is reached. A sudden increase in account lockouts across multiple accounts indicates an active brute force or password spraying attack. A single lockout might just be a forgotten password; multiple simultaneous lockouts indicate an attack."}, ]; const Q_2_5 = [ // Mitigation techniques {s:false,q:"Which mitigation technique divides a network into isolated segments to limit the lateral movement of attackers and contain breaches?",o:["A. Patching","B. Segmentation","C. Encryption","D. Least Privilege"],a:"B",e:"Segmentation divides the network into isolated zones (VLANs, subnets, security zones) with controlled traffic flows between them. An attacker who compromises one segment cannot freely access others. This is fundamental to Zero Trust and defense in depth — containing the blast radius of any breach."}, {s:false,q:"An application allow list is BEST described as:",o:["A. A list of blocked malicious applications","B. A security control that only permits pre-approved applications to execute","C. A firewall rule allowing specific IP addresses","D. A list of approved security vendors"],a:"B",e:"An application allow list (formerly whitelist) is a preventive control that only permits explicitly approved applications to run. Any application not on the list is blocked. This is highly effective against malware because even unknown/new malware is blocked if it's not pre-approved."}, {s:false,q:"Which hardening technique disables all unnecessary network services and closes ports that are not required for a system's designated function?",o:["A. Patching","B. Encryption","C. Disabling unnecessary ports and protocols","D. Default password changes"],a:"C",e:"Disabling unnecessary ports and protocols reduces the attack surface. Every open port and running service is a potential entry point. Hardening involves disabling Telnet, FTP, unused web services, and closing all ports not required for the system's function — following the principle of minimal functionality."}, {s:true,sc:"After a series of ransomware attacks in the industry, a security team wants to limit what damage can occur if any single user's account is compromised. They implement a policy ensuring users can only access the specific systems and data required for their current tasks.",q:"Which mitigation technique are they implementing?",o:["A. Segmentation","B. Patching","C. Least Privilege","D. Decommissioning"],a:"C",e:"Least privilege limits users to the minimum access rights needed for their job function. If an account with least privilege is compromised, the attacker's access is limited to only what that account can reach — minimizing blast radius. This is a preventive mitigation directly reducing breach impact."}, {s:false,q:"Which mitigation technique involves applying vendor-released fixes to address known software vulnerabilities?",o:["A. Encryption","B. Segmentation","C. Patching","D. Monitoring"],a:"C",e:"Patching applies vendor-provided fixes for known vulnerabilities, closing security gaps. An effective patch management program includes: inventory of all assets, prioritization by CVSS score, testing in staging, and deployment within defined windows. Unpatched systems are the #1 source of exploitable vulnerabilities."}, {s:false,q:"A Host-Based Intrusion Prevention System (HIPS) BEST falls under which hardening technique category?",o:["A. Default password changes","B. Disabling ports/protocols","C. Installation of endpoint protection","D. Encryption"],a:"C",e:"HIPS is an endpoint protection technology installed on individual hosts. It monitors and blocks suspicious behavior at the system level — preventing exploitation attempts, malicious process execution, and unauthorized system modifications. It's part of the broader endpoint protection hardening category."}, {s:true,sc:"A security team discovers several servers in the DMZ that are running Windows Server 2003, an OS that has been end-of-life since 2015. These servers handle credit card processing and cannot be immediately replaced due to legacy application dependencies.",q:"Which combination of mitigation techniques would BEST address this risk?",o:["A. Patching and encryption only","B. Isolation/segmentation, enhanced monitoring, host-based firewall, and application allow listing as compensating controls","C. Decommissioning the servers immediately","D. Updating the application to modern OS — only long-term option"],a:"B",e:"When patching is impossible, compensating controls apply: Isolation (restrict network access), enhanced monitoring (detect attacks early), host-based firewall (restrict connections), application allow listing (prevent malware execution). These mitigate risk while a longer-term solution is developed."}, {s:false,q:"Encryption as a mitigation technique PRIMARILY addresses which security objective?",o:["A. Availability — keeping systems operational","B. Integrity — preventing unauthorized modification","C. Confidentiality — protecting data from unauthorized access/disclosure","D. Non-Repudiation — proving who performed actions"],a:"C",e:"Encryption primarily protects Confidentiality — ensuring data is only readable by authorized parties. Encrypted data is unreadable to unauthorized parties even if they physically possess it. Encryption at rest protects stored data; encryption in transit protects data being transmitted."}, {s:false,q:"Which mitigation technique permanently and securely removes systems, software, or data that are no longer needed, eliminating them as attack vectors?",o:["A. Patching","B. Isolation","C. Decommissioning","D. Configuration Enforcement"],a:"C",e:"Decommissioning eliminates the attack surface entirely by permanently retiring unneeded systems, applications, or accounts. An asset that doesn't exist cannot be compromised. Decommissioning includes proper data sanitization (wiping) before disposal and revoking all associated access credentials."}, {s:false,q:"Configuration enforcement ensures that systems maintain a secure state. Which tool MOST commonly implements configuration enforcement across an enterprise?",o:["A. SIEM","B. NAC","C. Group Policy / Configuration Management tools (e.g., Ansible, Chef)","D. IDS"],a:"C",e:"Configuration enforcement tools like Group Policy (Windows), Ansible, Chef, Puppet, or SCCM push and maintain consistent secure configurations across all systems. They detect configuration drift (changes from the baseline) and automatically remediate or alert. This prevents manual misconfigurations and maintains hardening standards."}, {s:false,q:"Host-based firewalls as a hardening technique provide which primary benefit over network firewalls?",o:["A. They are faster than network firewalls","B. They protect individual hosts from threats that have already bypassed the network perimeter — including east-west traffic","C. They encrypt all network traffic","D. They replace the need for network firewalls"],a:"B",e:"Host-based firewalls operate on individual endpoints and control traffic to/from that specific host — even within the internal network. This provides protection against east-west lateral movement that bypasses perimeter firewalls. Network firewalls protect the perimeter; host firewalls protect individual hosts."}, {s:true,sc:"A security administrator reviews a new web server and finds: default admin password still set, FTP service running (not needed), SNMP using default community string 'public', and test pages accessible from the internet.",q:"Which mitigation technique addresses ALL of these issues?",o:["A. Patching","B. Encryption","C. System Hardening — changing defaults, removing unnecessary services","D. Segmentation"],a:"C",e:"All four issues are hardening targets: default password changes (admin password, SNMP community string), disabling unnecessary ports/protocols (FTP), and removing unnecessary software/features (test pages). Hardening is the systematic reduction of attack surface by addressing configuration weaknesses."}, {s:false,q:"The principle of 'isolation' as a mitigation technique is BEST described as:",o:["A. Encrypting data so it cannot be read","B. Physically or logically separating a system from the rest of the environment to contain risk","C. Removing vulnerable software","D. Applying security patches"],a:"B",e:"Isolation places a system, network segment, or process in a contained environment where it has no or limited ability to affect or communicate with other systems. Used for: containing compromised systems (incident response), sandboxing suspicious files, air-gapping critical systems, and protecting legacy systems."}, {s:false,q:"Continuous monitoring as a mitigation technique PRIMARILY provides which security capability?",o:["A. Prevents all attacks from occurring","B. Provides ongoing visibility into system and network activity for early detection and response","C. Automatically patches vulnerabilities when discovered","D. Encrypts all data in transit"],a:"B",e:"Continuous monitoring provides ongoing visibility — log analysis, security alerts, anomaly detection, network traffic analysis. It doesn't prevent attacks (that's preventive controls) but provides early detection capability, enabling faster response before damage escalates. SIEM, UEBA, and EDR are monitoring tools."}, {s:false,q:"Access Control Lists (ACLs) as a mitigation technique PRIMARILY provide which benefit?",o:["A. Encrypt data as it flows through the network","B. Define and enforce what resources each user or system is permitted to access","C. Detect and alert on malicious activity","D. Prevent physical access to systems"],a:"B",e:"ACLs define who/what can access specific resources and what operations are permitted. They enforce the principle of least privilege at the resource level — specifying permitted traffic (network ACLs) or user permissions (file system ACLs). They are fundamental access control mechanisms."}, {s:true,sc:"An organization experiences a ransomware attack. The attacker encrypted servers, but the backup administrator notices that offline backup tapes from last week are unaffected. The team restores operations within 48 hours.",q:"Which mitigations contributed MOST to the successful recovery?",o:["A. IDS detection and firewall rules","B. Regular offline backups (corrective) and tested recovery procedures","C. Encryption of data at rest","D. Application allow listing"],a:"B",e:"Regular offline backups were the critical corrective control enabling recovery without paying ransom. Offline backups are immune to ransomware because they're not connected to the network. Tested recovery procedures ensured the team could execute the restore quickly. This is the primary anti-ransomware mitigation."}, {s:false,q:"Changing default passwords as a hardening technique addresses which vulnerability type?",o:["A. Zero-Day Vulnerabilities","B. Default Credential Vulnerabilities","C. Buffer Overflow Vulnerabilities","D. Race Condition Vulnerabilities"],a:"B",e:"Many devices and systems ship with well-known default credentials that attackers can look up publicly. Changing all defaults immediately upon deployment (first hardening step) closes this trivially exploitable attack vector. Mirai botnet, which compromised millions of IoT devices, exploited default credentials."}, {s:false,q:"Which mitigation technique removes software that is not required for a system's function, reducing the attack surface?",o:["A. Patching","B. Monitoring","C. Removal of unnecessary software","D. Encryption"],a:"C",e:"Removing unnecessary software reduces the attack surface — every installed application is a potential vulnerability source. Following the principle of minimal functionality means only installing software required for the system's designated role. This also applies to OS features, services, and components."}, {s:false,q:"Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) tools provide which primary mitigation capability?",o:["A. Network perimeter protection and traffic filtering","B. Continuous monitoring, threat detection, and response capabilities on individual endpoints","C. Data encryption at rest and in transit","D. User authentication and access control"],a:"B",e:"EDR solutions provide continuous monitoring of endpoint activity, behavioral threat detection (beyond signatures), incident investigation capabilities, and automated response actions. EDR can detect fileless malware, living-off-the-land attacks, and behavioral anomalies that traditional AV misses."}, {s:false,q:"Which term describes the practice of maintaining a current, hardened configuration standard that all systems must comply with?",o:["A. Patch Management","B. Security Baseline","C. Access Control List","D. Security Audit"],a:"B",e:"A security baseline (or secure baseline configuration) defines the minimum security settings that all systems of a given type must implement. Baselines are derived from frameworks like CIS Benchmarks or DISA STIGs. Configuration management tools enforce and monitor compliance with the baseline."}, {s:true,sc:"After a penetration test reveals that an attacker could pivot from the web tier to the database tier by exploiting excessive network permissions, the security team implements firewall rules between application tiers allowing only specific ports and protocols.",q:"Which mitigation technique has been applied?",o:["A. Least Privilege","B. Patching","C. Segmentation with ACLs enforcing need-to-communicate","D. Decommissioning"],a:"C",e:"Segmentation with ACLs: Network segmentation separates the web and database tiers into different security zones; ACLs enforce that only specific required traffic (e.g., TCP 1433 from app servers to DB) is permitted. This limits lateral movement even if the web tier is compromised."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following BEST describes why compensating controls are necessary in security?",o:["A. They are always more effective than primary controls","B. They address risk when the preferred/primary security control cannot be implemented","C. They are the least expensive security controls","D. They replace the need for monitoring"],a:"B",e:"Compensating controls are implemented when the primary control is not feasible — due to technical limitations, business requirements, or cost. They provide an alternative means of risk reduction. PCI-DSS explicitly allows compensating controls when standard requirements can't be met, provided they offer equivalent protection."}, {s:false,q:"Isolation is used in incident response primarily to:",o:["A. Permanently destroy the compromised system","B. Contain the incident by preventing the compromised system from communicating with other systems","C. Encrypt the compromised system's data","D. Back up the compromised system's data"],a:"B",e:"During incident response, isolation/containment prevents the compromised system from spreading malware, communicating with C2 servers, or affecting other systems — while preserving it for forensic investigation. This is the Containment phase of incident response. The system is disconnected from the network but preserved."}, {s:false,q:"Which mitigation technique specifically addresses the risk of attackers targeting web applications with injection attacks and protocol exploits?",o:["A. HIPS","B. Web Application Firewall (WAF)","C. Network Firewall","D. VPN"],a:"B",e:"A WAF (Web Application Firewall) specifically protects web applications from OWASP Top 10 attacks — SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, and protocol-level attacks. Unlike network firewalls (Layer 3/4), WAFs operate at Layer 7, understanding HTTP/HTTPS to distinguish legitimate requests from attacks."}, {s:false,q:"Application allow listing as a mitigation technique differs from application deny listing (blacklisting) in which key way?",o:["A. Allow listing only blocks known malware signatures","B. Allow listing only permits pre-approved applications — unknown/new malware is blocked by default; deny listing only blocks known threats","C. Deny listing is always more effective than allow listing","D. They provide equivalent security"],a:"B",e:"Allow listing (whitelist): only explicitly approved applications can run — anything unknown is blocked. This provides stronger protection because new/unknown malware is blocked by default. Deny listing (blacklist): blocks known bad applications but allows everything else — zero-day malware not yet on the list will run. Allow listing is more restrictive but far more effective against unknown threats."}, ]; // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ // DOMAIN 3.0 — SECURITY ARCHITECTURE // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ const Q_3_1 = [ // Security implications of architecture models {s:false,q:"In the cloud shared responsibility model, which security tasks are ALWAYS the customer's responsibility regardless of the service model (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS)?",o:["A. Patching the hypervisor","B. Managing physical data center security","C. Protecting their own data and managing user access/identity","D. Securing the cloud provider's network infrastructure"],a:"C",e:"In all cloud service models, customers are always responsible for their own data and identity/access management. The shared responsibility line shifts for infrastructure, OS, and applications depending on IaaS/PaaS/SaaS, but data and identity remain the customer's responsibility in every model."}, {s:false,q:"Which cloud architecture consideration refers to the potential legal and regulatory requirements for data to remain within specific geographic boundaries?",o:["A. Hybrid Cloud","B. Third-Party Vendors","C. Data Sovereignty","D. Responsibility Matrix"],a:"C",e:"Data sovereignty is the concept that data is subject to the laws and governance structures of the nation where it is stored or processed. Organizations must understand where cloud providers store data and ensure compliance with regulations like GDPR (EU), POPIA (South Africa), or specific industry regulations."}, {s:false,q:"Infrastructure as Code (IaC) raises which PRIMARY security concern compared to traditional infrastructure management?",o:["A. IaC makes systems more difficult to patch","B. A single misconfigured template can instantiate thousands of vulnerable resources simultaneously","C. IaC eliminates the ability to audit configuration changes","D. IaC requires physical access to servers"],a:"B",e:"IaC security concern: a single vulnerable or misconfigured template, when executed at scale, can provision thousands of insecure resources simultaneously. This is much faster than traditional misconfiguration propagation. IaC requires security scanning of templates (SAST for IaC) before deployment."}, {s:true,sc:"An organization is designing a system where medical IoT devices (patient monitors, infusion pumps) connect to the hospital network.
