DAST finds runtime vulnerabilities that static analysis misses — authentication flaws, session management issues, server configuration problems. Fuzzing is an automated form of dynamic testing."}, {s:false,q:"Bluetooth security best practice to reduce attack surface includes:",o:["A. Always leaving Bluetooth discoverable for ease of use","B. Disabling Bluetooth when not in use and using Bluetooth pairing in non-discoverable mode","C. Using WEP encryption for Bluetooth","D. Only using Bluetooth on mobile devices"],a:"B",e:"Bluetooth security: disable when not in use (eliminates attack surface), use non-discoverable mode (device not visible to unauthorized scanners), require PIN/passkey for pairing, keep firmware updated, use Bluetooth 5.x with stronger security features. Bluejacking, Bluesnarfing, and Bluebugging exploit enabled/discoverable Bluetooth."}, {s:false,q:"Package monitoring for application security involves:",o:["A. Monitoring physical package deliveries to the office","B. Continuously scanning software dependencies and libraries for known vulnerabilities (CVEs) as they are discovered","C. Monitoring application installation packages on endpoints","D. Tracking software license packages"],a:"B",e:"Package monitoring (Software Composition Analysis) continuously monitors third-party libraries and dependencies in applications for newly discovered CVEs. A library that was secure last month may have a new critical vulnerability today. Tools like Dependabot, Snyk, or Black Duck provide continuous package vulnerability monitoring."}, {s:false,q:"Site surveys for wireless deployment are conducted BEFORE installation to:",o:["A. Test network speeds after installation","B. Identify physical obstacles, interference sources, coverage requirements, and optimal AP placement","C. Configure RADIUS authentication","D. Establish the SSID and password policy"],a:"B",e:"Site surveys (pre-deployment) physically walk the facility to: identify walls/obstacles that attenuate signal, locate interference sources (microwaves, other wireless networks), determine coverage requirements per area, and determine optimal AP count and placement. This prevents coverage gaps that lead to rogue APs or poor security."}, {s:false,q:"Authentication protocols for wireless networks (like EAP-TLS) require which authentication method considered strongest?",o:["A. Shared pre-shared key (PSK)","B. Username and password only","C. Mutual certificate-based authentication — both client and server authenticate with digital certificates","D. MAC address filtering"],a:"C",e:"EAP-TLS with mutual certificate authentication is the strongest wireless authentication: the server presents a certificate (client verifies it's the legitimate network) AND the client presents a certificate (server verifies it's an authorized device). This requires a PKI infrastructure but prevents both rogue AP attacks and unauthorized device connections."}, {s:false,q:"Cryptographic protocols used in wireless security include WPA3 with which cipher suite?",o:["A. RC4 (used in WEP)","B. TKIP (used in original WPA)","C. AES-CCMP (WPA2) and AES-GCMP-256 (WPA3)","D. DES (outdated symmetric encryption)"],a:"C",e:"WPA2 uses AES-CCMP (Counter Mode with CBC-MAC Protocol). WPA3 uses AES-GCMP-256 (Galois/Counter Mode Protocol) — stronger authenticated encryption. WEP used RC4 (broken); original WPA used TKIP (also deprecated). Organizations should use WPA3 where supported and WPA2-AES where WPA3 isn't available."}, {s:false,q:"Secure baselines for cloud infrastructure should include:",o:["A. Default cloud provider settings without modification","B. Hardened configurations following CIS benchmarks — disabling default public access, enforcing MFA, enabling logging, and restricting permissions","C. Only encryption of stored data","D. Network firewall rules only"],a:"B",e:"Cloud infrastructure secure baselines include: disable public access by default, enforce MFA for all accounts, enable comprehensive logging (CloudTrail, Cloud Audit Logs), apply least-privilege IAM policies, encrypt data at rest and in transit, disable unused services, and follow CIS Cloud benchmarks specific to AWS/Azure/GCP."}, {s:false,q:"Embedded systems hardening differs from standard server hardening because:",o:["A. Embedded systems have more available security tools","B. Embedded systems often have limited processing, no standard OS, proprietary firmware, and restricted update mechanisms — requiring specialized hardening approaches","C. Embedded systems are always air-gapped","D. Embedded systems use standard Windows hardening guidelines"],a:"B",e:"Embedded system hardening: limited resources constrain what security can run; no standard OS (can't use standard AV/EDR); proprietary firmware requires vendor-specific updates; update mechanisms may be manual or absent. Focus: disable unused interfaces (USB, serial, Bluetooth), change default credentials, segment from other networks, and update firmware when possible."}, {s:false,q:"Which power protection device provides both short-term battery backup AND long-duration power generation for extended outages?",o:["A. UPS alone","B. Surge protector","C. UPS bridging to generator — UPS provides instant backup while generator starts, then generator sustains power","D. PDU (Power Distribution Unit)"],a:"C",e:"The standard data center power protection stack: UPS provides instant battery backup (seconds to minutes) bridging the gap while the generator starts. Generator provides extended power during outages (hours to days, limited by fuel). Together they protect against both brief power fluctuations and extended outages."}, ]; const Q_4_6 = [ // Identity and access management {s:false,q:"Which IAM process removes or disables a user account and revokes all access when an employee leaves the organization?",o:["A. Provisioning","B. Federation","C. De-provisioning","D. Attestation"],a:"C",e:"De-provisioning (offboarding) immediately removes or disables accounts and revokes all access when users leave. This is a critical security process — orphaned accounts are a common attack vector. Automated de-provisioning tied to HR systems ensures no accounts are missed."}, {s:false,q:"Identity proofing is BEST described as:",o:["A. Verifying a user's password complexity","B. Verifying that a person requesting an account or credential is actually who they claim to be before provisioning access","C. Testing that identity systems are functioning correctly","D. Auditing existing user accounts for compliance"],a:"B",e:"Identity proofing establishes the real-world identity of a person before issuing credentials. In-person identity proofing (showing a government ID) is strongest. Remote proofing uses document verification, biometrics, and knowledge-based questions. Strong identity proofing prevents account creation fraud."}, {s:false,q:"Single Sign-On (SSO) using SAML allows users to:",o:["A. Use a single password for all systems","B. Authenticate once to an Identity Provider and access multiple Service Providers without re-authenticating","C. Sign documents with a digital signature","D. Use biometrics instead of passwords"],a:"B",e:"SAML SSO: user authenticates to the Identity Provider (IdP) once; the IdP issues a SAML assertion; the Service Provider (application) trusts the IdP's assertion and grants access without requiring the user to log in again. Reduces password fatigue and centralizes authentication for auditing."}, {s:false,q:"OAuth 2.0 is PRIMARILY used for:",o:["A. Encrypting user passwords","B. Authorization — allowing applications to access resources on behalf of users without sharing credentials","C. Multi-factor authentication","D. Certificate-based authentication"],a:"B",e:"OAuth 2.0 provides authorization delegation — allows a user to grant an application access to their resources (e.g., 'allow this app to read your Google Drive') without sharing their Google credentials with that app. OAuth is authorization ('what can you do?'); OpenID Connect adds authentication ('who are you?') on top of OAuth."}, {s:true,sc:"A new employee joins the finance team. The IT team needs to grant her access to the financial reporting system, expense management tool, and the company's ERP system. Three weeks later, she is promoted to Finance Manager and needs additional access.",q:"What IAM process covers both of these situations?",o:["A. De-provisioning and identity proofing","B. Access provisioning and permission assignment based on role changes","C. Federation and SSO","D. Attestation and MFA enrollment"],a:"B",e:"Access provisioning grants initial access when someone joins or changes roles. Permission assignment (based on RBAC — her Finance Manager role) determines which specific permissions she receives. When promoted, her role-based permissions should be updated to reflect her new responsibilities. This is the complete joiner-mover-leaver lifecycle."}, {s:false,q:"Mandatory Access Control (MAC) assigns access based on:",o:["A. The data owner's discretion","B. The user's job role","C. Security labels/clearance levels assigned by the system — the user cannot change their own access","D. The time of day the access is requested"],a:"C",e:"MAC uses security labels (Unclassified, Confidential, Secret, Top Secret) assigned by system administrators. Users cannot change their own clearance or object labels. Access is granted when the user's clearance level equals or exceeds the data's classification. Used in government/military systems where data sensitivity is formally defined."}, {s:false,q:"Which access control model allows data owners to set permissions on their own resources at their discretion?",o:["A. Mandatory Access Control (MAC)","B. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)","C. Discretionary Access Control (DAC)","D. Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)"],a:"C",e:"DAC (Discretionary Access Control) allows the data/resource owner to set permissions at their discretion. Examples: Windows file NTFS permissions (owner can grant/deny access), Unix file permissions. Flexible but inconsistent — relies on owners making good security decisions. Less suitable for high-security environments."}, {s:false,q:"Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) makes access decisions based on:",o:["A. Only the user's role","B. Security classification labels","C. Multiple attributes of user, resource, environment, and action — enabling fine-grained, context-aware policies","D. The data owner's manual approval"],a:"C",e:"ABAC evaluates multiple attributes: user attributes (role, department, clearance), resource attributes (classification, owner), environmental attributes (time, location, network), and action attributes (read/write). This enables very granular policies: 'Finance staff in the office during business hours can read financial reports.' More flexible than RBAC but more complex to manage."}, {s:false,q:"Time-of-day restrictions as an access control implement which security principle?",o:["A. Least Privilege — applied temporally","B. Non-Repudiation","C. Data Sovereignty","D. Defense in Depth"],a:"A",e:"Time-of-day restrictions implement least privilege temporally — users can only access systems during hours when they legitimately need access. If accounts should only be used 8am-6pm weekdays, any after-hours access attempt is blocked and flagged. This limits the window of opportunity for compromised credential abuse."}, {s:false,q:"Password vaulting in Privileged Access Management (PAM) provides which benefit?",o:["A. Forces users to create complex passwords","B. Stores privileged account credentials securely and provides them on-demand without users knowing the actual password","C. Automatically rotates all user passwords daily","D. Prevents password reuse"],a:"B",e:"Password vaulting stores privileged credentials in a secure vault. When needed, the vault provides temporary access without revealing the actual password to the user — preventing credential sharing, theft, or screenshot capture. After the session, the password can be automatically rotated. Users get access to systems without ever seeing passwords."}, {s:false,q:"Just-in-time (JIT) permissions in PAM means:",o:["A. Passwords expire every minute","B. Privileged access is granted only when needed, for the minimum required duration, then automatically revoked","C. Users receive permissions as soon as they request them","D. Permissions are assigned based on real-time risk scores"],a:"B",e:"Just-in-Time (JIT) permissions: privileged access (admin rights, elevated permissions) is provisioned only when explicitly requested for a specific task, for a defined time window, then automatically revoked. Standing privileged accounts (always-on admin) are eliminated. This dramatically reduces the attack surface for privileged account compromise."}, {s:false,q:"Multi-factor authentication (MFA) using a hardware security key (FIDO2) provides what advantage over SMS-based OTP?",o:["A. Hardware keys are cheaper than SMS","B. Hardware keys are phishing-resistant — the key is bound to specific domains and cannot be used on fake sites","C. Hardware keys don't require internet","D. Hardware keys provide longer session tokens"],a:"B",e:"FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware keys are phishing-resistant — the cryptographic challenge is bound to the legitimate website's origin. A fake phishing site cannot obtain a valid response from the key. SMS OTP is vulnerable to SIM-swapping, SS7 attacks, and real-time phishing. Hardware keys are the strongest widely-available MFA factor."}, {s:false,q:"Ephemeral credentials in PAM are:",o:["A. Credentials with very long passwords","B. Short-lived, automatically expiring credentials generated on-demand for specific sessions — never reused","C. Credentials stored in encrypted vaults","D. Credentials requiring biometric verification"],a:"B",e:"Ephemeral credentials are temporary, single-use credentials generated for a specific session and automatically expire. Even if captured, they cannot be reused after expiration. Cloud environments increasingly use ephemeral credentials for service accounts (AWS STS tokens, GCP service account short-lived tokens) — eliminating long-term static credentials."}, {s:false,q:"Federation differs from SSO in that:",o:["A. They are identical concepts","B. SSO operates within a single organization; federation extends authentication trust across organizational boundaries","C. Federation is less secure than SSO","D. SSO requires certificates; federation uses passwords"],a:"B",e:"SSO: authenticate once, access multiple apps within the same organization (same IdP). Federation: trust relationship between separate organizations' identity systems — authenticate with Organization A's IdP to access Organization B's services (e.g., login with Google to access a third-party service). SAML and OAuth enable federation."}, {s:false,q:"Attestation in IAM refers to:",o:["A. Authenticating hardware devices to the network","B. Periodic review and certification by managers that user access rights are still appropriate and needed","C. Recording all user authentication events","D. Verifying software code integrity"],a:"B",e:"Access attestation/certification: periodic review (quarterly, annually) where managers certify that their reports' access rights are still appropriate. Catches privilege creep — accumulated permissions from role changes. When a manager cannot justify an access right, it's removed. This is an access governance control required by many compliance frameworks."}, {s:false,q:"LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) is used in authentication architectures to:",o:["A. Encrypt user passwords before storage","B. Access and maintain distributed directory information — commonly used to authenticate users against Active Directory","C. Provide certificate-based authentication","D. Configure network access policies"],a:"B",e:"LDAP is the protocol used to query directory services (Active Directory, OpenLDAP) for authentication and user information. When you log into a corporate system with your domain credentials, LDAP queries AD to verify your username/password and retrieve group memberships for authorization decisions."}, {s:false,q:"Something you are (biometric) authentication factors include:",o:["A. Passwords, PINs","B. Smart cards, hardware tokens","C. Fingerprints, retina scans, facial recognition, voice recognition","D. One-time passwords via SMS"],a:"C",e:"'Something you are' factors are biometric — based on physical or behavioral characteristics: fingerprint, retina scan, iris scan, facial recognition, voice recognition, palm vein pattern. These are inherently linked to the person and cannot be forgotten — but can potentially be copied (deepfake) and raise privacy concerns."}, {s:false,q:"Rule-based access control differs from role-based access control in that:",o:["A. They are the same — both abbreviated RBAC","B. Rule-based uses specific defined rules (IF-THEN conditions) applied to all users; role-based assigns permissions based on organizational roles","C. Rule-based only applies to network access","D. Role-based is more granular than rule-based"],a:"B",e:"Rule-based access control: defines specific rules that apply to all users (e.g., 'no access allowed between 11pm-6am', 'block access from outside the country'). Role-based (RBAC): groups users by job role and assigns permissions to roles. Rules can be layered on top of RBAC for additional constraints."}, {s:false,q:"Password length contributes more to password security than complexity because:",o:["A. Length is irrelevant to brute-force resistance","B. Each additional character exponentially increases the search space — a 16-character password takes astronomically longer to crack than an 8-character complex one","C. Complex passwords are easier to remember","D. Length only matters for system-generated passwords"],a:"B",e:"Password length exponentially increases brute-force resistance. A 16-character random lowercase password has more combinations than an 8-character complex password with mixed case, numbers, and symbols. Modern guidance (NIST 800-63B) emphasizes length (minimum 8, encourage longer) over complex character requirements that cause password reuse."}, {s:false,q:"Passwordless authentication methods include:",o:["A. Very long passwords over 30 characters","B. FIDO2 passkeys, biometrics, magic links, and certificate-based authentication — eliminating traditional passwords","C. Single-character PINs","D. Password managers"],a:"B",e:"Passwordless authentication eliminates the shared secret (password) entirely. Methods: FIDO2 passkeys (cryptographic key pair bound to device), biometrics, magic email links, push notifications. Benefits: eliminates phishing of passwords, no credential stuffing, no brute force of password databases. This is the direction modern identity is heading."}, {s:false,q:"Interoperability in IAM means:",o:["A. All users must use the same authentication method","B. Identity systems can work together across different platforms, vendors, and organizations using open standards","C. Multiple users can share the same credentials","D. Authentication systems work without internet access"],a:"B",e:"IAM interoperability enables identity systems to work together using open standards: SAML (enterprise federation), OAuth/OIDC (web/mobile), SCIM (user provisioning), LDAP (directory queries). Without interoperability standards, organizations would need separate accounts for every system and couldn't federate across organizations."}, {s:true,sc:"A user is trying to access a healthcare database from an unrecognized device in a foreign country at 2am. The system requires them to complete additional verification (biometric + manager approval) before granting temporary read-only access.",q:"Which access control concept is being applied?",o:["A. Mandatory Access Control","B. Time-of-day restrictions only","C. Adaptive/risk-based access control with step-up authentication","D. Discretionary Access Control"],a:"C",e:"This is adaptive/risk-based access control — the system continuously evaluates risk signals (unknown device, foreign country, unusual time) and dynamically adjusts authentication requirements (step-up auth). This aligns with Zero Trust's adaptive identity concept. Normal low-risk access uses standard MFA; high-risk access requires additional verification or temporary limited access."}, {s:false,q:"The principle of least privilege for service accounts means:",o:["A. Service accounts should use simple passwords","B. Service accounts should have only the specific permissions needed for the service's function — not broad admin rights","C. Services should not have accounts","D. Service accounts should be rotated daily"],a:"B",e:"Least privilege for service accounts: many breaches occur because compromised service accounts had excessive permissions. A web service account should only be able to read the specific database tables it needs — not have DBA rights. Service accounts with admin/root permissions are high-value targets that can enable complete environment compromise."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following BEST describes a privilege access workstation (PAW)?",o:["A. A workstation with administrative shortcuts","B. A dedicated, hardened workstation used exclusively for privileged administrative tasks — not for email, web browsing, or standard work","C. A workstation with extra RAM for running multiple VMs","D. A laptop with biometric authentication"],a:"B",e:"A PAW (Privileged Access Workstation) is a dedicated, hardened device used only for administrative tasks — never for email, web browsing, or general use. Keeps privileged sessions isolated from the malware risks of standard computing. Credentials entered on a PAW are much less likely to be captured by keyloggers or phishing."}, {s:false,q:"Which PAM control requires two separate authorized individuals to simultaneously perform a sensitive administrative action?",o:["A. Just-in-time permissions","B. Ephemeral credentials","C. Dual control / Two-person integrity","D. Password vaulting"],a:"C",e:"Dual control / Two-person integrity (TPI) requires two authorized individuals to simultaneously participate in a sensitive action — for example, two administrators must both authenticate to unlock a root vault. This prevents any single person from performing unauthorized privileged actions unilaterally, and is common in financial and military systems."}, ]; // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ // DOMAIN 5.0 — SECURITY PROGRAM MANAGEMENT & OVERSIGHT // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ const Q_5_1 = [ // Effective security governance {s:false,q:"An Acceptable Use Policy (AUP) PRIMARILY defines:",o:["A. Technical security controls for systems","B. Acceptable and unacceptable behaviors for users when using organizational technology and data","C. Encryption requirements for data","D. Network architecture standards"],a:"B",e:"An AUP sets expectations for user behavior — what employees can and cannot do with company systems, networks, and data. It covers: personal use limits, prohibited activities (illegal content, harassment), social media, email use, and consequences for violations. Users must acknowledge reading and agreeing to the AUP."}, {s:false,q:"Which governance document provides high-level principles and direction for security without specifying technical implementation details?",o:["A. Procedures","B. Standards","C. Policy","D. Guidelines"],a:"C",e:"A policy provides high-level direction and mandatory requirements (e.g., 'All data must be classified'). Standards specify specific requirements (AES-256 encryption). Guidelines are recommendations. Procedures are step-by-step instructions. Policies drive standards which drive procedures."}, {s:false,q:"Which role is PRIMARILY responsible for making day-to-day decisions about data usage and ensuring data quality in their business area?",o:["A. Data Custodian","B. Data Owner/Controller","C. Data Processor","D. Data Steward/Custodian"],a:"D",e:"Data Stewards/Custodians implement and manage the technical controls for data protection on behalf of data owners. Data Owners/Controllers have business responsibility and decide what data is collected/used. Under GDPR: Controller determines purposes of processing; Processor processes on the controller's behalf."}, {s:false,q:"A Change Management policy belongs to which governance category?",o:["A. Physical Security Standards","B. Information Security Policies","C. Encryption Standards","D. Access Control Standards"],a:"B",e:"Change management policy falls under information security policies — it governs how changes to IT systems are evaluated, approved, and implemented to minimize security risk. Other information security policies include: incident response, acceptable use, data classification, business continuity, and SDLC policies."}, {s:true,sc:"A company's CEO asks the security team why they need formal security policies since 'everyone knows what they should do.' The CISO must justify the need for documented policies.",q:"Which governance benefit BEST justifies formal documented security policies?",o:["A. Policies make the company look good to customers","B. Policies establish clear, enforceable expectations — providing the legal and operational foundation for security controls, accountability, and incident response","C. Policies are only needed for regulatory compliance","D. Policies replace the need for technical controls"],a:"B",e:"Formal policies: establish clear expectations (what is required), enable enforcement (violations must be documented to be actionable), support legal defensibility (prove due care in litigation), satisfy regulatory requirements, provide the foundation for technical controls, and create accountability. Without documented policy, 'everyone knows' is an unenforceable assumption."}, {s:false,q:"The Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC) policy in security governance ensures:",o:["A. Software is developed as quickly as possible","B. Security is integrated throughout the development process — from requirements through deployment and maintenance","C. All software is outsourced to third parties","D. Developers use only approved programming languages"],a:"B",e:"An SDLC security policy mandates that security be considered at every phase: requirements (security requirements), design (threat modeling), development (secure coding standards, SAST), testing (DAST, penetration testing), deployment (configuration hardening), and maintenance (patch management). This is 'shift-left security' — catching flaws early when they're cheapest to fix."}, {s:false,q:"External governance considerations include which of the following?",o:["A. Internal audit committee decisions","B. Industry regulations, national laws, global standards, and local regulatory requirements that impose security obligations","C. Employee handbook updates","D. Board of directors resolutions"],a:"B",e:"External governance considerations: regulations (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, POPIA), industry standards (ISO 27001, NIST), national laws (country-specific cybersecurity legislation), local/regional requirements, and global frameworks. Organizations must identify all applicable external requirements and ensure their security program complies."}, {s:false,q:"Centralized governance means:",o:["A. Security decisions are made by individual business units","B. Security policies, standards, and oversight are managed by a central security function with organization-wide authority","C. All data is stored in one location","D. Only the CEO makes security decisions"],a:"B",e:"Centralized governance: a central security team (CISO/security office) sets policies, standards, and oversight that apply organization-wide. Benefits: consistency, economies of scale, clear accountability. Risks: bottleneck, disconnect from business units, less agility. Decentralized governance distributes security responsibility to business units — more agile but less consistent."}, {s:false,q:"Data processors under GDPR are:",o:["A. Technology vendors providing hardware","B. Entities that process personal data on behalf of the data controller — such as cloud providers or payroll processors","C. Employees who enter data manually","D. IT staff who manage databases"],a:"B",e:"Under GDPR, a data processor processes personal data on behalf of the controller. Examples: cloud providers (process your customer data), payroll processors, email marketing platforms. Processors must have a Data Processing Agreement with the controller and are subject to GDPR obligations. Controllers bear primary responsibility for GDPR compliance."}, {s:false,q:"Onboarding/offboarding procedures as security controls specifically address which risk?",o:["A. Data breach through network attacks","B. Insider threat — ensuring access is properly provisioned on arrival and revoked on departure","C. Malware through email","D. Physical security breaches"],a:"B",e:"Onboarding/offboarding procedures directly manage the insider threat lifecycle: onboarding ensures new employees receive appropriate access (not too much, not too little), complete security training, and sign policies; offboarding ensures all access is revoked immediately, devices are returned, and credentials are changed when someone leaves."}, {s:false,q:"Security playbooks serve which governance purpose?",o:["A. Provide general security awareness information to all staff","B. Document step-by-step response procedures for specific security scenarios — ensuring consistent, effective responses","C. Define the security architecture","D. Track security metrics and KPIs"],a:"B",e:"Playbooks document step-by-step procedures for specific scenarios: ransomware response playbook, phishing response playbook, DDoS mitigation playbook. They ensure consistent, effective responses by providing clear instructions that any trained responder can follow — reducing decision fatigue and errors during high-stress incidents."}, {s:false,q:"Monitoring and revision of security governance refers to:",o:["A. Monitoring employee internet usage","B. Regularly reviewing policies and controls for continued relevance, effectiveness, and alignment with changing threats and regulations","C. Monitoring network traffic","D. Reviewing server logs"],a:"B",e:"Security governance monitoring and revision: policies and controls must be regularly reviewed and updated as: threat landscape changes, new regulations emerge, technology evolves, business changes, or incidents reveal gaps. A policy written in 2015 may not address current cloud, remote work, or AI-related threats. Regular review cycles (annual policy review) are a governance best practice."}, {s:false,q:"Which governance structure type has formal authority and ultimate accountability for the organization's risk posture?",o:["A. Security awareness training committee","B. Board of Directors","C. External auditors","D. IT helpdesk"],a:"B",e:"The Board of Directors has ultimate fiduciary and governance responsibility — including cybersecurity risk. They approve risk appetite, major security investments, and hold the CEO/CISO accountable. Modern boards increasingly have cybersecurity expertise and receive regular security briefings. Board-level accountability drives security investment."}, {s:true,sc:"A mid-sized company has no formal security policy. An employee accidentally emails a customer database to an external address. When disciplinary action is attempted, HR says they cannot enforce consequences because there is no written policy the employee violated.",q:"This scenario illustrates which governance failure?",o:["A. Lack of technical controls","B. Absent formal security policies — making security requirements unenforceable","C. Insufficient security training","D. Weak network security"],a:"B",e:"Without formal documented policies, organizations cannot enforce expectations. Disciplinary action requires violating a documented rule. Security policies establish the rules; HR enforces violations. This scenario also highlights that data classification and handling policies would have made clear that customer data cannot be externally emailed."}, {s:false,q:"Physical security standards in a governance framework would include:",o:["A. Password complexity requirements","B. Data center access controls, visitor management, clean desk policy, and environmental controls requirements","C. Software patch management procedures","D. Network encryption standards"],a:"B",e:"Physical security standards define requirements for protecting physical assets: data center access controls (badge readers, biometrics), visitor management procedures, clean desk/screen lock policies, environmental controls (temperature, humidity, fire suppression), hardware disposal procedures, and media handling requirements."}, {s:false,q:"Encryption standards in governance specify:",o:["A. Who is responsible for encryption","B. The approved encryption algorithms, key lengths, and protocols to be used — such as AES-256 for data at rest, TLS 1.3 for data in transit","C. Physical lock standards for secure areas","D. Password hashing requirements only"],a:"B",e:"Encryption standards specify mandatory technical requirements: approved algorithms (AES-256, RSA-2048+), minimum key lengths, approved protocols (TLS 1.3 — not 1.0/1.1/SSL), prohibited weak algorithms (DES, MD5, RC4), and where encryption is required (data at rest, data in transit, backup media). Standards translate policy into specific technical requirements."}, {s:false,q:"Access control standards differ from access control policies in that:",o:["A. They are identical documents","B. Policies define high-level requirements; standards specify the specific technical controls — e.g., 'MFA required for privileged access using TOTP or hardware key'","C. Standards apply to technical staff only","D. Policies are voluntary; standards are mandatory"],a:"B",e:"Policy: 'Multi-factor authentication is required for all privileged access.' Standard: 'MFA must use TOTP (minimum 6 digits), hardware FIDO2 keys, or biometrics. SMS OTP is not permitted for privileged accounts.' Standards translate policy intent into specific, measurable, technical requirements that can be audited."}, {s:false,q:"Disaster recovery policy falls under which governance category?",o:["A. Physical Security Standards","B. Information Security Policies","C. HR Policies","D. Financial Policies"],a:"B",e:"Disaster recovery policy is an information security policy that mandates: DR planning requirements, RTO/RPO objectives, testing frequency, roles/responsibilities, and offsite backup requirements. It provides the governance mandate; the DR plan itself provides the operational procedures."}, {s:true,sc:"A healthcare organization processing EU patient data must comply with both HIPAA (US) and GDPR (EU). The compliance team identifies that GDPR's right to erasure conflicts with HIPAA's 6-year retention requirement for medical records.",q:"What type of governance consideration does this represent?",o:["A. Internal audit conflict","B. Conflicting external regulatory requirements requiring legal analysis and risk-based decision making","C. Policy vs. standard conflict","D. Technical implementation challenge"],a:"B",e:"This represents conflicting external regulatory requirements — a common governance challenge in multinational organizations. When regulations conflict, organizations must: identify which law takes precedence, seek legal guidance on compliance obligations, implement the more protective requirement where possible, and document the decision rationale. This requires legal and governance expertise, not just technical decisions."}, {s:false,q:"Incident response policy in governance defines:",o:["A. The specific technical steps to respond to malware","B. The requirement to have an incident response capability, roles and responsibilities, reporting requirements, and escalation procedures","C. Only the forensic procedures for evidence collection","D. The list of approved security tools for incident response"],a:"B",e:"The incident response policy provides governance mandate and high-level structure: defines what constitutes a security incident, establishes the requirement for an IRP, assigns roles/responsibilities, mandates reporting timelines (including regulatory notification requirements), and establishes escalation paths. The IR plan and playbooks provide the operational details."}, {s:false,q:"Data custodians are PRIMARILY responsible for:",o:["A. Determining what data is collected and its purpose","B. Implementing and maintaining the technical controls that protect data on behalf of the data owner","C. Setting data retention policies","D. Authorizing user access to data"],a:"B",e:"Data custodians (often IT) implement and maintain the technical controls: storage, backups, access control implementation, encryption, and system maintenance. They protect data on behalf of the data owner who has business responsibility. The owner decides what data needs protection; the custodian implements the protection."}, {s:false,q:"Government entities as a governance structure type have what role in organizational security?",o:["A. Provide free security consulting to organizations","B. Establish regulatory requirements, investigate breaches, and enforce compliance through legislation and oversight bodies","C. Operate as internal security teams","D. Provide security certifications to individuals"],a:"B",e:"Government entities (FTC, CISA, ICO, POPIA Regulator) establish binding legal requirements for security, investigate breaches, impose fines for non-compliance, and in some cases provide security guidance and frameworks. Organizations must monitor relevant government agencies' requirements and guidance in their operating jurisdictions."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following represents a complete governance hierarchy from most strategic to most tactical?",o:["A. Procedures → Standards → Policies → Guidelines","B. Policies → Standards → Procedures → Guidelines","C. Standards → Policies → Procedures","D. Policies → Standards → Guidelines → Procedures"],a:"D",e:"Governance hierarchy: Policies (what must be done — high-level mandatory requirements) → Standards (specific technical requirements — how to implement policies) → Guidelines (recommendations and best practices) → Procedures (step-by-step operational instructions). Each level is more specific and tactical than the previous."}, {s:false,q:"The role of an audit committee in security governance is to:",o:["A. Conduct penetration tests","B. Provide independent oversight of security controls, review audit findings, and ensure management addresses identified deficiencies","C. Develop security policies","D. Manage day-to-day security operations"],a:"B",e:"The audit committee (typically a board subcommittee) provides independent oversight: reviews internal and external audit findings, ensures management appropriately addresses deficiencies, reviews financial controls and cybersecurity programs, and reports to the full board. The audit committee provides accountability and independent challenge to management's security posture."}, {s:false,q:"Business continuity policy differs from disaster recovery policy in that:",o:["A. They cover identical scope","B. Business continuity focuses on maintaining operations during disruption; disaster recovery focuses on restoring IT systems after disruption","C. Disaster recovery is broader than business continuity","D. Business continuity only applies to physical disasters"],a:"B",e:"Business Continuity (BCP) ensures the organization keeps functioning during a disruption — alternate processes, manual workarounds, essential function prioritization, communication plans, alternate locations. Disaster Recovery (DRP) focuses on restoring IT systems. BCP is broader; DRP is the IT-specific subset. You need BCP while DRP is executing."}, ]; const Q_5_2 = [ // Risk management process {s:false,q:"Qualitative risk analysis differs from quantitative in that:",o:["A. Qualitative uses financial calculations; quantitative uses descriptive terms","B. Qualitative uses descriptive terms (High/Medium/Low); quantitative uses numerical values (ALE, SLE, ARO)","C. They are the same process with different names","D. Quantitative is always better than qualitative"],a:"B",e:"Qualitative risk analysis: rates risks using descriptive scales (Critical/High/Medium/Low/Informational). Faster, requires less data, widely used. Quantitative risk analysis: assigns financial values (ALE = SLE × ARO). Requires more data but supports ROI calculations for controls. Most organizations use qualitative for prioritization and quantitative for major investments."}, {s:false,q:"Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) is calculated as:",o:["A. Threat frequency × Asset value","B. Asset value × Exposure Factor","C. ALE ÷ ARO","D. Probability × Impact"],a:"B",e:"SLE = Asset Value × Exposure Factor (EF). Example: Server worth $100,000 with 40% exposure factor (40% of value likely lost per incident) = SLE of $40,000. SLE is then used to calculate ALE: ALE = SLE × ARO (Annualized Rate of Occurrence)."}, {s:true,sc:"A company's database server is valued at $500,000. Security analysts estimate that a successful ransomware attack would affect 60% of its value. The historical frequency of similar attacks on comparable systems is once every 5 years.",q:"What is the Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)?",o:["A. $300,000","B. $60,000","C. $500,000","D. $100,000"],a:"B",e:"SLE = $500,000 × 0.60 = $300,000. ARO = 1/5 years = 0.2. ALE = $300,000 × 0.2 = $60,000. This means, on average, this risk costs $60,000 per year. Any security control costing less than $60,000/year with equivalent risk reduction represents a positive ROI."}, {s:false,q:"Risk appetite differs from risk tolerance in that:",o:["A. They are identical concepts","B. Risk appetite is the overall amount of risk an organization is willing to accept; risk tolerance is the acceptable variation around specific risk targets","C. Risk tolerance is higher-level than risk appetite","D. Risk appetite applies only to financial risks"],a:"B",e:"Risk appetite: the board-level strategic statement of how much risk the organization is willing to accept in pursuit of its objectives ('We will not accept risks that could result in regulatory sanctions'). Risk tolerance: the operational acceptable variation ('Systems can be down up to 4 hours per year'). Tolerance operationalizes appetite."}, {s:false,q:"Which risk management strategy involves implementing controls to reduce the likelihood or impact of a risk?",o:["A. Risk Transfer","B. Risk Acceptance","C. Risk Avoidance","D. Risk Mitigation"],a:"D",e:"Risk mitigation implements controls to reduce risk likelihood (preventive controls) or impact (corrective controls). Example: mitigating ransomware risk by deploying EDR, offline backups, and email filtering. It doesn't eliminate risk but reduces it to an acceptable level. Most security controls implement risk mitigation."}, {s:false,q:"Risk avoidance as a strategy means:",o:["A. Documenting a risk and accepting it","B. Transferring the risk to an insurance company","C. Eliminating the risk by not engaging in the risky activity or technology","D. Reducing the risk through controls"],a:"C",e:"Risk avoidance eliminates risk by choosing not to engage in the activity that creates the risk. Example: not collecting customer biometric data (avoids the risk of biometric data breach), not using a vulnerable third-party service. Often the most effective risk strategy but may limit business capabilities."}, {s:false,q:"A risk register is BEST described as:",o:["A. A list of security vulnerabilities found by a scanner","B. A centralized document tracking identified risks, their owners, likelihood/impact ratings, risk responses, and current status","C. A database of security patches","D. A log of past security incidents"],a:"B",e:"A risk register is the central repository for risk management: each identified risk has — description, likelihood rating, impact rating, risk score (L×I), risk owner (accountable person), planned response (mitigate/accept/transfer/avoid), current status, and residual risk after controls. It's the operational foundation of risk management."}, {s:false,q:"An 'expansionary' risk appetite means an organization:",o:["A. Is extremely risk-averse in all activities","B. Is willing to accept higher risk in pursuit of growth, innovation, and competitive advantage","C. Only accepts risks in one business area","D. Transfers all risks to insurance"],a:"B",e:"Expansionary risk appetite: the organization actively accepts higher risk to pursue growth opportunities, innovation, and market leadership. Common in startups, high-growth tech companies. Contrasted with conservative (minimal risk tolerance, heavily regulated industries) and neutral (balanced approach)."}, {s:false,q:"Key Risk Indicators (KRIs) in a risk register serve what purpose?",o:["A. List known attack techniques","B. Provide early warning metrics that signal increasing risk levels — enabling proactive response before a risk materializes","C. Document completed risk assessments","D. Track security tool performance"],a:"B",e:"KRIs are leading indicators that signal rising risk levels before incidents occur. Examples: increasing number of unpatched critical vulnerabilities (KRI for breach), increasing phishing click rates (KRI for credential compromise), rising employee turnover in security team (KRI for insider threat/capability decline). KRIs trigger proactive risk management."}, {s:false,q:"Risk threshold in a risk register defines:",o:["A. The maximum budget for security controls","B. The level at which a risk requires immediate escalation or additional action — the line between acceptable and unacceptable risk","C. The minimum CVSS score for patching","D. The frequency of risk assessments"],a:"B",e:"A risk threshold is the level at which a risk becomes unacceptable and requires immediate action — it defines the boundary between acceptable residual risk (below threshold) and unacceptable risk (above threshold). Risks above threshold must be escalated to management and require additional mitigation or acceptance by senior leadership."}, {s:false,q:"Continuous risk assessment means:",o:["A. Completing a risk assessment every 10 years","B. Ongoing, real-time monitoring and evaluation of risks as the threat landscape, technology, and business change","C. Running the same risk assessment checklist every month","D. Assessing risks only when new systems are deployed"],a:"B",e:"Continuous risk assessment maintains an up-to-date risk picture by constantly monitoring for: new vulnerabilities (CVEs), threat intelligence, business changes, technology changes, and control effectiveness. This replaces the traditional annual point-in-time assessment that may miss rapidly evolving threats. Risk registers are living documents, not annual snapshots."}, {s:false,q:"The impact dimension of risk analysis evaluates:",o:["A. How likely an attack is to succeed","B. The consequences if the risk materializes — financial loss, operational disruption, reputational damage, regulatory penalties","C. The technical sophistication of threat actors","D. The number of vulnerabilities in a system"],a:"B",e:"Impact measures the severity of consequences if the risk materializes: financial (direct losses, fines), operational (downtime, service degradation), reputational (customer trust, brand damage), legal (litigation, regulatory penalties), and safety (physical harm). High-impact/high-likelihood risks top the priority list."}, {s:false,q:"Risk reporting serves which organizational purpose?",o:["A. Provides technical details to IT staff","B. Communicates risk posture, significant risks, and risk management effectiveness to decision-makers — enabling informed investment and governance decisions","C. Documents audit findings only","D. Lists all security vulnerabilities"],a:"B",e:"Risk reporting translates technical risk into business language for executives, board, and regulators: current risk posture, top risks and trends, risk treatment status, metrics/KRIs, and resource needs. Effective risk reporting enables informed governance decisions (where to invest, what to accept) and demonstrates due care to regulators."}, {s:true,sc:"A company's security team identifies 450 vulnerabilities from their last scan. The CTO asks the security manager to prioritize them for remediation but says they have limited patching resources this quarter.",q:"Which risk analysis approach should be used to prioritize which vulnerabilities to patch first?",o:["A. Patch all vulnerabilities alphabetically","B. Quantitative analysis using CVSS scores combined with asset criticality and business impact — prioritizing critical/high-CVSS vulnerabilities on critical systems","C. Accept all vulnerabilities and document in risk register","D. Transfer all vulnerability risk to cyber insurance"],a:"B",e:"Effective prioritization: CVSS score (severity) × asset criticality (business importance of affected system) × exploitability (is it being actively exploited?). A CVSS 9.8 vulnerability on a customer-facing payment server takes priority over a CVSS 7.0 on a non-critical internal system. This is quantitative risk analysis applied to vulnerability management."}, {s:false,q:"Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO) represents:",o:["A. The financial impact of a single security incident","B. The estimated frequency of a specific risk occurring per year","C. The percentage of assets affected by a breach","D. The time required to recover from an incident"],a:"B",e:"ARO estimates how often a specific risk event occurs per year. Examples: ARO = 1 (once per year), ARO = 0.1 (once per decade), ARO = 10 (ten times per year). Used in calculating ALE: ALE = SLE × ARO. Determining ARO requires historical data, threat intelligence, and industry statistics."}, {s:false,q:"Which risk management strategy transfers financial risk to a third party while retaining operational responsibility?",o:["A. Risk Mitigation","B. Risk Avoidance","C. Risk Transfer (cyber insurance)","D. Risk Acceptance"],a:"C",e:"Risk transfer shifts the financial burden of a risk to a third party — most commonly through cyber insurance. The organization still operates the risky activity but the insurer pays for breach costs, legal fees, and recovery. The organization retains operational responsibility (and the reputational risk) but transfers financial exposure."}, {s:false,q:"Mean Time to Repair (MTTR) in a Business Impact Analysis represents:",o:["A. The maximum acceptable downtime","B. The average time required to restore a system to full operation after a failure","C. The acceptable data loss in hours","D. The frequency of system failures"],a:"B",e:"MTTR is the average recovery time after failure. Lower MTTR = faster recovery. MTTR drives DR planning decisions: if your MTTR is 8 hours but RTO is 4 hours, you need to improve recovery capability. Reducing MTTR requires: better documentation, trained staff, pre-staged recovery resources, and automation."}, {s:false,q:"'Accepting' a risk with an exception means:",o:["A. The risk is reduced to acceptable levels through controls","B. Senior management formally acknowledges and accepts a specific risk that cannot be mitigated within policy — with documented justification","C. The risk is transferred to insurance","D. The risk is ignored without documentation"],a:"B",e:"A formal risk acceptance with exception: when a risk exceeds normal tolerance but cannot be mitigated (legacy system, cost-prohibitive control), senior management formally accepts it in writing with documented justification, compensating controls, and review schedule. This documents the informed decision and assigns accountability — different from simply ignoring the risk."}, {s:false,q:"Exposure factor (EF) in quantitative risk analysis represents:",o:["A. The number of systems affected by a vulnerability","B. The percentage of an asset's value expected to be lost if a specific threat materializes","C. The probability of a threat occurring","D. The cost of implementing security controls"],a:"B",e:"Exposure Factor (EF) is the percentage of asset value expected to be lost per incident. If a $1,000,000 data center would lose 30% of its value in a fire (fire suppression protects 70%), EF = 0.30. SLE = Asset Value × EF = $1,000,000 × 0.30 = $300,000 per fire incident."}, {s:false,q:"A Business Impact Analysis (BIA) specifically identifies which information?",o:["A. All technical vulnerabilities in the IT environment","B. Critical business processes, their dependencies, financial impacts of disruption, and recovery priorities (RTO/RPO)","C. The complete list of security controls","D. All threat actors targeting the organization"],a:"B",e:"A BIA identifies: critical business processes (which functions are essential), dependencies (what systems/people/suppliers they rely on), financial impact of disruption over time, and drives RTO/RPO decisions. The BIA is the foundation of DR and BCP planning — answering 'what must we restore first and how quickly?'"}, {s:false,q:"'Probability' in risk analysis refers to:",o:["A. The severity of impact if a risk occurs","B. The likelihood that a specific threat will successfully exploit a vulnerability in a given time period","C. The frequency of security audits","D. The percentage of data encrypted"],a:"B",e:"Probability (Likelihood): the chance that a threat will successfully exploit a vulnerability and materialize as an incident. Influenced by: threat actor capability, vulnerability severity, existing controls, and threat frequency. Probability × Impact = Risk Score (in qualitative matrices) or forms the basis of ARO in quantitative analysis."}, {s:false,q:"Risk owners in a risk register are responsible for:",o:["A. Implementing all technical security controls","B. Accepting accountability for managing a specific risk — ensuring controls are implemented and residual risk is at acceptable levels","C. Performing external audits","D. Setting the organization's risk appetite"],a:"B",e:"A risk owner is the individual accountable for managing a specific risk — typically a business leader, not an IT person. They ensure controls are implemented, monitor risk status, decide on risk treatment, and report to senior management. Risk ownership cannot be delegated to IT — business leaders must own business risks."}, {s:false,q:"Recurring risk assessment means:",o:["A. Performing the same risk assessment once at initial deployment","B. Conducting periodic risk assessments on a scheduled basis (quarterly, annually) to identify new risks and changes to existing ones","C. Assessing risks only after a security incident","D. Continuously monitoring technical controls"],a:"B",e:"Recurring risk assessments are conducted on a defined schedule — annual comprehensive assessments, quarterly reviews of top risks, assessment triggered by major changes. This ensures the risk picture remains current and identifies emerging risks before they materialize. Point-in-time ad-hoc assessments are insufficient in rapidly changing environments."}, {s:false,q:"Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) is used in risk analysis to:",o:["A. Measure how long it takes to recover from an incident","B. Estimate the expected operational lifespan of hardware components — informing replacement planning and availability calculations","C. Calculate annual loss expectancy","D. Determine backup frequency requirements"],a:"B",e:"MTBF is a hardware reliability metric. Higher MTBF = less frequent failures = higher availability. Used for: hardware procurement (selecting more reliable components), maintenance scheduling, availability calculations (Availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)), and identifying components requiring redundancy for HA requirements."}, {s:false,q:"Risk exemption differs from risk exception in that:",o:["A. They are identical terms","B. An exception is temporary approval to deviate from policy; an exemption is a permanent exclusion from a specific policy requirement","C. Exceptions are permanent; exemptions are temporary","D. Neither requires senior management approval"],a:"B",e:"Risk exception: temporary, time-limited acceptance of a deviation from a security policy — with a remediation plan and review date. Risk exemption: permanent exclusion of a specific system or process from a policy requirement — typically for legacy systems where compliance is technically impossible. Both require senior management approval and documentation."}, ]; const Q_5_6 = [ // Security awareness practices {s:false,q:"Which security awareness topic is MOST effective at reducing phishing susceptibility?",o:["A. Password complexity requirements","B. Recognizing phishing indicators — urgency, suspicious sender, unexpected requests, mismatched URLs","C. Network architecture training","D. Encryption configuration training"],a:"B",e:"Effective phishing awareness teaches specific recognition skills: urgency/pressure tactics, sender address verification (display name vs actual address), URL hovering (hover before clicking to see actual destination), unexpected requests for credentials/payments, and poor grammar/spelling. Generic security training is less effective than specific, scenario-based phishing recognition training."}, {s:false,q:"Phishing simulation campaigns in security awareness programs serve what purpose?",o:["A. Test technical email filtering systems","B. Send realistic fake phishing emails to employees — measuring click rates and providing immediate teachable moments","C. Train only the IT security team","D. Replace formal security training"],a:"B",e:"Phishing simulations (services like KnowBe4, Proofpoint Security Awareness): send realistic but harmless fake phishing emails to employees, track who clicks/provides credentials, automatically enroll 'caught' employees in targeted training, and measure improvement over time. Immediate feedback at the moment of failure is highly effective learning."}, {s:false,q:"Security awareness training should be conducted:",o:["A. Only when a new employee joins","B. Initially upon joining (onboarding) and recurrently throughout employment — with periodic refreshers and updates for new threats","C. Only after a security incident","D. Annually, with no other touchpoints"],a:"B",e:"Effective security awareness: initial training at onboarding (baseline), annual refreshers (maintain awareness, cover new threats), role-based training (specific training for privileged users, executives), just-in-time training (phishing simulation feedback), and ongoing communication (security tips, phishing alerts). Annual-only training is insufficient in a dynamic threat environment."}, {s:false,q:"Insider threat awareness training should cover which behaviors to watch for?",o:["A. Poor password practices only","B. Unusual access patterns, unusual data downloads, disgruntlement indicators, policy violations, and unusual working hours","C. Only technical security skills","D. External threat actor techniques only"],a:"B",e:"Insider threat indicators to train employees and managers to recognize: unusual working hours, accessing systems outside job role, large data downloads, expressed disgruntlement, financial stress indicators, policy violations, and removable media usage. A culture where employees feel safe reporting concerning behavior is as important as the training itself."}, {s:true,sc:"After a phishing simulation, the security team finds that 30% of employees clicked the link. The CISO wants to reduce this to under 5% within 6 months.",q:"Which combination of awareness activities would BEST achieve this goal?",o:["A. Block all email attachments at the gateway","B. Monthly phishing simulations with immediate targeted training for those who click, combined with ongoing awareness communications","C. Terminate employees who click phishing links","D. Provide a one-time mandatory phishing training module"],a:"B",e:"Research shows regular simulations with immediate teachable moments + ongoing awareness communications reduce phishing click rates more effectively than any single intervention. Monthly simulations create habitual vigilance. Targeted training at the moment of failure (clicking) is most impactful. One-time training shows temporary improvement that quickly fades."}, {s:false,q:"Password management training should teach users to:",o:["A. Write passwords on paper and store near their workstation for convenience","B. Use a password manager to generate and store unique, complex passwords for every site","C. Use the same strong password for all work systems","D. Share passwords with trusted colleagues for backup access"],a:"B",e:"Password management best practice training: use a password manager (creates unique, complex passwords for every site — eliminates reuse), enable MFA on all accounts, never share passwords (even with IT), report suspicious password-reset requests, and never reuse passwords across personal and work accounts. Password reuse is one of the highest risks."}, {s:false,q:"Removable media and cables awareness training addresses which threat?",o:["A. Network-based DDoS attacks","B. Malware introduction and data exfiltration via unauthorized USB drives, charging cables, and portable storage devices","C. Phishing via email","D. Social media privacy"],a:"B",e:"Removable media risks: USB drives found in parking lots (baiting), infected personal drives brought from home, data theft via USB, malicious charging cables (OMG Cable). Training: never plug in found USB devices, only use company-approved media, disable personal storage on work systems, report found devices to security team."}, {s:false,q:"Operational security (OPSEC) awareness training teaches employees to:",o:["A. Operate security tools correctly","B. Protect sensitive information from being disclosed — avoiding oversharing on social media, in public places, or with unauthorized parties","C. Respond to security incidents","D. Configure network security devices"],a:"B",e:"OPSEC awareness: don't share work details on social media (attackers use LinkedIn for reconnaissance), avoid discussing sensitive matters in public places, be cautious about what you put in email, don't reveal organizational information to unverified callers, and be aware that small pieces of information can be combined into a larger intelligence picture."}, {s:false,q:"Anomalous behavior recognition training teaches employees and managers to identify which type of risk?",o:["A. Network security anomalies requiring technical tools","B. Behavioral changes in colleagues that may indicate insider threat — security policy violations, access pattern changes, data hoarding","C. New phishing techniques","D. External attack indicators"],a:"B",e:"Anomalous behavior recognition: employees/managers trained to notice: colleagues accessing unusual systems, downloading large amounts of data, working unusual hours, expressing disgruntlement about leaving, bringing personal storage to work, or showing signs of financial stress. Human observation is a powerful complement to technical monitoring."}, {s:false,q:"Hybrid and remote work environment security awareness should specifically address:",o:["A. How to use video conferencing software","B. Home network security risks, shoulder surfing in public places, VPN usage, secure Wi-Fi practices, and physical document security","C. Only corporate office security policies","D. Personal device ownership policies"],a:"B",e:"Remote/hybrid work security: secure home Wi-Fi (WPA3, changed default credentials), always use VPN on public Wi-Fi, avoid sensitive conversations in public (shoulder surfing), lock screens when stepping away, secure printing and destruction of printed materials at home, ensure family members don't use work devices, and report security incidents promptly even remotely."}, {s:false,q:"Social engineering awareness training should teach employees to recognize which manipulation techniques?",o:["A. Only phishing via email","B. Pretexting, impersonation, urgency/scarcity tactics, authority exploitation, and reciprocity — across email, phone, and in-person interactions","C. Only technical exploits","D. Only attacks on executives"],a:"B",e:"Comprehensive social engineering awareness covers: pretexting (fabricated scenarios), impersonation (IT, executives, vendors), urgency ('do this NOW'), authority ('I'm from the CEO's office'), reciprocity ('I did you a favor, now do me one'), and fear ('your account will be suspended'). These techniques work across all communication channels."}, {s:false,q:"Situational awareness in security means:",o:["A. Knowing the location of all security cameras","B. Being alert to one's environment and recognizing potential security threats — physical tailgating, shoulder surfing, suspicious individuals","C. Understanding the organization's network topology","D. Awareness of all current CVEs"],a:"B",e:"Situational awareness: employees are trained to notice and respond to: people tailgating through secure doors, shoulder surfing at workstations, suspicious individuals in secure areas, unattended devices, and potential surveillance. This extends security from the digital realm to the physical environment every employee operates in."}, {s:false,q:"Security awareness program execution requires which elements for effectiveness?",o:["A. Only annual training modules","B. Leadership support, relevant engaging content, measured outcomes (phishing click rates, policy compliance), and continuous reinforcement","C. Mandatory attendance without measurement","D. Technical training only for IT staff"],a:"B",e:"Effective awareness program execution: leadership support (sets tone from top), engaging relevant content (not just compliance checkbox), measured outcomes (track phishing click rates, training completion, incident rates), continuous reinforcement (not just annual), and tailored role-based content. Programs measured on completion rate but not behavior change fail to improve security."}, {s:false,q:"Responding to reported suspicious messages — what should employees be trained to do when they recognize a phishing attempt?",o:["A. Delete the email immediately without reporting","B. Report to the security team via the designated reporting mechanism and avoid clicking any links","C. Forward the phishing email to colleagues as a warning","D. Reply to the email asking if it's legitimate"],a:"B",e:"Employees should be trained to: report suspicious emails via the designated channel (report button, security email address), not click any links or attachments, not forward to colleagues (spreads risk), and not reply (confirms your email is active). Reporting creates threat intelligence for the security team to block the campaign for all employees."}, {s:true,sc:"A security team notices that phishing click rates dropped from 25% to 8% following a security awareness campaign, but employees are still occasionally violating the clean desk policy — leaving confidential documents unsecured.",q:"What should the security awareness program's next focus be?",o:["A. More phishing simulations","B. Physical security and clean desk policy reinforcement — addressing the remaining behavior gap","C. Increase technical email filtering","D. Terminate policy violators"],a:"B",e:"Effective security awareness programs use metrics to identify behavior gaps and focus training accordingly. Phishing is improving (8% from 25%) — that program is working. The identified gap is physical security / clean desk policy. The program should now focus on physical security awareness: importance of clean desks, document handling, risks of exposed information, and how to properly secure/dispose of documents."}, {s:false,q:"Development of a security awareness program should begin with:",o:["A. Creating the training content immediately","B. Identifying the target audience, current threat landscape, regulatory requirements, and measuring the baseline security behavior before training","C. Selecting the training platform vendor","D. Setting the training budget"],a:"B",e:"Security awareness program development: start with needs assessment — who is the audience (executives, developers, general staff)? What are the current threats? What regulations require specific training? What is the baseline (current phishing click rates, policy compliance)? Measure before training to know what gaps exist and measure improvement afterward."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following represents 'unintentional' risky behavior that security awareness training should address?",o:["A. A malicious insider stealing data","B. An employee accidentally emailing customer PII to the wrong address due to email autocomplete","C. An attacker compromising an employee's credentials","D. A hacker exploiting a software vulnerability"],a:"B",e:"Unintentional risky behavior: accidental data disclosure (wrong email address), clicking phishing links without recognizing them, misconfiguring settings, using weak passwords without understanding the risk, leaving devices unlocked, or inadvertently sharing sensitive information in public forums. Training reduces unintentional risk by building security-conscious habits."}, {s:false,q:"Which measurement BEST indicates security awareness program effectiveness?",o:["A. Number of employees who completed training","B. Measurable behavioral change — reduced phishing click rates, increased incident reporting, fewer policy violations","C. Employee satisfaction scores with training content","D. Cost of the training program"],a:"B",e:"Behavior change metrics are the true measure of awareness effectiveness: reduced phishing click rates (over time, through regular simulations), increased security incident reporting (employees reporting suspicious activity), reduced policy violations, and improved scores on social engineering tests. Completion rates measure engagement, not effectiveness. The goal is changed behavior, not trained employees."}, {s:false,q:"Initial security awareness reporting refers to:",o:["A. Reporting security incidents to regulators","B. Establishing baseline metrics before a security awareness program begins — measuring initial phishing click rates, policy compliance, and security knowledge","C. The first quarterly security report to management","D. Initial onboarding security documentation"],a:"B",e:"Initial reporting/baseline: before launching a security awareness program, establish baseline measurements — phishing click rate (send a baseline simulation), security knowledge assessment, policy compliance audit. This baseline allows you to demonstrate program impact ('We reduced phishing clicks from 28% to 6%') and justifies program investment."}, {s:false,q:"Business email compromise (BEC) awareness should train employees to verify:",o:["A. Email formatting and fonts","B. Unusual financial transfer requests by calling the requestor using a known-good phone number — not replying to the suspicious email","C. The sender's email signature format","D. The time the email was sent"],a:"B",e:"BEC prevention training: for any unusual financial request (wire transfer, gift card purchase, payment redirect), employees must verify by calling the apparent requester directly using a phone number from a trusted directory — not a number provided in the suspicious email. BEC attackers own the email chain; they cannot fake an out-of-band phone call to a known number."}, {s:false,q:"Security awareness program metrics should measure which outcomes?",o:["A. Only training completion rates","B. Behavioral change metrics — reduced phishing click rates, increased incident reporting, fewer policy violations over time","C. Employee satisfaction with training content","D. Number of security tools deployed"],a:"B",e:"Outcome metrics measure actual behavior change: phishing simulation click rates (trending down over time), near-miss reporting rates (employees reporting suspicious activity), policy violations from audits, and security incident rates attributable to human error. Training completion metrics only measure whether people attended, not whether they changed behavior."}, {s:false,q:"Which of the following represents 'risky' behavior that security awareness should address?",o:["A. Using company-approved applications","B. Using weak passwords, reusing passwords across sites, or sharing credentials with colleagues","C. Locking screen when leaving desk","D. Reporting suspicious emails to security team"],a:"B",e:"Risky behavior in security awareness: weak/reused passwords (enables credential attacks), credential sharing (violates accountability), using personal devices without MDM, clicking unknown links, connecting to unsecured Wi-Fi without VPN, leaving sensitive data unattended, and using unapproved apps (Shadow IT). Awareness training must explicitly address these specific behaviors."}, {s:false,q:"When an employee receives a suspicious email and reports it to the security team, this represents which awareness program success indicator?",o:["A. The employee failed the phishing test","B. A successful awareness outcome — the employee recognized a threat and followed proper reporting procedures","C. A security incident that needs investigation only","D. Evidence the email filter failed"],a:"B",e:"Employee incident reporting is a key positive outcome metric. An employee who recognizes a suspicious email and reports it (rather than clicking or ignoring it) has demonstrated exactly the behavior security awareness training aims to produce. Organizations should make reporting easy, acknowledge reporters, and track reporting rates as a KPI."}, {s:false,q:"Security awareness training for executives should PRIMARILY focus on which topics?",o:["A. Technical security tool configuration","B. Spear phishing/whaling, business email compromise, social engineering targeting executives, and their role in security culture","C. Network security architecture","D. Penetration testing techniques"],a:"B",e:"Executives are primary targets for spear phishing/whaling (highly targeted attacks impersonating CEO/CFO), BEC (fraudulent wire transfer requests), and social engineering (attackers leveraging executive authority). Executive awareness focuses on these high-value attack scenarios. Executives also set security culture — their visible commitment to security has outsized organizational impact."}, {s:true,sc:"A security awareness manager presents metrics to the CISO: 94% training completion rate, but phishing click rates remain at 22% after 12 months of the program. The CISO is concerned the program isn't working.",q:"What does this data suggest about the awareness program?",o:["A. The program is highly effective — high completion rates indicate success","B. Training completion doesn't equal behavior change — the program needs more frequent simulations, better content relevance, and targeted training for repeat clickers","C. The phishing simulations are too difficult","D. 22% click rate is the industry standard and is acceptable"],a:"B",e:"High completion rate + no click rate improvement = program is check-the-box compliance, not behavior change. Fixes: more frequent and varied phishing simulations (not just annual), immediate targeted training for repeat clickers, more engaging/relevant content (not generic slides), role-based training, and management reinforcement. The goal is behavior change, not training attendance."}, ]; const Q_EXAM = [ // ── GENERATED (50 questions) ── {s:false,obj:"1.1",q:"Which type of security control is BEST described as one that guides user behavior through policies and training?",o:["A. Detective","B. Preventive","C. Directive","D. Corrective"],a:"C",e:"Directive controls guide and mandate behavior — acceptable use policies, security training, and procedures are directive controls. They tell people what they should do."}, {s:false,obj:"1.1",q:"A smoke detector that triggers an alarm when fire is detected is an example of which control type?",o:["A. Preventive","B. Detective","C. Corrective","D. Compensating"],a:"B",e:"A smoke detector identifies (detects) a fire in progress and alerts — this is a detective control. Fire suppression systems (sprinklers) that respond to the fire would be corrective."}, {s:true,obj:"1.1",sc:"A hospital's legacy radiology system runs Windows XP and handles sensitive patient data. The vendor no longer provides patches, and the hospital cannot upgrade without replacing expensive equipment.",q:"Which combination of controls BEST compensates for the inability to patch?",o:["A. Delete all data from the system","B. Network isolation, enhanced monitoring, and strict access controls","C. Install a third-party antivirus and hope for the best","D. Apply the patch manually from the internet"],a:"B",e:"Classic compensating controls for unpatched legacy systems: network isolation (limits attack surface), enhanced monitoring (detective), and strict access controls (preventive). This is a standard PCI-DSS and HIPAA compensating control scenario."}, {s:false,obj:"1.1",q:"Physical locks, security fencing, and badge readers are examples of which control category?",o:["A. Technical","B. Operational","C. Managerial","D. Physical"],a:"D",e:"Physical controls are tangible, real-world safeguards. Locks, fencing, badge readers, bollards, and security cameras are all physical controls."}, {s:false,obj:"1.2",q:"A company stores customer credit card data. They replace each card number with a random 16-digit string that cannot be reversed. The original is stored in a separate, highly secure vault system. What technique is this?",o:["A. Encryption","B. Hashing","C. Tokenization","D. Data masking"],a:"C",e:"Tokenization replaces sensitive data with a non-sensitive, non-reversible token. The original value is stored in a vault and retrieved when needed. Unlike encryption, the token has no mathematical relationship to the original."}, {s:false,obj:"1.2",q:"Which physical control prevents a second unauthorized person from following an authorized person through a secure door?",o:["A. Bollards","B. RFID badge","C. Access control vestibule","D. Motion sensor"],a:"C",e:"An access control vestibule (mantrap) prevents tailgating by requiring one door to close before the next opens — only one person can pass through at a time."}, {s:true,obj:"1.2",sc:"An organization discovers that an employee has been downloading large quantities of HR files to an external drive over the past month. When the investigation starts, the employee says 'I never touched those files.' The access logs show the employee's username, badge access to the server room, and exact timestamps of every file copied.",q:"Which security principle allows the organization to refute the employee's claim?",o:["A. Availability","B. Non-repudiation","C. Integrity","D. Confidentiality"],a:"B",e:"Non-repudiation prevents parties from denying their actions. The combination of authenticated session logs, badge access records, and timestamps creates an irrefutable audit trail. The employee cannot credibly deny accessing the files."}, {s:false,obj:"1.2",q:"In Zero Trust, which plane is responsible for enforcing access decisions on actual network traffic?",o:["A. Control Plane","B. Management Plane","C. Data Plane","D. Application Plane"],a:"C",e:"The Data Plane is where actual traffic flows and where the PEP enforces access decisions. The Control Plane (Policy Engine, Policy Admin) makes decisions; the Data Plane enforces them."}, {s:false,obj:"1.3",q:"Before ANY change can be approved in a formal change management process, which document MUST be prepared?",o:["A.
