Performance benchmark report","B. Stakeholder satisfaction survey","C. Backout/rollback plan","D. Employee training plan"],a:"C",e:"A backout plan (rollback plan) is mandatory before any change is approved. It documents exactly how to reverse the change if it causes problems. No change should be approved without one."}, {s:true,obj:"1.3",sc:"A developer bypasses the change management process and directly modifies the firewall configuration to allow a port needed for his project. He doesn't document the change. Two years later, a security audit discovers the unauthorized rule, but no one knows why it exists or if it's safe to remove it.",q:"What is the PRIMARY security risk this situation demonstrates?",o:["A. Lack of version control","B. Undocumented, unauthorized changes create security risks that persist indefinitely","C. Insufficient firewall capacity","D. Lack of antivirus on the developer's workstation"],a:"B",e:"Undocumented, unauthorized changes persist because no one knows they exist or why they were made. This is the core reason change management exists — to ensure every change is tracked, authorized, and removable. The risk is the ongoing exposure from a forgotten, unreviewed rule."}, {s:false,obj:"1.3",q:"What is the purpose of a maintenance window in change management?",o:["A. To allow users to perform personal tasks on work computers","B. To schedule changes during low-impact periods to minimize disruption","C. To conduct employee performance reviews","D. To perform physical maintenance on server hardware only"],a:"B",e:"A maintenance window is a pre-agreed period of low business activity when IT changes can be applied with minimal disruption. Changes outside maintenance windows risk impacting users during peak hours."}, {s:false,obj:"1.4",q:"Which encryption algorithm is the current standard for bulk data encryption and is approved by NIST?",o:["A. DES","B. 3DES","C. AES","D. RSA"],a:"C",e:"AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) is the NIST-approved standard for symmetric encryption. It replaced DES and 3DES due to their vulnerabilities. AES-128, AES-192, and AES-256 are all considered secure."}, {s:false,obj:"1.4",q:"What is a root of trust in PKI?",o:["A. The first certificate ever created","B. The Root CA certificate, which is self-signed and serves as the ultimate anchor of trust in a PKI hierarchy","C. A hardware token storing private keys","D. The process of verifying a user's identity before issuing a certificate"],a:"B",e:"The root of trust is the Root CA — the self-signed certificate at the top of the PKI hierarchy. All other certificates derive their trust from the root. Browsers and operating systems come pre-installed with trusted root CA certificates."}, {s:true,obj:"1.4",sc:"An attacker intercepts encrypted messages between a client and server. She records the encrypted sessions. Even though she can't decrypt them now, she plans to decrypt them later once computing power is sufficient to break the key.",q:"Which cryptographic property, if implemented, would protect past sessions even if the key is eventually broken?",o:["A. Key escrow","B. Perfect Forward Secrecy (not in objectives — use DHE)","C. Symmetric key rotation","D. Certificate pinning"],a:"C",e:"Regular symmetric key rotation means that even if one session key is eventually broken, past sessions encrypted with different keys remain secure. This limits the damage of key compromise to only data encrypted with that specific key."}, {s:false,obj:"1.4",q:"Blockchain provides security primarily through:",o:["A. Symmetric encryption of all records","B. A distributed, immutable ledger where each block is cryptographically linked to the previous one","C. Centralized key management","D. Hardware security modules"],a:"B",e:"Blockchain is a distributed, append-only ledger where each block contains a hash of the previous block. Modifying any past block would invalidate all subsequent blocks and be immediately detectable. This makes it tamper-evident and immutable."}, {s:false,obj:"1.1",q:"Which of the following is an example of a corrective PHYSICAL control?",o:["A. Installing security cameras","B. Replacing a broken lock on a server room door after a breach","C. Issuing warning signs on equipment","D. Conducting a fire drill"],a:"B",e:"Replacing a broken lock after a breach is a corrective physical control — it corrects (fixes) a physical security deficiency that was exploited. Installing cameras = detective; warning signs = deterrent; fire drills = operational."}, {s:false,obj:"1.2",q:"Which CIA component is MOST directly attacked by a man-in-the-middle attack that intercepts and modifies data in transit?",o:["A. Availability","B. Confidentiality only","C. Both Integrity and Confidentiality","D. Non-repudiation"],a:"C",e:"A MitM attack that intercepts data attacks Confidentiality (attacker reads the data). If the attacker also modifies the data, Integrity is also violated. Both components are impacted when data is both read and altered."}, {s:false,obj:"1.2",q:"Implicit Trust Zones in Zero Trust are BEST described as:",o:["A. Areas of the network that are fully trusted without verification","B. Network micro-segments with default-deny posture where trust must be explicitly granted per session","C. External cloud environments trusted by default","D. Zones where physical access implies network trust"],a:"B",e:"Implicit Trust Zones in Zero Trust are tightly controlled micro-segments with default-deny posture. Unlike traditional networks where 'internal = trusted,' Zero Trust implicit trust zones still require explicit authorization for every session. The word 'implicit' refers to the level of trust built into the segment's design, not unconditional trust."}, {s:true,obj:"1.2",sc:"A company installs a convincingly realistic fake server labeled 'FINANCE-SERVER-01' on an isolated network segment. The server has realistic-looking files but contains no real data. Every access to this server triggers a SIEM alert, and all commands are logged.",q:"This is BEST described as which type of deception technology, and what control type does it represent?",o:["A. Honeytoken — Preventive","B. Honeypot — Detective","C. Honeyfile — Compensating","D. Honeynet — Corrective"],a:"B",e:"A fake, decoy server is a honeypot. It functions as a detective control — it doesn't prevent intrusion but detects attackers who have already gained network access by logging all their actions when they interact with the fake server."}, {s:false,obj:"1.3",q:"What does 'version control' allow a security team to do after a change introduces a vulnerability?",o:["A. Automatically patch the vulnerability","B. Roll back to the previous known-good configuration and identify exactly what changed","C. Alert users about the vulnerability","D. Block all network traffic until the vulnerability is fixed"],a:"B",e:"Version control allows the team to: (1) identify exactly what changed between versions (diff), (2) roll back to the previous working configuration, and (3) audit who made the change and when. This is critical for rapid incident response."}, {s:false,obj:"1.4",q:"Hashing is described as a one-way function. What does this mean?",o:["A. It can only be used on files traveling one direction across the network","B. Given a hash value, it is computationally infeasible to determine the original input","C. It only works on data one byte at a time","D. It uses only one encryption key"],a:"B",e:"A one-way hash function is irreversible — you can compute the hash from the input, but you cannot compute the original input from the hash (without brute-force or rainbow tables). This is why passwords are stored as hashes rather than in encrypted or plaintext form."}, {s:false,obj:"1.4",q:"What does AES-256 refer to?",o:["A. AES encryption using 256 rounds of processing","B. AES encryption with a 256-bit key length","C. AES implemented with 256 different algorithms","D. AES version 256"],a:"B",e:"AES-256 refers to AES encryption with a 256-bit key length. Longer keys are harder to brute force. AES supports 128, 192, and 256-bit keys. AES-256 is considered military-grade and is used to protect top-secret information."}, {s:true,obj:"1.4",sc:"A developer creates a software tool and wants users to be able to verify that the downloaded tool is genuinely from her and hasn't been modified since release. She creates a digital artifact using her private key.",q:"Which action should users take to verify the software?",o:["A. Decrypt the software using the developer's private key","B. Verify the digital signature using the developer's public key","C. Hash the software and compare to the developer's private key hash","D. Encrypt the software with their own public key"],a:"B",e:"Users verify a digital signature using the signer's public key. If the signature validates, it proves: (1) the software was signed with the developer's private key (authenticity) and (2) no bits have changed since signing (integrity). The public key is safe to distribute widely."}, {s:false,obj:"1.1",q:"A company installs an intrusion prevention system (IPS) that automatically drops malicious packets before they reach their destination. This is BEST classified as which control type?",o:["A. Detective","B. Compensating","C. Preventive","D. Corrective"],a:"C",e:"An IPS that drops malicious packets is a preventive control — it stops attacks before they cause damage. Contrast with IDS (detection only = detective). The key distinction: IDS alerts, IPS acts."}, {s:false,obj:"1.2",q:"Authentication using 'something you know, something you have, and something you are' is BEST described as:",o:["A. Single-factor authentication","B. Risk-based authentication","C. Multi-factor authentication (MFA)","D. Adaptive Identity"],a:"C",e:"MFA uses two or more different factor types: something you know (password/PIN), something you have (token/phone), something you are (biometric). Using multiple factor types is MFA. Using two instances of the same factor type (two passwords) is NOT MFA."}, {s:false,obj:"1.3",q:"Which of the following represents an 'approval process' in change management?",o:["A. A developer approving their own code changes","B. A Change Advisory Board (CAB) reviewing and authorizing changes before production deployment","C. Automatic deployment approval by a CI/CD pipeline","D. User acceptance testing by end users"],a:"B",e:"A Change Advisory Board (CAB) is the formal body that reviews change requests, assesses risks and impacts, and provides official approval before changes are deployed to production. The CAB ensures the right stakeholders have reviewed and accepted the risks."}, {s:false,obj:"1.4",q:"Which of the following BEST describes data masking?",o:["A. Encrypting data with a public key before storage","B. Replacing sensitive data with realistic but fictional data for dev/test environments","C. Hiding data inside other files using steganography","D. Replacing sensitive data with non-sensitive tokens for production use"],a:"B",e:"Data masking replaces real sensitive data with realistic-looking fake data for development, testing, or training purposes. Developers get usable data structures without PII/financial data exposure. Tokenization is for production; steganography hides data in files; encryption preserves the original."}, {s:true,obj:"1.2",sc:"A security team is evaluating their organization's security posture. They compare their current controls against ISO 27001 and identify that they lack controls in 8 of the 14 control domains. They document the missing controls and create a 12-month implementation roadmap.",q:"What process have they completed?",o:["A. Risk assessment","B. Penetration testing","C. Gap analysis","D. Business impact analysis"],a:"C",e:"Gap analysis — comparing current state against a target framework (ISO 27001) to identify what is missing. The output (list of gaps + remediation roadmap) is the deliverable of a gap analysis. This is different from a risk assessment (identifies and quantifies threats) or a pen test (actively attacks the system)."}, {s:false,obj:"1.4",q:"Which of the following is the correct function of OCSP in PKI?",o:["A. Issues new certificates to end entities","B. Provides real-time revocation status of a specific certificate","C. Signs certificate signing requests from intermediate CAs","D. Stores the Root CA's private key securely"],a:"B",e:"OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol) provides real-time status of whether a specific certificate has been revoked. A client sends an OCSP request to the OCSP responder (operated by the CA) and receives a signed response indicating 'good,' 'revoked,' or 'unknown.'"}, {s:false,obj:"1.1",q:"An organization cannot disable a legacy application that processes sensitive transactions because removing it would halt operations. Instead, they place it on an isolated VLAN, add enhanced logging, and require additional authentication to access it. These measures are BEST described as:",o:["A. Preventive controls replacing the legacy system","B. Compensating controls addressing the inability to decommission the legacy system","C. Detective controls identifying new attacks","D. Directive controls requiring use of the legacy system"],a:"B",e:"When the ideal control (decommissioning the legacy system) is not possible, compensating controls are implemented instead. Network isolation, enhanced logging, and additional authentication are all compensating for the risk created by keeping the legacy application in operation."}, {s:false,obj:"1.2",q:"What is the purpose of a honeytoken?",o:["A. To attract attackers to a fake network","B. To detect attackers by using a fake data element that triggers an alert when accessed or used","C. To encrypt legitimate user tokens for secure transmission","D. To provide a backup authentication token if the primary fails"],a:"B",e:"A honeytoken is a fake data element (credential, record, file, token) with no legitimate use. If it is ever accessed or used, it definitively signals unauthorized activity — legitimate users never interact with it. Examples: fake API keys, fake DNS records, fake database records, canary tokens."}, // ── YOUR QUESTIONS FROM PDF (40 added so far) ── {s:false,obj:"1.4",q:"Which of the following ensures that a sender cannot deny sending a message?",o:["A. Encryption","B. Hashing","C. Digital Signature","D. Symmetric Key Exchange"],a:"C",e:"Digital signatures ensure non-repudiation — they prove who sent the data using the sender's private key. The sender cannot later deny signing since only they hold their private key."}, {s:false,obj:"2.1",q:"Which type of threat actor is MOST likely to have the greatest resources and patience for an extended attack?",o:["A. Insider","B. Nation-State","C. Script Kiddie","D. Hacktivist"],a:"B",e:"Nation-state actors have the highest resources, skills, and patience for prolonged, sophisticated attacks. They are often backed by government funding and have strategic, long-term objectives."}, {s:false,obj:"2.3",q:"What type of attack involves inserting malicious code into a legitimate web application to steal information from users?",o:["A. Phishing","B. SQL Injection","C. Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)","D. DNS Spoofing"],a:"C",e:"XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) injects malicious scripts into web applications to steal session cookies, user data, or redirect users. SQL Injection targets databases; phishing uses deceptive messages; DNS spoofing redirects traffic."}, {s:false,obj:"4.6",q:"A company needs to prevent unauthorized devices from connecting to its internal network. What technology should be used?",o:["A. Firewall","B. VPN","C. NAC (Network Access Control)","D. IDS"],a:"C",e:"NAC (Network Access Control) checks device health, identity, and compliance before allowing network access. It can quarantine or block non-compliant devices. A firewall filters traffic but doesn't enforce device identity; IDS only detects threats."}, {s:false,obj:"3.4",q:"Which backup type saves only the changes made since the last full backup?",o:["A. Incremental","B. Differential","C. Full","D. Snapshot"],a:"A",e:"Incremental backup captures only the changes made since the last backup of any type (full or incremental). Differential saves changes since the last full backup only. This makes incremental backups smaller and faster but slower to restore."}, {s:false,obj:"1.1",q:"What control type is a biometric fingerprint scanner?",o:["A. Technical","B. Administrative","C. Physical","D. Compensating"],a:"C",e:"A biometric fingerprint scanner is a physical control — it is a tangible device that controls physical access by authenticating users at entry points. Note: the software interpreting the scan could be considered technical, but the scanner device itself is physical."}, {s:false,obj:"4.1",q:"Which wireless security protocol is the most secure for corporate environments?",o:["A. WEP","B. WPA","C. WPA2-PSK","D. WPA3-Enterprise"],a:"D",e:"WPA3-Enterprise is the most secure wireless option for business environments — it uses 192-bit encryption in enterprise mode and requires individual authentication via RADIUS. WEP and WPA are deprecated and broken; WPA2-PSK uses a shared key which is less secure for enterprises."}, {s:false,obj:"5.6",q:"Which of the following would BEST help mitigate risks associated with phishing attacks?",o:["A. IDS","B. Security Awareness Training","C. Firewall Rules","D. Password Complexity Requirements"],a:"B",e:"Security Awareness Training is the most effective control against phishing — it teaches users to recognize and report phishing attempts. Technical controls like email filtering help, but the human element requires training. IDS detects after the fact; firewalls don't stop users from clicking links."}, {s:false,obj:"5.2",q:"Which risk response involves buying cyber insurance?",o:["A. Accept","B. Mitigate","C. Transfer","D. Avoid"],a:"C",e:"Buying cyber insurance is risk transfer — the financial risk of a breach is transferred to the insurance company. Accept = acknowledge and live with the risk; Mitigate = implement controls to reduce it; Avoid = eliminate the risky activity entirely."}, {s:false,obj:"4.6",q:"Which concept is being applied when access to files is based on job roles such as HR, IT, or Accounting?",o:["A. MAC","B. DAC","C. RBAC","D. ABAC"],a:"C",e:"RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) assigns permissions based on the user's job role in the organization. HR employees get HR access; IT gets IT access, etc. MAC uses labels; DAC lets owners decide; ABAC uses multiple attributes beyond just role."}, {s:false,obj:"4.1",q:"Which term describes an attack where a rogue Wi-Fi access point mimics a legitimate corporate wireless network to intercept user traffic?",o:["A. Rogue AP","B. Evil Twin","C. Bluejacking","D. MAC Spoofing"],a:"B",e:"An Evil Twin is a rogue access point configured to mimic a legitimate network — same SSID, appearing trustworthy. Users connect thinking it's real, allowing the attacker to intercept all traffic. A Rogue AP is unauthorized but doesn't necessarily mimic a legitimate one."}, {s:false,obj:"2.4",q:"What type of malware disguises itself as a legitimate program but delivers a malicious payload?",o:["A. Worm","B. Ransomware","C. Trojan","D. Rootkit"],a:"C",e:"A Trojan (Trojan Horse) appears to be a legitimate, useful program but contains hidden malicious code executed when the user runs it. Worms self-replicate; ransomware encrypts data; rootkits hide their presence by modifying the OS."}, {s:false,obj:"4.1",q:"Which process helps ensure that only needed ports and services are running on a server?",o:["A. Network segmentation","B. Baseline configuration","C. Change management","D. Hardening"],a:"D",e:"Hardening reduces the attack surface by disabling unnecessary services, closing unused ports, removing default accounts, and applying patches. Network segmentation isolates networks; baseline configuration documents the standard state; change management controls modifications."}, {s:false,obj:"4.6",q:"A database administrator is setting access so that users only have permission to view certain data. Which principle is being applied?",o:["A. Separation of Duties","B. Need-to-Know","C. Non-repudiation","D. Risk Transference"],a:"B",e:"Need-to-Know restricts access to only the specific data or information a user requires for their current task — even if they have the clearance level. It limits information exposure beyond what's operationally necessary."}, {s:false,obj:"3.4",q:"Which option BEST describes a warm site in disaster recovery planning?",o:["A. Fully operational copy of the production environment","B. Facility with basic hardware but not real-time data","C. Empty building with power and Internet only","D. Vendor-provided cloud backup solution"],a:"B",e:"A warm site has equipment installed and configured but requires data to be loaded and systems to be activated before use — typically takes hours to days to become operational. Hot site = fully operational immediately; cold site = empty building with only power/connectivity."}, {s:false,obj:"4.4",q:"Which technology would a company use to detect unauthorized changes to critical system files?",o:["A. DLP","B. File Integrity Monitoring (FIM)","C. SIEM","D. HIDS"],a:"B",e:"FIM (File Integrity Monitoring) creates cryptographic hashes of critical files and alerts when those hashes change — detecting unauthorized modifications to system files, configs, or executables. DLP prevents data exfiltration; SIEM correlates logs; HIDS monitors host behavior broadly."}, {s:false,obj:"2.5",q:"A phishing attack led to a ransomware infection. Which two controls would have BEST prevented the incident?",o:["A. Data Encryption","B. Email Filtering","C. Security Awareness Training","D. RAID 5"],a:"B",e:"Email Filtering blocks phishing emails before they reach users, and Security Awareness Training (C) teaches users to recognize and avoid phishing. Both B and C together address the attack vector. Data encryption protects data but doesn't prevent infection; RAID 5 provides redundancy, not security."}, {s:false,obj:"4.6",q:"Which type of access control is enforced by system policies rather than user discretion?",o:["A. DAC","B. RBAC","C. ABAC","D. MAC"],a:"D",e:"MAC (Mandatory Access Control) is strictly enforced by the operating system based on security labels — users and data owners have no discretion to change access. Used in government/military environments. DAC lets owners decide; RBAC uses roles; ABAC uses attributes."}, {s:false,obj:"1.2",q:"What is the primary purpose of a honeypot in cybersecurity?",o:["A. Encrypt sensitive data","B. Divert attackers away from real systems and study their behavior","C. Patch vulnerabilities automatically","D. Enforce firewall rules"],a:"B",e:"A honeypot is a decoy system designed to attract attackers, divert them from real production systems, and allow security teams to study attacker tactics and techniques. It is a detective and deception control."}, {s:false,obj:"4.8",q:"Which incident response phase involves learning lessons and updating the incident response plan after a security event?",o:["A. Detection","B. Containment","C. Recovery","D. Lessons Learned"],a:"D",e:"Lessons Learned (also called Post-Incident Activity) is the final incident response phase where the team analyzes what happened, what worked, what didn't, and updates processes and the IRP to improve future responses."}, {s:false,obj:"5.2",q:"Which of the following BEST describes a risk mitigation strategy?",o:["A. Ignoring a low-probability event","B. Purchasing cyber insurance","C. Installing a firewall to block threats","D. Documenting a risk acceptance form"],a:"C",e:"Risk mitigation reduces the likelihood or impact of a risk by implementing controls. Installing a firewall actively reduces the attack surface. Insurance = transfer; ignoring = accept; documenting acceptance = formal accept — none of these reduce the risk itself."}, {s:false,obj:"4.6",q:"An attacker is trying a few common passwords against thousands of different user accounts to avoid triggering lockout. What is this attack called?",o:["A. Dictionary Attack","B. Brute Force Attack","C. Password Spraying","D. Rainbow Table Attack"],a:"C",e:"Password Spraying tries a small set of common passwords (e.g., 'Password123') across many accounts — avoiding account lockout thresholds. Brute force tries many passwords against one account; dictionary attacks use wordlists; rainbow tables use pre-computed hashes."}, {s:false,obj:"3.4",q:"What is the purpose of a disaster recovery plan (DRP)?",o:["A. Prevent data breaches","B. Maintain operations during an attack","C. Restore critical business systems after disruption","D. Identify vulnerabilities before attacks occur"],a:"C",e:"A DRP (Disaster Recovery Plan) focuses on restoring IT systems and data after a disruptive event (natural disaster, cyberattack, hardware failure). BCP maintains business operations during disruption; vulnerability assessments identify weaknesses before attacks."}, {s:false,obj:"1.2",q:"Which concept ensures that sensitive data is only accessible to authorized individuals?",o:["A. Integrity","B. Confidentiality","C. Availability","D. Authentication"],a:"B",e:"Confidentiality (the 'C' in CIA) ensures that data is only accessible to authorized parties. Integrity ensures data accuracy; Availability ensures data is accessible when needed; Authentication verifies identity but isn't itself the CIA component protecting from disclosure."}, {s:false,obj:"4.3",q:"Which of the following BEST describes a vulnerability scanner?",o:["A. Blocks malicious traffic at the network perimeter","B. Actively exploits vulnerabilities to prove impact","C. Passively identifies potential weaknesses in systems","D. Encrypts sensitive communications"],a:"C",e:"A vulnerability scanner passively probes systems to identify known weaknesses, misconfigurations, and missing patches — without exploiting them. A penetration tester actively exploits vulnerabilities. Firewalls block traffic; encryption protects communications."}, {s:false,obj:"3.2",q:"Which of the following technologies uses security groups and microsegmentation to enhance cloud security?",o:["A. VPNs","B. Infrastructure as Code","C. Cloud-native firewalls","D. Software-Defined Networking (SDN)"],a:"D",e:"SDN (Software-Defined Networking) enables programmatic control of network segmentation, security groups, and microsegmentation in cloud environments. This allows granular, dynamic network security policies that traditional hardware-based networks cannot achieve as flexibly."}, {s:false,obj:"4.4",q:"A system administrator wants to monitor and correlate failed login attempts centrally across all systems. Which system should be deployed?",o:["A. SIEM","B. NAC","C. DLP","D. SOAR"],a:"A",e:"A SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) system collects, aggregates, and correlates logs from across the environment — including authentication failures. It enables centralized monitoring, alerting, and forensic analysis. NAC controls network access; DLP prevents data leakage; SOAR automates responses."}, {s:false,obj:"2.2",q:"Which attack occurs when a malicious actor corrupts a DNS server's cache to redirect users to fraudulent websites?",o:["A. DNS Poisoning","B. Domain Hijacking","C. IP Spoofing","D. ARP Poisoning"],a:"A",e:"DNS Poisoning (Cache Poisoning) corrupts a DNS server's cache with false records, redirecting users to attacker-controlled sites without their knowledge. Domain hijacking takes control of domain registration; IP spoofing fakes source IPs; ARP poisoning targets local network addressing."}, {s:false,obj:"4.6",q:"A company requires users to authenticate once and then have seamless access to multiple systems across different organizations without re-entering credentials. Which solution BEST meets this requirement?",o:["A. Federation","B. LDAP","C. Multifactor Authentication","D. VPN"],a:"A",e:"Federation enables single authentication across multiple domains or organizations using trust relationships (SAML, OAuth). This is different from SSO (same org, multiple apps). LDAP is a directory protocol; MFA adds authentication factors; VPN provides secure tunnel access."}, {s:false,obj:"3.4",q:"Which backup strategy would provide the QUICKEST recovery time in case of a server failure?",o:["A. Full Backup","B. Differential Backup","C. Incremental Backup","D. Snapshot Backup"],a:"D",e:"Snapshot backup captures the exact state of a system at a point in time and allows rapid rollback to that state — often in minutes. Full backups have the second-fastest restore; differential requires full + one differential; incremental requires full + all incrementals (slowest restore)."}, {s:false,obj:"4.6",q:"Which principle ensures that users are granted only the access necessary to perform their job functions — nothing more?",o:["A. Separation of Duties","B. Need-to-Know","C. Least Privilege","D. Role-Based Access Control"],a:"C",e:"Least privilege limits users to the minimum access rights required to perform their specific job function. This reduces the blast radius if an account is compromised. Need-to-know is similar but focuses on information access specifically; RBAC is a mechanism that can implement least privilege."}, {s:false,obj:"2.2",q:"An attacker captures wireless network traffic from a coffee shop without connecting to the network or interacting with any systems. What type of attack is this?",o:["A. Evil Twin","B. On-Path Attack (MITM)","C. Passive Eavesdropping","D. Session Hijacking"],a:"C",e:"Passive Eavesdropping involves silently capturing network traffic without actively injecting or modifying anything. The attacker listens only — no connection, no interception. An on-path/MITM attack actively inserts the attacker between communicating parties."}, {s:false,obj:"5.2",q:"What is the PRIMARY goal of a Business Impact Analysis (BIA)?",o:["A. Identify and prioritize critical business functions and quantify impact of their loss","B. Analyze threats against network security controls","C. Determine the effectiveness of existing security controls","D. Perform a penetration test of critical systems"],a:"A",e:"A BIA identifies which business functions are critical, what the financial/operational impact of their disruption would be, and establishes RTO (Recovery Time Objective) and RPO (Recovery Point Objective). This drives disaster recovery prioritization."}, {s:false,obj:"3.4",q:"What type of backup saves changes since the last full backup only (not since the last incremental), making recovery faster than incremental but using more space?",o:["A. Incremental","B. Full","C. Differential","D. Snapshot"],a:"C",e:"Differential backup saves all changes since the last full backup. Recovery requires only the full backup + the latest differential (2 sets). Incremental requires the full + every incremental since. Differential uses more storage than incremental but has faster restore times."}, {s:false,obj:"3.2",q:"Which of the following technologies BEST protects data confidentiality against on-path (Man-in-the-Middle) attacks when accessing a remote network?",o:["A. VLAN","B. IPS","C. VPN","D. RAID"],a:"C",e:"A VPN (Virtual Private Network) encrypts all traffic between the client and the network, preventing an on-path attacker from reading intercepted data. VLAN segments networks locally; IPS detects/blocks attacks but doesn't encrypt transit data; RAID provides storage redundancy."}, {s:false,obj:"4.8",q:"During which incident response phase would you isolate a compromised server from the rest of the network?",o:["A. Recovery","B. Containment","C. Lessons Learned","D. Identification"],a:"B",e:"Containment stops the spread of an incident by isolating affected systems. This occurs after Identification (detecting the incident) and before Eradication (removing the threat). Isolation prevents lateral movement to other systems while evidence is preserved."}, {s:false,obj:"4.6",q:"What security principle is being applied when employees must use separate accounts for administrative tasks versus their regular daily work?",o:["A. Separation of Duties","B. Least Privilege","C. Defense in Depth","D. Dual Control"],a:"B",e:"Using separate admin and standard accounts enforces least privilege — administrators use elevated accounts only when needed, reducing the window of exposure. If their daily-use account is compromised, attackers don't immediately have admin access. This is sometimes called 'privileged account separation.'"}, {s:false,obj:"3.1",q:"Which cloud service model allows the customer the MOST control over the operating system, middleware, and applications?",o:["A. SaaS","B. PaaS","C. IaaS","D. FaaS"],a:"C",e:"IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) provides the customer with virtualized computing, storage, and networking — the customer manages everything from the OS upward. PaaS = customer manages apps only; SaaS = customer only configures the app; FaaS = customer writes functions only."}, {s:false,obj:"3.1",q:"What is a PRIMARY security concern with Infrastructure as Code (IaC) in cloud environments?",o:["A. Outdated server hardware","B. Rapid spread of misconfigurations across many systems simultaneously","C. Vendor lock-in to a single cloud provider","D. Poor network performance due to automation overhead"],a:"B",e:"The primary security risk with IaC is that a misconfiguration in a template can be instantly deployed to hundreds of resources — amplifying errors at scale. A single insecure template can expose the entire infrastructure. This makes secure code review and template scanning critical."}, {s:false,obj:"2.2",q:"An attacker sends unsolicited messages to nearby Bluetooth-enabled devices without the owners' consent. What is this attack called?",o:["A. Bluesnarfing","B. Bluebugging","C. Bluejacking","D. Bluespoofing"],a:"C",e:"Bluejacking sends unsolicited messages to Bluetooth devices — it's generally considered more of a nuisance than a serious attack since it doesn't access data. Bluesnarfing steals data from Bluetooth devices; Bluebugging gives attackers control of the device."}, // ── YOUR QUESTIONS Q41–Q100 (from PDF) ── {s:false,obj:"3.4",q:"Which of the following BEST describes a cold site in disaster recovery?",o:["A. Operational data center ready for immediate use","B. Empty facility with basic infrastructure like power and HVAC","C. Fully equipped center with real-time data replication","D. Offsite cloud backup provider"],a:"B",e:"A cold site is an empty facility with only basic infrastructure (power, cooling, connectivity) but no equipment or data. It takes the longest time to become operational. Hot site = fully ready immediately; warm site = hardware ready, needs data."}, {s:false,obj:"4.6",q:"Which access control method enforces strict policies based on security labels such as 'Confidential' or 'Top Secret'?",o:["A. DAC","B. RBAC","C. MAC","D. ABAC"],a:"C",e:"MAC (Mandatory Access Control) uses security labels/classifications like Confidential, Secret, and Top Secret to control access. Users cannot override these policies — they are enforced by the system. Used in military and government environments."}, {s:false,obj:"2.2",q:"An employee plugs a personal USB drive into a company workstation without approval. What risk does this primarily represent?",o:["A. Insider Threat","B. Phishing Attack","C. Supply Chain Attack","D. Business Email Compromise"],a:"A",e:"Plugging an unauthorized USB device into a company system is an insider threat — whether malicious or accidental. The device could introduce malware, exfiltrate data, or bypass security controls. This is why removable media policies and endpoint controls (disable USB ports) are important."}, {s:false,obj:"4.5",q:"Which protocol secures email communication by digitally signing and encrypting messages?",o:["A. TLS","B. S/MIME","C. SSH","D. SSL"],a:"B",e:"S/MIME (Secure/Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions) provides end-to-end email security through digital signatures (authentication/non-repudiation) and encryption (confidentiality). TLS secures the transport channel but not the message itself; SSH is for secure remote access."}, {s:false,obj:"1.1",q:"Which type of control is implementing a security awareness training program?",o:["A. Physical","B. Technical","C. Preventive","D. Administrative"],a:"D",e:"A security awareness training program is an administrative control (also called managerial/operational control). Administrative controls include policies, procedures, standards, and training programs. They guide human behavior rather than using technology or physical barriers."}, {s:false,obj:"4.4",q:"What is the MOST appropriate tool to use when wanting to aggregate, correlate, and analyze logs from multiple systems?",o:["A. VPN","B. Firewall","C. SIEM","D. NAC"],a:"C",e:"A SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) system aggregates logs from across the enterprise, correlates events to identify patterns, and generates alerts. It is the central hub for security monitoring and analysis. NAC controls network access; firewalls filter traffic; VPNs encrypt connections."}, {s:false,obj:"1.2",q:"Which of the following would MOST help prevent unauthorized physical access to a data center?",o:["A. IDS","B. Biometric Access Controls","C. VPN","D. Anti-Malware"],a:"B",e:"Biometric access controls (fingerprint, retina, facial recognition) are highly effective physical security measures for data centers because they verify the person's unique biological traits — they cannot be shared, stolen like a badge, or guessed like a PIN. IDS detects network threats; VPNs protect remote access."}, {s:false,obj:"3.2",q:"What security concept involves separating services and functions into isolated containers or segments to minimize the attack surface?",o:["A. Microsegmentation","B. Defense in Depth","C. Least Privilege","D. Data Sovereignty"],a:"A",e:"Microsegmentation divides the network or workloads into small, isolated zones with their own security controls. Even if one segment is compromised, lateral movement to other segments is prevented. This is a key Zero Trust implementation technique used heavily in cloud and data center environments."}, {s:false,obj:"2.2",q:"An attacker successfully tricks a user into giving up login credentials via a fake login page that mimics a specific individual's company portal. What attack technique was used?",o:["A. Spear Phishing","B. Vishing","C. Smishing","D. Pharming"],a:"A",e:"Spear Phishing is a targeted phishing attack customized for a specific individual or organization. The fake login page tailored to the target's company is a hallmark of spear phishing. Vishing = phone-based; Smishing = SMS-based; Pharming = DNS manipulation redirecting all users."}, {s:false,obj:"4.8",q:"Which phase of the incident response process involves finding and removing malware from infected systems after it has been contained?",o:["A. Preparation","B. Containment","C. Eradication","D. Lessons Learned"],a:"C",e:"Eradication is the phase where the root cause and all traces of the threat (malware, backdoors, compromised accounts) are removed from affected systems. It follows Containment (stopping the spread) and precedes Recovery (restoring systems to production)."}, {s:false,obj:"4.4",q:"Which security tool uses signatures and anomaly detection to identify malicious network traffic without blocking it?",o:["A. Firewall","B. SIEM","C. IDS","D. DLP"],a:"C",e:"An IDS (Intrusion Detection System) uses signature matching and anomaly detection to identify suspicious network traffic and generate alerts — but it does NOT block traffic. An IPS (Intrusion Prevention System) adds the ability to block. DLP prevents data exfiltration; SIEM correlates logs."}, {s:false,obj:"3.4",q:"A company wants to ensure employees can recover files after a ransomware attack without paying the ransom. Which control BEST achieves this?",o:["A. IDS","B. Regular Offline Backups","C. VPN Access","D. Email Filtering"],a:"B",e:"Regular offline backups are the most effective defense against ransomware data loss. Because offline backups are not connected to the network, they cannot be encrypted by ransomware. When an attack occurs, systems can be restored from the clean backup without paying. Offline/air-gapped is critical — online backups can also be encrypted."}, {s:false,obj:"4.9",q:"Which of the following is MOST critical to maintain when preserving digital evidence for legal proceedings?",o:["A. Full Disk Encryption","B. Legal Hold","C. Chain of Custody","D. Incident Triage"],a:"C",e:"Chain of custody documents who had access to evidence, when, and what was done with it — ensuring evidence integrity for legal use. If chain of custody is broken, evidence may be inadmissible in court. Legal hold preserves evidence from deletion; chain of custody tracks its handling."}, {s:false,obj:"2.3",q:"A company configures a cloud storage bucket and mistakenly leaves it open to the public, exposing customer data. What type of vulnerability is this?",o:["A. Zero-Day","B. Misconfiguration","C. Insider Threat","D. Malware Infection"],a:"B",e:"Leaving a cloud storage bucket publicly accessible is a classic misconfiguration vulnerability. This is one of the most common cloud security failures — improper permissions settings exposing data. Zero-day involves unknown software flaws; insider threats are human actors; malware is malicious code."}, {s:false,obj:"3.2",q:"Which layer of the OSI model does a traditional packet-filtering firewall primarily operate at?",o:["A. Application","B. Transport","C. Network","D. Data Link"],a:"C",e:"Traditional packet-filtering firewalls operate primarily at Layer 3 (Network layer), filtering based on IP addresses, protocols, and ports. Next-Generation Firewalls (NGFWs) extend to Layer 7 (Application layer). Layer 4 (Transport) firewalls also inspect TCP/UDP ports."}, {s:false,obj:"4.6",q:"What security concept is enforced when two employees are BOTH required to approve a large wire transfer before it is executed?",o:["A. Dual Control","B. Least Privilege","C. Discretionary Access Control","D. Federation"],a:"A",e:"Dual control (also called two-person integrity) requires two authorized people to perform a sensitive action. This prevents any one person from acting alone on high-risk transactions, reducing fraud risk. Separation of duties is the broader principle; dual control is its specific implementation here."}, {s:false,obj:"1.4",q:"Which cryptographic concept is used to verify message integrity by generating a fixed-length fingerprint of the data?",o:["A. Symmetric Encryption","B. Asymmetric Encryption","C. Hashing","D. Key Exchange"],a:"C",e:"Hashing generates a fixed-length digest from input data. Any change to the data produces a completely different hash — verifying integrity. Hashing is one-way (cannot be reversed). Common hash algorithms: SHA-256, SHA-3, MD5 (deprecated). Encryption provides confidentiality; hashing provides integrity."}, {s:false,obj:"1.4",q:"What is the primary purpose of tokenization in data security?",o:["A. Encrypt sensitive data using a public key","B. Replace sensitive data with non-sensitive placeholder tokens","C. Hash sensitive data for secure storage","D. Create a secure encrypted communication channel"],a:"B",e:"Tokenization replaces sensitive data (credit card numbers, SSNs) with non-sensitive, randomly generated tokens. The original data is stored securely in a token vault. Unlike encryption, tokens have no mathematical relationship to the original data and cannot be reversed without the vault."}, {s:false,obj:"3.4",q:"Which type of backup provides the FASTEST full system recovery after a catastrophic failure because it contains all data in one set?",o:["A. Incremental","B. Full Backup","C. Differential","D. Cloud Backup"],a:"B",e:"A full backup contains a complete copy of all data, allowing the fastest and simplest restore — just restore one set. The tradeoff is that full backups take the most storage space and time to create. Incremental requires full + all incrementals; differential requires full + latest differential."}, {s:false,obj:"4.1",q:"A security team uses a sandbox environment to open and execute suspicious email attachments safely. What type of control is a sandbox?",o:["A. Preventive","B. Detective","C. Corrective","D. Compensating"],a:"A",e:"A sandbox is a preventive control — it isolates suspicious files in a controlled environment before they can execute on production systems. By containing execution to the sandbox, it prevents potential malware from causing damage. The observation aspect makes it slightly detective, but its primary purpose is prevention."}, {s:false,obj:"2.4",q:"Which of the following is a PRIMARY characteristic that distinguishes a rootkit from other malware?",o:["A. It encrypts files and demands ransom","B. It hides its existence by manipulating the operating system","C. It replicates itself automatically across the network","D. It sends unsolicited messages via Bluetooth"],a:"B",e:"The defining characteristic of a rootkit is its ability to conceal itself and other malware by modifying OS functions — hooking system calls, hiding files and processes. This makes it very difficult to detect with standard tools. Ransomware encrypts; worms replicate; Bluejacking sends Bluetooth messages."}, {s:false,obj:"5.2",q:"An organization wants to minimize data loss during a disaster. Which metric defines the maximum amount of data loss that is acceptable?",o:["A. RTO","B. MTD","C. RPO","D. ALE"],a:"C",e:"RPO (Recovery Point Objective) defines the maximum acceptable data loss — the point in time to which data must be recovered. It determines backup frequency. RTO = maximum acceptable downtime; MTD = maximum tolerable downtime; ALE = annualized loss expectancy (risk calculation)."}, {s:false,obj:"4.1",q:"Which wireless security protocol is considered obsolete, easily cracked within minutes, and should NEVER be used?",o:["A. WPA2","B. WPA","C. WPA3","D. WEP"],a:"D",e:"WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy) is completely broken — its encryption can be cracked in minutes using freely available tools. It was deprecated in 2004. WPA2 and WPA3 are current standards; WPA3 is the most secure. Never deploy WEP even in legacy environments."}, {s:false,obj:"4.3",q:"A system administrator automatically tests patches in a staging environment before deploying them to all production systems on a scheduled basis. This is an example of:",o:["A. Change Management","B. Patch Management","C. Hardening","D. Incident Response"],a:"B",e:"Patch management is the systematic process of identifying, testing, and deploying software updates/patches. The staging-first approach is a patch management best practice. Change management is the broader governance process; hardening reduces attack surface; incident response handles security events."}, {s:false,obj:"2.4",q:"What type of malware encrypts a victim's files or entire system and demands payment for the decryption key?",o:["A. Trojan","B. Worm","C. Spyware","D. Ransomware"],a:"D",e:"Ransomware encrypts files or systems and demands payment (usually cryptocurrency) for the decryption key. It is one of the most impactful cyber threats today. Trojans disguise as legitimate software; worms self-replicate; spyware secretly monitors user activity."}, {s:false,obj:"3.2",q:"Which term describes dividing a network into separate isolated segments — for example, separating Finance, HR, and Engineering departments — to improve security?",o:["A. Subnetting","B. Virtualization","C. Network Segmentation","D. Packet Filtering"],a:"C",e:"Network segmentation isolates different parts of the network to contain breaches and control traffic flow. Even if an attacker compromises the HR segment, they cannot directly reach Finance. Subnetting is a technical implementation; virtualization creates VMs; packet filtering is a firewall function."}, {s:false,obj:"1.2",q:"What concept does the principle of 'never trust, always verify' directly describe?",o:["A. VPN","B. Zero Trust","C. Single Sign-On","D. Role-Based Access Control"],a:"B",e:"'Never trust, always verify' is the foundational principle of Zero Trust architecture. Unlike traditional perimeter security (trust everything inside), Zero Trust requires continuous verification of every access request regardless of network location, user identity, or device."}, {s:false,obj:"4.3",q:"Which tool is specifically designed to discover and report vulnerabilities in systems but does NOT actively exploit them?",o:["A. Penetration Test","B. Exploit Framework","C. Vulnerability Scanner","D. SIEM"],a:"C",e:"A vulnerability scanner passively probes systems to identify known weaknesses, missing patches, and misconfigurations — without exploiting them. A penetration tester actively exploits findings to prove impact. Vulnerability scanners are passive; pen tests are active."}, {s:false,obj:"2.2",q:"An employee receives a phone call from someone claiming to be IT support asking for their login credentials. What type of social engineering attack is this?",o:["A. Phishing","B. Vishing","C. Smishing","D. Spear Phishing"],a:"B",e:"Vishing (Voice Phishing) is a social engineering attack conducted over the telephone. Attackers impersonate IT support, banks, or government agencies to extract credentials or sensitive information. Phishing = email; Smishing = SMS; Spear Phishing = targeted, personalized attack."}, {s:false,obj:"4.6",q:"A user logs into a secure facility using a badge (access card) and a PIN. What authentication factors are being used?",o:["A. Something you know and something you are","B. Something you know and something you have","C. Something you have and something you are","D. Two instances of something you know"],a:"B",e:"PIN = something you know; Badge/access card = something you have. Together these represent two different MFA factors. Biometrics (fingerprint, retina) would be 'something you are.' Two passwords would not be true MFA — they're both 'something you know.'"}, {s:false,obj:"3.2",q:"Which technology allows secure remote access to a corporate network by encrypting all traffic between the remote user and the network?",o:["A. VLAN","B. IDS","C. VPN","D. Proxy Server"],a:"C",e:"A VPN (Virtual Private Network) creates an encrypted tunnel between a remote user and the corporate network, protecting data in transit from eavesdropping. VLAN segments local networks; IDS detects threats; proxy servers filter web traffic but don't provide full network access."}, {s:false,obj:"1.2",q:"An employee leaves a confidential printed document on a shared printer in an open office area. What kind of risk does this represent?",o:["A. Insider Threat","B. Physical Security Risk","C. Supply Chain Risk","D. Malware Infection"],a:"B",e:"Leaving sensitive documents in shared, accessible areas is a physical security risk. Unauthorized individuals could read, photograph, or take the document. This is addressed through clean desk policies, secure print release (pull printing), and document shredding procedures."}, {s:false,obj:"2.5",q:"Which of the following would MOST effectively prevent malware from executing on endpoints by only allowing approved applications to run?",o:["A. Application Allowlisting","B. IDS Deployment","C. SSL/TLS Encryption","D. Role-Based Access Control"],a:"A",e:"Application allowlisting (formerly whitelisting) only permits pre-approved applications to execute on a system. Any unapproved software — including malware — is blocked from running. This is one of the most effective endpoint controls. IDS detects but doesn't prevent; RBAC controls access not execution."}, {s:false,obj:"4.6",q:"A company requires users to authenticate using a username, password, AND a fingerprint scan. This is an example of:",o:["A. Multi-Factor Authentication","B. Federation","C. SSO","D. Kerberos Authentication"],a:"A",e:"Using a username/password (something you know) AND a fingerprint scan (something you are) is Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) — combining two different factor types. MFA significantly reduces account compromise risk. SSO = authenticate once, access many; Federation = cross-domain SSO."}, {s:false,obj:"4.6",q:"Which security principle ensures that critical business functions are divided among multiple people specifically to prevent any one person from committing fraud undetected?",o:["A. Least Privilege","B. Separation of Duties","C. Job Rotation","D. Dual Control"],a:"B",e:"Separation of Duties divides critical tasks so no single person can both execute and approve a sensitive transaction. For example, the person who creates a payment cannot also approve it. This prevents fraud by requiring collusion. Job rotation detects fraud by moving people; dual control requires simultaneous action."}, {s:false,obj:"2.4",q:"An attacker floods a web server with millions of requests per second from thousands of compromised machines, causing it to crash. What type of attack is this?",o:["A. SQL Injection","B. DNS Poisoning","C. DDoS Attack","D. ARP Spoofing"],a:"C",e:"A DDoS (Distributed Denial of Service) attack uses a botnet of compromised machines to overwhelm a target server with traffic, causing it to become unavailable. The distributed nature makes it harder to block. SQL injection targets databases; DNS poisoning redirects traffic; ARP spoofing manipulates local network addressing."}, {s:false,obj:"3.2",q:"Which type of firewall inspects packets at the application layer, can identify specific applications (not just ports), and includes features like IPS, URL filtering, and SSL inspection?",o:["A. Traditional Packet-Filter Firewall","B. Proxy Server","C. Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)","D. Router"],a:"C",e:"A NGFW (Next-Generation Firewall) performs deep packet inspection at Layer 7, identifying applications regardless of port, integrating IPS, SSL/TLS inspection, URL filtering, and threat intelligence. Traditional firewalls only filter by IP/port; proxy servers filter web traffic specifically."}, {s:false,obj:"1.2",q:"Which method ensures that a user cannot deny performing an action, such as sending a digitally signed email?",o:["A. Non-Repudiation","B. Availability","C. Encryption","D. Role-Based Access Control"],a:"A",e:"Non-repudiation provides proof of actions — when an email is digitally signed with the sender's private key, they cannot later deny sending it. Only they possess the private key that created the signature. This is critical for legal, financial, and compliance purposes."}, {s:false,obj:"2.3",q:"An attacker exploits a race condition (Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use) vulnerability in a web application. What category of vulnerability is this?",o:["A. Improper Input Handling","B. Application Logic Flaw","C. Secure Coding Practice","D. Race Condition Vulnerability"],a:"D",e:"A race condition (TOC/TOU) vulnerability occurs when the security check and the use of a resource happen in separate steps, and an attacker can modify the resource between the check and the use. This is an application-layer vulnerability caused by improper handling of concurrent operations."}, {s:false,obj:"3.1",q:"Which of the following is a key security benefit of implementing Infrastructure as Code (IaC) correctly?",o:["A. Manual configuration of servers reduces errors","B. Consistent, repeatable, and auditable deployments","C. Physical separation of networks","D. Encrypted communication tunnels between servers"],a:"B",e:"IaC provides consistent, repeatable infrastructure deployments from version-controlled templates. Every environment is built the same way, reducing configuration drift and human error. Code reviews and scanning tools can check templates for security issues before deployment. Version control provides an audit trail."}, {s:false,obj:"4.4",q:"Which of the following BEST describes the primary benefit of implementing a SIEM system in an enterprise?",o:["A. Blocking unauthorized access attempts in real time","B. Preventing malware infections on endpoints","C. Aggregating and correlating security logs centrally for analysis and alerting","D. Encrypting sensitive data at rest across the environment"],a:"C",e:"The primary benefit of a SIEM is centralized aggregation, correlation, and analysis of security logs from across all systems. This enables detection of attack patterns that would be invisible in individual system logs, and provides a single pane of glass for the security team."}, {s:false,obj:"4.5",q:"What is the MAIN purpose of a DLP (Data Loss Prevention) system?",o:["A. Detect malware signatures on endpoints","B. Monitor and prevent unauthorized transfers of sensitive data","C. Block phishing emails before delivery","D. Scan networks for known vulnerabilities"],a:"B",e:"A DLP system monitors data in motion, at rest, and in use to detect and prevent unauthorized transmission of sensitive information. It can block emails containing PII, prevent uploads to cloud storage, and alert on policy violations. It protects against both accidental and intentional data exfiltration."}, {s:false,obj:"2.2",q:"An attacker creates a fake password reset page that looks exactly like a company's legitimate portal and tricks a user into entering their current credentials. What type of attack is this?",o:["A. Phishing","B. SQL Injection","C. Session Hijacking","D. Privilege Escalation"],a:"A",e:"Creating a fake login/password reset page to steal credentials is a classic phishing attack. The attacker spoofs the visual appearance of a legitimate site to trick users. Spear phishing is a targeted variant; pharming changes DNS to redirect all users automatically."}, {s:false,obj:"3.4",q:"Which backup method copies only the files that have changed since the LAST BACKUP OF ANY TYPE (full or incremental)?",o:["A. Full","B. Incremental","C. Differential","D. Snapshot"],a:"B",e:"Incremental backup backs up only changes since the last backup — regardless of whether that was a full or previous incremental. This results in the smallest, fastest individual backups but the slowest restore (need full + every incremental). Differential backs up since the last full only."}, {s:false,obj:"1.1",q:"What does the principle of Defense in Depth emphasize in security architecture?",o:["A. Using multiple overlapping layers of security controls so no single failure is catastrophic","B. Deploying only firewalls at the network perimeter","C. Using two-factor authentication for all user logins","D. Relying primarily on SIEM alerts for threat detection"],a:"A",e:"Defense in Depth (also called layered security) means implementing multiple overlapping security controls so that if one layer fails, others remain. No single point of failure. For example: perimeter firewall + endpoint AV + MFA + DLP + SIEM all working together."}, {s:false,obj:"1.1",q:"Which of the following is an example of an administrative (managerial) security control?",o:["A. Fire extinguisher in server room","B. Firewall rules blocking inbound traffic","C. Security awareness policy requiring annual training","D. Encryption of data at rest on hard drives"],a:"C",e:"A security awareness policy is an administrative control — it governs behavior through documented requirements. Fire extinguishers are physical; firewall rules are technical; encryption is technical. Administrative controls include policies, procedures, risk assessments, and training programs."}, {s:false,obj:"4.8",q:"A SOC analyst notices unusually large outbound data transfers to an unknown external IP address. What is the BEST immediate action?",o:["A. Shut down all network switches immediately","B. Disconnect or isolate the affected systems from the network","C. Reboot the affected systems to clear any malware","D. Call the Internet service provider"],a:"B",e:"The BEST immediate action is to isolate/disconnect the affected systems to stop the active data exfiltration (containment phase). Shutting down all switches would impact the entire organization; rebooting might destroy volatile forensic evidence; calling the ISP won't stop the active leak."}, {s:false,obj:"2.3",q:"Which term describes unauthorized commands submitted from a trusted user's browser to a web application, exploiting the trust the site has in that user?",o:["A. Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)","B. SQL Injection","C. Command Injection","D. Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)"],a:"D",e:"CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) tricks an authenticated user's browser into sending unauthorized requests to a trusted site — exploiting the site's trust in the user's session. XSS injects malicious scripts into the page itself; SQL injection targets databases; command injection executes OS commands."}, {s:false,obj:"4.6",q:"Which technology enables a single identity to access multiple applications across different organizations or domains without re-authenticating?",o:["A. Multifactor Authentication","B. Federation","C. VPN","D. Zero Trust"],a:"B",e:"Federation establishes trust relationships between different organizations' identity systems, enabling single authentication across organizational boundaries. SAML and OAuth are common federation protocols. SSO works within one organization; federation extends SSO across organizations."}, {s:false,obj:"4.8",q:"What is the FIRST action a security team should take upon detecting an active ransomware infection spreading across the network?",o:["A. Pay the ransom to minimize data loss","B. Immediately disconnect infected systems from the network","C. Run a full antivirus scan on all systems","D. Contact the cloud backup provider"],a:"B",e:"The FIRST action is containment — disconnect infected systems to stop ransomware from spreading to other network resources. Paying the ransom is generally discouraged (no guarantee of key, funds criminals). Running AV is a later eradication step. Backups are accessed during recovery."}, {s:false,obj:"4.6",q:"Which of the following terms describes a control that forces a user to authenticate again after a period of inactivity to prevent unauthorized access to an unattended session?",o:["A. Session Lock","B. Password Complexity","C. Single Sign-On","D. Federation"],a:"A",e:"A session lock (screen lock/timeout) requires re-authentication after inactivity, protecting unattended systems from unauthorized access. This is a preventive technical control. Password complexity strengthens passwords; SSO reduces login frequency; federation extends access across domains."}, {s:false,obj:"5.5",q:"What type of security assessment evaluates physical, administrative, and technical safeguards to identify risks WITHOUT actively exploiting any vulnerabilities?",o:["A. Vulnerability Scan","B. Penetration Test","C. Risk Assessment","D. Business Impact Analysis"],a:"C",e:"A risk assessment evaluates the overall security posture — identifying threats, vulnerabilities, and impacts — without actively testing or exploiting systems. A vulnerability scan automates finding technical weaknesses; a penetration test actively exploits vulnerabilities to prove impact."}, {s:false,obj:"1.4",q:"Which component is critical for ensuring confidentiality when transmitting sensitive data across the Internet?",o:["A. Hashing","B. Encryption","C. Load Balancing","D. IDS"],a:"B",e:"Encryption protects data confidentiality during transmission by making intercepted data unreadable to unauthorized parties. TLS/HTTPS encrypts web traffic; IPSec encrypts network traffic; VPNs encrypt tunnel traffic. Hashing ensures integrity; IDS detects threats; load balancing distributes traffic."}, {s:false,obj:"3.4",q:"What is the purpose of implementing redundant power supplies in critical servers?",o:["A. Improve encryption processing performance","B. Increase available network bandwidth","C. Enhance system availability by eliminating power as a single point of failure","D. Provide faster data processing speeds"],a:"C",e:"Redundant power supplies eliminate power as a single point of failure, directly supporting system availability. If one power supply fails, the other continues powering the system with no downtime. This is part of high-availability design alongside redundant network connections and RAID storage."}, {s:false,obj:"1.4",q:"Which of the following MOST accurately defines tokenization in data security?",o:["A. Encrypting all data in a database using AES-256","B. Replacing sensitive data elements with a randomly generated unique identifier (token)","C. Hashing user passwords with a salt before storage","D. Obfuscating source code to protect intellectual property"],a:"B",e:"Tokenization replaces sensitive values (credit card numbers, SSNs) with randomly generated tokens that have no mathematical relationship to the original. The real data lives only in a secure vault. Unlike encryption (reversible with a key), tokens are meaningless without the vault mapping."}, {s:false,obj:"2.3",q:"An attacker discovers and exploits a vulnerability in software that the vendor has no knowledge of and has not yet released a patch for. What type of attack is this?",o:["A. Zero-Day","B. Man-in-the-Middle","C. Cross-Site Scripting","D. Phishing"],a:"A",e:"A zero-day attack exploits a vulnerability that is unknown to the vendor or for which no patch exists yet. The name comes from 'zero days' of warning for defenders. These are particularly dangerous because standard patch management cannot address them — compensating controls (WAF, network segmentation) are needed."}, {s:false,obj:"5.6",q:"What is the BEST method to reduce the impact of social engineering attacks targeting employees?",o:["A. Install next-generation firewalls","B. Security Awareness Training","C. Regular Penetration Testing","D. Conduct Full Backups Regularly"],a:"B",e:"Security Awareness Training is the most effective defense against social engineering — it teaches employees to recognize manipulation tactics (phishing, vishing, pretexting) and respond correctly. Technical controls can't address the human element. A trained, security-aware workforce is the best defense."}, {s:false,obj:"2.4",q:"Which type of malware is specifically designed to provide an attacker with persistent, hidden administrative access to a compromised system?",o:["A. Ransomware","B. Trojan","C. Rootkit","D. Worm"],a:"C",e:"A rootkit provides persistent, hidden privileged access by modifying the OS kernel, system calls, or bootloader. It hides its presence from standard detection tools. Ransomware encrypts data; trojans disguise as legitimate software; worms self-replicate across networks."}, {s:false,obj:"3.4",q:"A backup strategy rotates backups using daily (son), weekly (father), and monthly (grandfather) sets. What is the PRIMARY purpose of the Grandfather-Father-Son method?",o:["A. Ensure absolutely zero data loss at all times","B. Maintain multiple historical versions of backups at different retention periods","C. Accelerate the speed of disaster recovery","D. Improve real-time data replication performance"],a:"B",e:"The Grandfather-Father-Son (GFS) backup rotation maintains multiple historical versions: daily (son) tapes for short-term recovery, weekly (father) for medium-term, and monthly (grandfather) for long-term retention. This balances storage efficiency with the ability to restore from various points in time."}, {s:false,obj:"3.2",q:"What security tool sits between users and the Internet, intercepting and filtering web traffic to enforce acceptable use policies and inspect for threats?",o:["A. Firewall","B. VPN","C. Proxy Server","D. Load Balancer"],a:"C",e:"A proxy server intercepts all outbound web traffic, filters content based on policies, logs activity, and can inspect for malware. It enforces acceptable use policies by blocking restricted sites. Firewalls filter by IP/port; VPNs encrypt tunnels; load balancers distribute traffic across servers."} ]; // UPDATED renderDashboard with domain grouping + all new topics // This replaces the old renderDashboard + TOPICS + STATE + TOPIC_QS const DOMAINS = [ {id:'d1',num:'1.0',label:'General Security Concepts',pct:'12%',color:'var(--cyan)', topics:[ {id:'t1',obj:'1.1',label:'Security Controls',color:'c1',timeMin:30,desc:'Security control categories (Technical, Managerial, Operational, Physical) and types (Preventive, Deterrent, Detective, Corrective, Compensating, Directive).'}, {id:'t2',obj:'1.2',label:'Fundamental Concepts',color:'c2',timeMin:30,desc:'CIA Triad, Non-repudiation, AAA, Gap Analysis, Zero Trust architecture, Physical Security, and Deception Technologies.'}, {id:'t3',obj:'1.3',label:'Change Management',color:'c3',timeMin:30,desc:'Change management processes, business impacts, technical implications, documentation, and version control.'}, {id:'t4',obj:'1.4',label:'Cryptographic Solutions',color:'c4',timeMin:30,desc:'PKI, encryption types and levels, TPM/HSM, obfuscation, hashing, digital signatures, and certificates.'}, ]}, {id:'d2',num:'2.0',label:'Threats, Vulnerabilities & Mitigations',pct:'22%',color:'#ff6b6b', topics:[ {id:'t5',obj:'2.1',label:'Threat Actors & Motivations',color:'c3',timeMin:30,desc:'Nation-states, hacktivists, insider threats, organized crime, script kiddies. Motivations: espionage, financial gain, disruption, blackmail, war.'}, {id:'t6',obj:'2.2',label:'Threat Vectors & Attack Surfaces',color:'c3',timeMin:30,desc:'Phishing, vishing, smishing, watering hole, typosquatting, supply chain, removable devices, BEC, and social engineering techniques.'}, {id:'t7',obj:'2.3',label:'Types of Vulnerabilities',color:'c3',timeMin:30,desc:'SQL injection, XSS, buffer overflow, zero-day, race conditions, VM escape, misconfiguration, supply chain, mobile, and cloud vulnerabilities.'}, {id:'t8',obj:'2.4',label:'Indicators of Malicious Activity',color:'c3',timeMin:30,desc:'Ransomware, keyloggers, rootkits, worms, trojans, DDoS, DNS attacks, credential replay, impossible travel, and IoC indicators.'}, {id:'t9',obj:'2.5',label:'Mitigation Techniques',color:'c3',timeMin:30,desc:'Segmentation, patching, encryption, least privilege, application allow listing, hardening, EDR, decommissioning, and compensating controls.'}, ]}, {id:'d3',num:'3.0',label:'Security Architecture',pct:'18%',color:'var(--blue)', topics:[ {id:'t10',obj:'3.1',label:'Architecture Models',color:'c1',timeMin:30,desc:'Cloud security, IaC, serverless, microservices, IoT, SCADA/ICS, RTOS, embedded systems, on-premises vs cloud, centralized vs decentralized.'}, {id:'t11',obj:'3.2',label:'Secure Enterprise Infrastructure',color:'c1',timeMin:30,desc:'Device placement, security zones, failure modes, network appliances, port security, firewall types, VPN, SD-WAN, SASE, and secure communications.'}, {id:'t12',obj:'3.3',label:'Data Protection Strategies',color:'c1',timeMin:30,desc:'Data types, classification, data states (at rest/transit/use), sovereignty, geographic restrictions, encryption, masking, tokenization, segmentation.'}, {id:'t13',obj:'3.4',label:'Resilience & Recovery',color:'c1',timeMin:30,desc:'High availability, hot/warm/cold sites, RTO/RPO, backups (3-2-1 rule), testing types, capacity planning, generators, UPS, and multi-cloud.'}, ]}, {id:'d4',num:'4.0',label:'Security Operations',pct:'28%',color:'var(--yellow)', topics:[ {id:'t14',obj:'4.1',label:'Security Techniques for Resources',color:'c4',timeMin:30,desc:'Secure baselines, hardening, wireless security, MDM/BYOD/COPE, application security (SAST/DAST), sandboxing, and monitoring.'}, {id:'t15',obj:'4.6',label:'Identity & Access Management',color:'c4',timeMin:30,desc:'Provisioning/de-provisioning, identity proofing, federation, SSO (SAML/OAuth), MFA, password concepts, PAM tools, and access controls.'}, ]}, {id:'d5',num:'5.0',label:'Security Program Management',pct:'20%',color:'var(--green)', topics:[ {id:'t16',obj:'5.1',label:'Security Governance',color:'c5',timeMin:30,desc:'Guidelines, policies (AUP, IR, BCP, SDLC), standards, procedures, external considerations, governance structures, and data roles.'}, {id:'t17',obj:'5.2',label:'Risk Management',color:'c5',timeMin:30,desc:'Risk identification, assessment, analysis (qualitative/quantitative), SLE/ALE/ARO, risk register, risk appetite, risk strategies, and BIA.'}, {id:'t18',obj:'5.6',label:'Security Awareness',color:'c5',timeMin:30,desc:'Phishing campaigns, anomalous behavior recognition, user guidance, password management, social engineering, OPSEC, and hybrid work security.'}, ]}, ]; // Flatten topics for backward compat const TOPICS = DOMAINS.flatMap(d=>d.topics); const TOPIC_QS = { t1:Q_1_1,t2:Q_1_2,t3:Q_1_3,t4:Q_1_4, t5:Q_2_1,t6:Q_2_2,t7:Q_2_3,t8:Q_2_4,t9:Q_2_5, t10:Q_3_1,t11:Q_3_2,t12:Q_3_3,t13:Q_3_4, t14:Q_4_1,t15:Q_4_6, t16:Q_5_1,t17:Q_5_2,t18:Q_5_6 }; const STATE = { progress:Object.fromEntries(TOPICS.map(t=>[t.id,{score:null,passed:false}])), currentTopic:null,isFinalExam:false,questions:[],current:0, selected:null,submitted:false,score:0,answered:0, timer:null,secondsLeft:0,timeUsed:0,userAnswers:[] }; function saveState(){try{localStorage.setItem('sp_state_v2',JSON.stringify(STATE.progress))}catch(e){}} function loadState(){try{const d=localStorage.getItem('sp_state_v2');if(d){const p=JSON.parse(d);Object.keys(p).forEach(k=>{if(STATE.progress[k])STATE.progress[k]=p[k]});}}catch(e){}} function renderDashboard(){ loadState(); const grid=document.getElementById('topicsGrid'); grid.innerHTML=''; let topicIdx=0; DOMAINS.forEach(domain=>{ // Domain header const dh=document.createElement('div'); dh.className='domain-header'; dh.style.cssText=`grid-column:1/-1;display:flex;align-items:center;gap:1rem;margin:1rem 0 0.3rem;padding:0.7rem 1.2rem;background:rgba(255,255,255,0.03);border-left:3px solid ${domain.color};border-radius:4px`; dh.innerHTML=`
DOMAIN ${domain.num}
${domain.label}
${domain.pct} OF EXAM
`; grid.appendChild(dh); domain.topics.forEach(t=>{ const globalIdx=topicIdx; topicIdx++; const p=STATE.progress[t.id]; // First topic of first domain always unlocked; others need previous passed const prevId=globalIdx===0?null:TOPICS[globalIdx-1].id; const locked=globalIdx>0&&!STATE.progress[prevId].passed; const pct=p.score!==null?Math.round(p.score*100):null; const card=document.createElement('div'); card.className=`topic-card ${locked?'locked':''} ${p.passed?'completed':''}`; if(!locked&&!p.passed) card.className+=' active-topic'; card.onclick=()=>{if(!locked)startTopic(t.id)}; const barColor=p.score===null?'transparent':p.passed?'var(--green)':'var(--red)'; const barW=p.score===null?0:Math.round(p.score*100); card.innerHTML=`
OBJ ${t.obj}
${locked?'🔒':p.passed?'✅':p.score!==null?'❌':'▶️'}

${t.label}

${t.desc}

25 QUESTIONS · ${t.timeMin} MIN
${pct!==null?pct+'%':locked?'LOCKED':'START'}
`; grid.appendChild(card); }); }); const allPassed=TOPICS.every(t=>STATE.progress[t.id].passed); const examCard=document.getElementById('finalExamCard'); const startBtn=document.getElementById('startExamBtn'); const unlockStatus=document.getElementById('examUnlockStatus'); if(allPassed){ examCard.className='exam-card unlocked'; startBtn.disabled=false; startBtn.textContent='START EXAM →'; unlockStatus.textContent='✅ All topics passed — UNLOCKED'; unlockStatus.style.color='var(--green)'; } } function startTopic(topicId){ const t=TOPICS.find(x=>x.id===topicId); STATE.currentTopic=t; STATE.isFinalExam=false; STATE.questions=shuffle(TOPIC_QS[topicId]); STATE.current=0;STATE.selected=null;STATE.submitted=false; STATE.score=0;STATE.answered=0;STATE.secondsLeft=t.timeMin*60; STATE.timeUsed=0;STATE.userAnswers=new Array(STATE.questions.length).fill(null); document.getElementById('quizTopicLabel').textContent=`OBJECTIVE ${t.obj} — ${t.label.toUpperCase()}`; showScreen('quizScreen'); startTimer(); renderQ(); } function startFinalExam(){ const allPassed=TOPICS.every(t=>STATE.progress[t.id].passed); if(!allPassed)return; STATE.isFinalExam=true;STATE.currentTopic=null; STATE.questions=shuffle(Q_EXAM); STATE.current=0;STATE.selected=null;STATE.submitted=false; STATE.score=0;STATE.answered=0;STATE.secondsLeft=180*60; STATE.timeUsed=0;STATE.userAnswers=new Array(STATE.questions.length).fill(null); document.getElementById('quizTopicLabel').textContent='FINAL EXAM — ALL OBJECTIVES'; showScreen('quizScreen'); startTimer(); renderQ(); } function shuffle(arr){return[...arr].sort(()=>Math.random()-0.5)} function startTimer(){ clearInterval(STATE.timer); updateTimerDisplay(); STATE.timer=setInterval(()=>{ STATE.secondsLeft--;STATE.timeUsed++; updateTimerDisplay(); if(STATE.secondsLeft<=0){clearInterval(STATE.timer);finishQuiz();} },1000); } function updateTimerDisplay(){ const m=Math.floor(STATE.secondsLeft/60),s=STATE.secondsLeft%60; const el=document.getElementById('quizTimer'); el.textContent=`${String(m).padStart(2,'0')}:${String(s).padStart(2,'0')}`; el.className='quiz-timer'+(STATE.secondsLeft<=300?' danger':''); } function renderQ(){ const q=STATE.questions[STATE.current]; const total=STATE.questions.length; STATE.selected=null;STATE.submitted=false; document.getElementById('qNumber').textContent=`Q${STATE.current+1} of ${total}`; const tag=document.getElementById('qObjTag'); const obj=q.obj||STATE.currentTopic?.obj||'EXAM'; tag.textContent=`OBJ ${obj}`; tag.style.cssText=`background:rgba(0,200,255,0.07);color:var(--cyan);border:1px solid rgba(0,200,255,0.25);border-radius:4px;font-family:'Share Tech Mono',monospace;font-size:.62rem;letter-spacing:.15em;padding:.25rem .7rem`; const sf=document.getElementById('qScenarioFlag'); const sb=document.getElementById('qScenarioBlock'); if(q.s&&q.sc){sf.style.display='inline-flex';sb.style.display='block';sb.innerHTML=q.sc;} else{sf.style.display='none';sb.style.display='none';} document.getElementById('qStem').textContent=q.q; const od=document.getElementById('optionsDiv');od.innerHTML=''; q.o.forEach((opt,i)=>{ const letters=['A','B','C','D']; const div=document.createElement('div');div.className='opt';div.dataset.letter=letters[i]; div.innerHTML=`
${letters[i]}
${opt.substring(3).trim()}
`; div.addEventListener('click',()=>selectOpt(letters[i])); od.appendChild(div); }); document.getElementById('expBox').className='explanation-box'; document.getElementById('expBox').innerHTML=''; const sb2=document.getElementById('submitBtn'); sb2.textContent='SUBMIT ANSWER';sb2.className='btn-q btn-submit'; sb2.disabled=true;sb2.onclick=submitAnswer; document.getElementById('quizProgressFill').style.width=`${(STATE.current/total)*100}%`; document.getElementById('quizPct').textContent=`${Math.round((STATE.current/total)*100)}%`; document.getElementById('liveScore').textContent=STATE.score; document.getElementById('liveAnswered').textContent=STATE.answered; } function selectOpt(letter){ if(STATE.submitted)return; STATE.selected=letter; document.querySelectorAll('.opt').forEach(o=>o.classList.toggle('selected',o.dataset.letter===letter)); document.getElementById('submitBtn').disabled=false; } function submitAnswer(){ if(STATE.submitted||!STATE.selected)return; STATE.submitted=true; const q=STATE.questions[STATE.current]; const correct=STATE.selected===q.a; STATE.answered++;if(correct)STATE.score++; STATE.userAnswers[STATE.current]={selected:STATE.selected,correct:q.a,isCorrect:correct}; document.querySelectorAll('.opt').forEach(o=>{ const l=o.dataset.letter; o.classList.add('disabled'); if(l===q.a){o.classList.add(correct&&l===STATE.selected?'correct':'missed');o.querySelector('.opt-result').textContent='✓';} else if(l===STATE.selected&&!correct){o.classList.add('wrong');o.querySelector('.opt-result').textContent='✗';} }); const eb=document.getElementById('expBox'); eb.innerHTML=q.e;eb.className=`explanation-box show ${correct?'correct-exp':'wrong-exp'}`; if(!eb.querySelector('.exp-label')){const lbl=document.createElement('span');lbl.className='exp-label';lbl.textContent=correct?'✅ CORRECT':'❌ INCORRECT — EXPLANATION';eb.prepend(lbl);} const sb=document.getElementById('submitBtn');
